key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Shawn Joseph, Interim Superintendent of Schools for Prince George’s County Public Schools
Judith White, Prince George’s County Public Schools Chief Academic Officer
Alexandra Tyukavina, Paint Branch PTA Secretary
organizations: Prince George’s County Board of Education
51753.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted to send a letter to the Prince George’s County Board of Education in support of continued funding for the Chinese immersion program at Paint Branch Elementary School. The letter, signed by Mayor Fazlul Kabir, asks the board to fund the full program for all Paint Branch students, stating that “The program has become an important part of the broader educational ecosystem serving College Park residents” and that “Changes that significantly reduce immersion opportunities could undermine years of investment by educators, students and the community.” The council’s letter comes as the board is scheduled to vote on its proposed fiscal 2027 budget, which includes a plan to limit enrollment to 25 students in each grade from kindergarten through fifth grade.

key_people: Sami Saeed, University of Maryland Public Policy Major
Holly Simmons, College Park Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
David Dorsch, College Park Resident and Landlord
Richard Biffl, Landlord
Nick DiSpirito, University of Maryland Student Liaison to the College Park City Council
51534.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously approved an ordinance preventing landlords from asking tenants to renew a lease sooner than 180 days from the end of the current lease. The ordinance was introduced in September and supported by students renting apartments in high-rise buildings, who shared stories of landlords approaching them as soon as a month after move-in with threats of rent increases or replacement. Sami Saeed, a University of Maryland public policy major, said 'The stress that that caused me … trying to find anyone to live with for next year. I had to call 50 people. … That is the situation renters are faced with. That is the situation I was faced with. It happened to me.'

key_people: Donald Hays, College Park Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee Subcommittee Chairman
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: College Park Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee
51397.ai_summary: A citizen-led committee resubmitted a year-old report on school bus stop safety to the College Park City Council, recommending upgrades at seven bus stops with low safety scores. Donald Hays, the chairman of the BPAC subcommittee, stated 'I personally feel that is an outrage and there has been very little movement and very little progress' regarding the implementation of the recommendations. The committee's requests include adding bike lanes and sidewalks to protect elementary school children, with some council members, such as Jacob Hernandez and Alan Hew, supporting the additions.

key_people: Maxine Gross, Lakeland Community Heritage Project Member
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
51400.ai_summary: The Lakeland neighborhood in College Park is planning to add 10 murals to highlight its history, as part of a continuation of a decades-long effort. According to Maxine Gross, a member of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project, 'It's just a way for folks to be able to see the community that we have been talking about for a number of years.' The project has a total budget of $28,382, with $12,650 coming from a 2022 grant from the Maryland Heritage Areas Authority and $15,732 from city funds, and will be a joint effort among city officials, community members, and University of Maryland partners.

key_people: Frances Chen, University of Maryland Student
Nicholas Crane, University of Maryland Student
Ashna Pandya, University of Maryland Student
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
51390.ai_summary: Three University of Maryland students, Frances Chen, Nicholas Crane, and Ashna Pandya, presented to the College Park City Council on ways to ease neighborhood tensions near the university. Chen stated that the university campus can only accommodate about 20% of its current 30,000 student population, leading to what she called 'studentification' of established neighborhoods, which can degrade a community's quality of life. Councilmember Alan Hew suggested that the university should take more responsibility in teaching its students how to be good neighbors, stating 'The university well knows that, after two years living on campus, students are expected to live off campus... They have not taken any responsibility in preparing the students living off campus.'

key_people: Jim Miller, Parking Enforcement Officer
Susan Whitney, College Park Councilmember
51376.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering expanding permit parking in residential neighborhoods, with Parking Enforcement Officer Jim Miller proposing a study to examine the issue. Miller suggested that permit-only parking could help address overcrowding in homes by only allowing homeowners and tenants whose names appear on a lease to qualify for permits, and proposed charging an annual fee for each permit, saying 'Where are they going to park their car now, because they are not on a lease agreement, they are not listed as [the] owner-occupied property owner... Without providing the necessary documentation that we require, they won’t be able to get a permit, so they’ll have to figure out where their car goes.' Councilmembers had mixed reactions to the proposal, with former Councilmember Susan Whitney suggesting that paid street parking could lead to less green space and increased stormwater runoff, and instead proposing incentives such as tax breaks to encourage residents to own fewer cars, saying 'We want people who need their cars to be able to park them, and we want people who can get by with one car to do that and to be encouraged to do that'.

key_people: Matthew Tedesco, Lawyer for Cruz Development Corp.
Kelly Jordan, College Park City Councilmember
Carlos Nunes, Senior Planner for the City of College Park
51441.ai_summary: The College Park City Council agreed to recommend amending a parking ordinance to allow a developer to build an 87-unit affordable senior housing apartment building on Branchville Road. According to Matthew Tedesco, the project "emphasizes aging in place" and will include elevators, social spaces, and amenities for senior citizens. As Councilmember Kelly Jordan noted, "There is a real need for senior housing in College Park," and the developer will use a 4% federal low-income housing tax credit to finance the project, with rents based on 60% of the area median income.

key_people: Wes Moore, Maryland Governor
Joseline Peña-Melnyk, House Speaker, Maryland House of Delegates
Jim Rosapepe, State Senator, Maryland Senate
Tom Dernoga, Prince George’s County Councilmember
51373.ai_summary: Maryland Governor Wes Moore has proposed a bill to change Maryland zoning law, allowing for more affordable homes by reducing the required property size per single-family house to 5,000 square feet, which would enable at least eight houses per acre. House Speaker Joseline Peña-Melnyk supports the proposal, stating 'Maryland has a housing shortage of roughly 100,000 homes... When people can’t afford to live here, we lose workers. We lose families. And we lose the economic growth that allows us to fund schools, transportation and public safety.' However, State Senator Jim Rosapepe and Prince George’s County Councilmember Tom Dernoga have expressed concerns or opposition to the proposal, with Dernoga calling it a 'sprawl multiplier which will worsen congestion, pollution, school overcrowding and will strain public safety resources.'

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Erica Johnson, Prince George's County Police Department Sergeant
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
51366.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember Alan Hew has graduated from three different citizen police academies, learning about police operations and creating relationships with community members. Hew stated, 'There's a lot of speculation of how they operate and I wanted to be able to speak confidently in support of the departments that operate within our city.' His training has helped him contribute to council discussions about safety and understand police operations, which he can share with his constituents, and according to Fazlul Kabir, 'it is important' for building trust and understanding between the community and police.

key_people: Aisha Braveboy, Prince George’s County Executive
Keith R. Sydnor, Laurel Mayor
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
organizations: City of Laurel Public Works Department
51316.ai_summary: A dangerous winter storm named Fern brought snow, sleet, and freezing rain to the Route 1 corridor, prompting local jurisdictions to mount snow removal and emergency response efforts. Prince George’s County Executive Aisha Braveboy stated, 'The county’s snow response operation is fully active. Crews are working around the clock to clear and treat the roadways and public safety remains are top priority.' College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir reminded residents to report icy sidewalks and unsafe conditions to the city and to check when the streets they live on were last plowed through an interactive map.

key_people: Tina Nguyen, Owner of Queens Chapel Barbershop
Joshua Solomon, College Park City Councilmember Ward 5
Kelson Nisbett, College Park City Councilmember Ward 5
Jarod Towers, Hyattsville Police Chief
Laura Lanham, Hyattsville Deputy Police Chief
Merrill Hartson, Retired Editor of AP News and Customer of Queens Chapel Barbershop
Melvin Franklin (Frank) Key, Long-time Customer of Queens Chapel Barbershop
51173.ai_summary: The Queens Chapel Barbershop celebrated its 85th anniversary with a party attended by customers, city council members Joshua Solomon and Kelson Nisbett, and Hyattsville Police Chief Jarod Towers. Merrill Hartson, a customer and retired editor of AP News, gave remarks, saying: 'For 85 years, the Queens Chapel Barbershop has persevered, never short of sheers, never at a loss for cheers' and noting that 'everyone knows your name' at the barbershop. The event was a testament to the barbershop's loyal customer base and its importance in the community.

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Alex Posorske, Veo Policy and Partnerships Manager for the East Coast
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
51475.ai_summary: Veo, the company that owns electric scooters and bikes in College Park, is cracking down on where riders can park them due to a 62% increase in usage between fall 2024 and fall 2025. As Councilmember Alan Hew noted, 'This is just almost malicious,' regarding a rider who left a scooter in the middle of a sidewalk near a designated parking site. Mayor Fazlul Kabir supports Veo, saying 'We are very happy that we have this micromobility system in College Park because this is in line with our strategic sustainability goal,' and added 'Every time someone rides a Veo scooter, that person is not using the car, right? So we’re taking out the cars from the street.'

key_people: Daniel Oates, College Park City Councilmember
Kelly Jordan, College Park City Councilmember
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Holly Simmons, College Park City Councilmember
51408.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has become younger with the election of three newcomers, with five of the eight members now under 50 years old. According to Daniel Oates, College Park City Councilmember, 'To me what’s interesting is less [about] the relative ages than the new people coming on the council and bringing different experiences and different perspectives from that vein.' The addition of new council members with diverse experiences and perspectives is expected to bring fresh ideas to the city's governance, particularly on issues such as utility bills, schools, and safety, as noted by Kelly Jordan, College Park City Councilmember, who said 'We’ll all have lots of questions and … when you’re used to something, comfortable with something, new questions can bring new ideas for how you’re used to looking at it.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Anthony Brown, Maryland Attorney General
organizations: U.S. Bureau of City Labor Statistics
key_issues: job losses due to federal government shutdown and policy changes
economic impact on College Park residents
51404.ai_summary: A year into President Donald Trump's second term, College Park is dealing with the impact of executive orders, employee layoffs, funding cuts, and new policies, affecting the city's residents, economy, and development plans, according to College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir. 'We do not know how things will actually go in the future,' Kabir said, 'Our policies will stay as used to be, and we will be supporting the community in any way we can.' The city has increased its financial assistance program budget to $100,000 to help residents affected by job losses, with Kabir stating 'Neighbors have had to take jobs that they are not trained for, and with much lower pay,' and 'I know that they're concerned and they're struggling.'

key_people: Joseline Peña-Melnyk, Maryland House of Delegates Speaker
Adrienne A. Jones, Baltimore County Delegate and Former Maryland House of Delegates Speaker
Jim Rosapepe, Maryland State Senator
Ben Barnes, Maryland Delegate
Mary Lehman, Maryland Delegate for District 21
organizations: U.S. Attorney’s Office for the District of Columbia
House Health and Government Operations Committee
50899.ai_summary: Joseline Peña-Melnyk, a College Park resident and former College Park City Councilmember, was sworn in as the 109th Speaker of the Maryland House of Delegates, becoming the second woman speaker and the first Afro-Latina to hold the position. Peña-Melnyk said, 'My journey did not begin in these chambers. It began in the Dominican Republic in a small wooden house with a tin roof, one with holes where the rain will come through.' She also stated, 'This moment belongs not just to me, but to every child catching rain in a pot,' and expressed her intention to collaborate with both Democrats and Republicans as an 'inclusive' leader.

key_people: Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Edouard Haba, Hyattsville City Council Member and Prince George’s County Municipal Association President
Monica Casañas, Colmar Manor Mayor and Prince George’s County Municipal Association Vice President
50826.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has paused its membership in the Prince George’s County Municipal Association (PGCMA) after a former treasurer allegedly embezzled over $30,000. Councilmember Jacob Hernandez stated, 'It’s our taxpayer money... Why would we give money to an organization that is clearly lacking integrity?' PGCMA President Edouard Haba said, 'We know what we went through was traumatic... We all want all of us to stay together and move forward,' and noted the organization's value is 'camaraderie and networking' among elected municipal leaders.

key_people: Carly Zapernick, Article Managing Director of Construction and Development
Julie Bringman, Hope Lutheran Church Pastor
Chris Hatcher, Attorney representing Article
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park Councilmember (District 2)
Denise Mitchell, College Park Councilmember (District 4)
50763.ai_summary: College Park officials pressed Article Student Living to include affordable units in a proposed 192-unit student housing complex on Guilford Drive. The project would replace the Hope Lutheran Church and Student Center with a five-story apartment complex, and Pastor Julie Bringman said the new church space might have room for offices or affordable housing units. Mayor Fazlul Kabir encouraged Article to use the city's tax incentive program, saying 'The catch is that you must have affordable housing in it,' while Councilmembers raised concerns about affordability, with Denise Mitchell stating 'We have a vision moving forward, and we're not going to negate that vision.'

key_people: Nisha Kumar, University of Maryland student
Scott Tebbe, University of Maryland student
Richard Biffle, Landlord
Lisa Miller, Prince George’s Property Owners Association President
Nick DiSpirito, UMD student liaison to the College Park City Council
Amira Abujuma, UMD student liaison to the College Park City Council
50602.ai_summary: Nearly 30 people, mostly University of Maryland students, asked the College Park City Council to pass an ordinance preventing landlords from pressuring tenants to renew leases before they have lived in their units for six months. Students like Nisha Kumar and Scott Tebbe spoke about the stress and pressure of being asked to re-sign leases early, with Kumar saying 'We’ve just moved in for the semester and we already have to figure out next year... Who’s going to take my spot? … Will I have to pay over $1,000 a month for a place I’m not even living in?' Landlords, including Richard Biffle, opposed the ordinance, with some arguing that it would limit their control over tenant selection. The proposed ordinance aims to protect students from 'predatory' lease-renewal agreements and promote fair landlord-tenant relations in College Park.

key_people: Samantha McLoughlin, Parent Teacher Organization (PTO) President
Ashley Franceski, Kindergarten Teacher at Hollywood Elementary School
Carl Franceski, General Manager of Sales and Operations for Coca-Cola Consolidated
Carlos Johnson, Principal of Hollywood Elementary School
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember (District 1)
50509.ai_summary: The local bottler for Coca-Cola gave each of Hollywood Elementary School’s 301 students new sneakers, socks and school supplies on Nov. 12 through Coca-Cola Consolidated’s Back-to-School with Purpose initiative, in partnership with the nonprofit Samaritan’s Feet. According to Principal Carlos Johnson, 'I’m just happy that we are on the receiving end,' and 'When you can provide for a parent and the things that they need at home, school and home become one.' Parent Teacher Organization (PTO) President Samantha McLoughlin said, 'We believe our kids deserve just as much as the opportunities that better-funded schools get,' and City Councilmember Jacob Hernandez praised Principal Johnson’s efforts to build relationships in the local community.

key_people: Helen Reniere, Resident of Attick Towers
Glenn Reniere, Engineer at Vitro Corp.
Charlotte Davis, Staff Member at Attick Towers
50310.ai_summary: Helen Reniere, a resident of Attick Towers in College Park, celebrated her 100th birthday and received an official proclamation from the College Park mayor and City Council, praising her kindness and perseverance. Reniere said, 'I get up in the morning, I take my medicine and … I guess you got to put Jesus first.' She spends her days watching 'Wheel of Fortune', listening to a Catholic radio station, and talking on the phone with her son, who lives in Arizona, and values her friendship with Charlotte Davis, a staff member at Attick Towers, who helps her stay positive.

key_people: Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Sonja Ewing, Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission
Bob Patten, Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission
50276.ai_summary: The College Park City Council sent a letter to the Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission supporting the construction of a bridge connecting Old Calvert Road Park to the Northeast Branch Trail. Councilmember Ray Ranker said, 'We don’t want to spend another month waiting,' and 'We want to get [construction] started … tomorrow,' as his constituents have waited a decade for a bridge to connect the park to the trail system. The proposed bridge is estimated to cost around $2 million, and Councilmember Ranker emphasized that this has been something the community has been asking for for at least 10 years, as the current route to the trail is not safe.

key_people: Gavin Neubauer, University of Maryland Public Policy Student and College Park Child Care Task Force Member
Christina Martin Kenny, College Park Estates Resident and College Park Child Care Task Force Member
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
organizations: College Park Child Care Task Force
50298.ai_summary: The College Park Child Care Task Force proposed ways to lower child care costs, including offering grants to home-based child care providers and property tax credits to established centers. As Gavin Neubauer, a University of Maryland public policy student and task force member, said, 'If you’re able to even increase the amount of businesses here that can survive and care for children, we’re going to be a real standout city in this entire metropolitan area.' The task force's report aims to address the high cost of child care in College Park, where the average cost for center-based care is over $15,000 a year.

key_people: Jatinder Khokhar, College Park Director of Public Services
Stephanie Anderson, College Park City Attorney
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: City of College Park Public Services Department
50304.ai_summary: The College Park City Council introduced an ordinance to increase fines for city code violations, including construction, fire safety, housing, noise and unruly social gatherings, to align with a Maryland law that took effect Oct. 1. Director of Public Services Jatinder Khokhar and City Attorney Stephanie Anderson presented the proposed changes, which considered fairness and consistency of enforcement. As Anderson said, 'We are not the highest but we want to make sure that the types of violations that are occurring are taken seriously,' and Councilmember John Rigg noted that fines for noise violations should be lower than those for unruly social gatherings because 'noise should be bad, but unruly social gatherings almost always include noise and it includes a lot more.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
50292.ai_summary: In the recent College Park election, 16- and 17-year-olds were allowed to cast ballots for the first time, with six teenagers voting and 53 registering to vote. Mayor Fazlul Kabir won 1,480 votes, while District 1 councilmembers Jacob Hernandez and Alan Hew were the top vote-getters for council seats. The election had a voter turnout of 9.9% of registered voters in College Park, with a total of 1,598 people voting.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Director
Marc Lore, Wonder Founder
Bobby Flay, Bobby Flay Steak Owner
50876.ai_summary: The Wonder food hall chain has opened its first Maryland location in College Park on Baltimore Avenue, featuring takeout and delivery from over 15 restaurants and brands. According to College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, 'The city has been working hard, and our economic development staff in particular have been working hard to try to really bring an exciting concept here,' and 'I think they hit it out of the park.' The city's economic development director, Michael Williams, said the city was looking for a business to fill the building left empty by Bagel Place, and 'We felt like the residents would like the variety that [Wonder] offered.'

key_people: Holly Simmons, College Park City Councilmember
Kelly Jordan, College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Chris Gill, College Park City Council Candidate
Daniel Oates, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
50191.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has three new members after voters in Districts 2 and 3 elected newcomers to the dais on Nov. 4. Holly Simmons and Kelly Jordan won a three-way election for two District 2 seats, with Jordan beating opponent Chris Gill by three votes after a hand recount. In District 3, first-time candidate Daniel Oates won the seat that Councilmember John Rigg has held for eight years. As Jordan said, 'It's a good feeling... I'm happy to be able to serve the residents and excited about the next two years.' Simmons added that her first action will be to 'listen and learn. I think there's a lot of important work that's underway in the city.'

key_people: Brencis D. Smith, Laurel City Councilmember
Christine Johnson, Laurel City Councilmember
Holly Simmons, College Park City Councilmember
Christopher Gill, College Park City Council Candidate
Kelly Jordan, College Park City Council Candidate
50185.ai_summary: The results of the local elections in College Park and Laurel are uncertain, with some candidates running unopposed and others in tight contests. In Laurel, Brencis D. Smith may have an edge over incumbent Christine Johnson for the At-Large council seat, while in College Park District 2, Holly Simmons seems likely to have secured one of two seats on the council, with Christopher Gill and Kelly Jordan only one vote apart for the second seat. The mail-in and provisional ballots will be counted and results will be certified soon to determine the final outcome.

key_people: Chris Gill, College Park City Council Candidate
Kelly Jordan, College Park City Council Candidate
Holly Simmons, College Park City Council Candidate
Shawn Anderson, WTOP Radio Moderator
Llatetra Brown Esters, Former College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, Former College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
key_issues: restorative justice for the Lakeland community
50017.ai_summary: The three candidates for two vacant College Park City Council seats in District 2, Chris Gill, Kelly Jordan, and Holly Simmons, discussed their priorities at a candidates' forum hosted by College Park Here & Now. They agreed that restorative justice for the Lakeland community and affordable housing are key issues, with Simmons stating 'I remember [at] one of the events that I attended for restorative justice in Lakeland, a gentleman said, ‘I want my 40 acres and a mule,’” and Jordan suggesting that each new housing development should be required to incorporate some affordable housing units. The candidates are running to fill the seats of former councilmembers Llatetra Brown Esters and Susan Whitney, with incumbent Fazlul Kabir running unopposed for mayor.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
49848.ai_summary: College Park voters will elect a mayor and eight City Councilmembers in November, with early voting and Election Day voting taking place at designated locations. Fazlul Kabir, the current mayor of College Park, is running for re-election and has a vision for building a stronger, more vibrant College Park by expanding affordable housing. As Kabir states, he is committed to 'building a stronger, more vibrant College Park' and has a history of community involvement, including co-founding the annual College Park Day and launching the Hollywood Farmers Market.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Christopher Gill, College Park City Council Candidate
Kelly Jordan, College Park City Council Candidate
Holly Simmons, College Park City Council Candidate
49536.ai_summary: Three candidates, Christopher Gill, Kelly Jordan, and Holly Simmons, are running for two District 2 seats on the College Park City Council. Gill, an IT project manager, wants the city to be a place for people in all phases of life, and is concerned about housing affordability and owner-occupied homes being flipped into rentals, saying 'I don’t want people to be pushed out of their community.' Jordan, who has been active in District 2 civic life, is seeking to extend her service by running for city council, with a campaign rooted in representation, saying 'I want to make sure that there are lots of different interests that all get heard from and incorporated into the decision-making.'

key_people: Lucas Rigg, Son of College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Lovisa Jordan, Daughter of District 2 City Council Candidate
Kelly Jordan, District 2 City Council Candidate
Lazarus McCarthy, College Park Resident
Dylan Cooney-Barrera, College Park Resident
organizations: Prince George's County Board of Elections
49533.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted 5-3 to lower the voting age for city elections from 18 to 16, making College Park the ninth Maryland city to allow teens to vote. Lucas Rigg, a 16-year-old resident, said 'I think it lets the people who didn’t choose to live here have a say.' Lovisa Jordan, also 16, noted that preparing to vote could make teenagers more aware of what’s going on in the city and the candidates who want to run it, saying 'when we don’t vote, we don’t really have a reason to know.'

key_people: Danny Copeland, Vice President for RST Development
Orlando Velez, Director of Operations and Housing Initiatives for College Park City-University Partnership
Kendra McAbee, Communications Coordinator for College Park City-University Partnership
David Varnado, Senior Program and Operations Manager
organizations: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD)
locations: College Park–University of Maryland Metro Station
49529.ai_summary: The city of College Park offers affordable housing options, including the Flats at College Park, a 317-unit affordable housing community, and Atworth, a 451-unit apartment building. The College Park Community Preservation Trust also provides affordable homeownership options by selling single-family homes to those who earn less than 140% of the area's median income. According to Orlando Velez, the trust's permission is required for homeowners to sell the house, and the program has sold nine homes so far, as part of the city's efforts to address the lack of affordable housing in College Park.

key_people: Maxine Gross, Restorative Justice Commission Chair
Ruth Murphy, Restorative Justice Commission Member
Karin Brown, Restorative Justice Commission Member
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
50287.ai_summary: The Restorative Justice Commission is urging the College Park City Council to provide reparations to displaced Lakelanders, including financial compensation, housing priority, and free college tuition. As Commission Chair Maxine Gross said, 'When you talk to people who experienced Lakeland before urban renewal, they speak of a vibrant community.' The commission's proposals aim to address the harm done to the Lakeland neighborhood during urban renewal in the 1970s and 80s, and Mayor Fazlul Kabir has stated that city staff will help the commission identify what action the city can take to help meet the community's goals.

key_people: Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember District 1
Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
51542.ai_summary: College Park officials are considering annexing multiple properties, including the soon-to-be-vacant Beltsville Agricultural Research Center (BARC) and other nearby properties, to expand the city's boundaries. 'We're kind of just barely in the initial stages of talking about it,' said Councilmember Jacob Hernandez, 'We're trying to take a common-sense approach to every single one of these areas.' The city would consider annexing land to protect it, for economic development or to boost population, according to Mayor Fazlul Kabir.

key_people: Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Ryna Quiñones, College Park Communications and Events Manager
Connor Johnson, Former College Park Resident
Jhonny Ayquipa, Trumpet Player for La Rumbera Mayor
Danielle Delgado, College Park Supporter
Ricardo Morales, Berwyn Resident
49215.ai_summary: The City of College Park hosted its second annual Bailamos! event to celebrate Hispanic Heritage Month, with nearly 200 residents and visitors gathering at the College Park Woods Clubhouse. According to Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember, 'when you break bread, when you sit down with one another, and you have that opportunity to, no kidding, just engage, it's a whole other world.' The event's attendance was lower than the previous year, with some attendees suggesting that the country's political climate may have discouraged some potential attendees, as noted by Connor Johnson, who asked 'how is the broader political situation and hesitancy to go out and be visible in the community impacting turnout?'

key_people: Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Teresa Way-Pezzuti, College Park Director of Human Resources
key_issues: mayor and mayor pro tem salary increase
49170.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering a proposal to double the part-time salaries of councilmembers from $10,000 to $20,000 a year and increase the mayor's salary from $15,000 to $25,000. Councilmembers believe the increase is necessary to cover costs, with Jacob Hernandez stating 'Everyone on this dais works incredibly hard for the residents that we care for... The responsibility can be awesome, but taxing.' The proposed increase would cost the city $90,000 a year and take effect in January.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Miriam Bader, College Park Director of Planning and Community Development
Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Director
Richard Biffle, Property Owner
organizations: City of College Park Planning and Community Development Department
49106.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted to require owners of vacant homes and commercial properties to register their empty buildings with the city. As College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir wrote, 'Vacant properties can negatively impact our community... A registry will allow the city to track these properties, maintain up-to-date contact information for owners and ensure compliance with maintenance standards.' City Councilmember Jacob Hernandez agreed, stating 'It supports improving public safety... It protects property values.'

key_people: Maria Cordone, College Park Resident
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
49102.ai_summary: A city-commissioned noise analysis by RK&K found that traffic noise is 'clearly audible in the front yards' on Edmonston Road in College Park's Yarrow neighborhood, and warrants a more detailed study and potential noise mitigation by the State Highway Administration. 'I'm not surprised; I've been living it,' said Maria Cordone, a resident who owns one of the three homes where the study took place. The city is preparing to plant 80-plus trees and shrubs between Edmonston Road and Kenilworth Avenue to help with sound and beautification, according to College Park City Councilmember Ray Ranker.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember

49098.ai_summary: Five first-time candidates and five incumbents have filed to run for seats on the College Park City Council in the Nov. 4 election. Mayor Fazlul Kabir will run unopposed for re-election. The candidates are competing for seats in Districts 1, 2, 3, and 4, with some incumbents running without opposition. The election site is City Hall and early voting is Nov. 2 at Davis Hall.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Christian Shannon, CALS Chief
49093.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, who is also a volunteer paramedic, expressed his opposition to speed humps as a solution to speeding cars on Autoville Drive, citing his experience with patients in ambulances and the pain caused by speed bumps. Rigg suggested that a sidewalk would be a better solution. As a paramedic with 16 years of experience with Calvert Advanced Life Support (CALS), Rigg's expertise in emergency healthcare has influenced his decisions as a city official, including advising on taking council meetings online during the COVID-19 pandemic.

key_people: Nausicaä Sinclair, Calvert Hills Resident
Michelle Johnson, Executive Director of the Housing Authority of the City of College Park
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: Housing Authority of the City of College Park
U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
49087.ai_summary: The lack of affordable housing in College Park has led some residents, like Nausicaä Sinclair, to move away due to soaring rent prices. According to Michelle Johnson, Executive Director of the Housing Authority of the City of College Park, 'Affordable housing is a human right' and 'To me, affordable housing is being able to go to work, come home, pay your bills, pay your rent, without stress.' College Park City Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters emphasized the importance of diversity and affordability in the community, stating 'No matter how much money you make, you should be able to come in this community and find housing, housing that is suitable and housing that is quality.'

key_people: Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
49076.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmembers Llatetra Brown Esters, Susan Whitney, and John Rigg have decided not to run for re-election, citing the part-time job's impact on their personal and family time. As Esters notes, 'There are things that can happen in a given day that need my immediate attention … and focus' at her job, and 'my time and attention to what I do with the city is just as important.' Mayor Fazlul Kabir agrees that while there's a minimum time commitment required, being 'realistic about how much time and energy you have' is key to avoiding burnout in the role.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor

land use authority in College Park
code enforcement for single-family home conversions
49021.ai_summary: College Park officials, including Mayor Fazlul Kabir, met with Prince George's County Executive Aisha Braveboy to discuss issues facing the city, including the shutdown of the Beltsville Agricultural Research Center (BARC) and land use authority in College Park. Braveboy stated she has been looking into the closing of BARC since it was announced in July and opposes it. As Kabir noted, 'There are 2,600 people who [work at BARC], many of them live in College Park and the surrounding communities... and their livelihoods are at stake, but also, more importantly, many of them, when they work there, they also attend the businesses in College Park.'

key_people: Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park Councilmember
Jeffrey Ratnow, Cruz Development Corp Consultant
Matthew Tedesco, Attorney
Sandy Lora, Branchville Volunteer Fire Co. Ladies Auxiliary
Daniel Cruz Jr., Cruz Development Senior Vice President
Arelis Pérez, College Park Housing Authority Board Chair
48941.ai_summary: A Boston-based development company, Cruz Development Corp, has proposed an 87-unit affordable senior housing project at Branchville Road and University Boulevard. Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell expressed concerns about the affordability of the project, stating 'It seems like the rate of rent is still high for seniors on subsidized incomes.' The project's consultant, Jeffrey Ratnow, noted that tenants will pay based on their incomes, with prices reflecting 60% of the area median income for Prince George’s County.

key_people: Amira Abujuma, Deputy Student Liaison to the College Park City Council
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember (District 4)
48774.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is urging legislation against rent price-setting after a request by Councilmember John Rigg to support possible action against RealPage and other software companies. Amira Abujuma, the deputy student liaison, stated that 'Housing in College Park is not a luxury. It’s a necessity' and emphasized that 'Access to affordable housing is essential, and RealPage is a threat to that.' The council agreed to send a letter to state legislators and discuss potential further action at a future meeting, highlighting the need to address the high rent prices in College Park, which are 16% higher than the national average.

key_people: Charkera Ervin, African American Redress Network Legal and Policy Director
Keith Webster, College Park Restorative Justice Commission Member
Kayla Edwards-Scott, Contributing Researcher
organizations: Braxton Institute for Sustainability, Resilience and Joy
48751.ai_summary: Restorative justice advocates presented a report to the College Park City Council, urging the city to help the Lakeland community improve education, transportation, and housing. The report, compiled by the African American Redress Network and the Braxton Institute for Sustainability, Resilience and Joy, details the history and impact of urban renewal on Lakeland, with Keith Webster stating, 'Lakeland has a different look and a different feel, let's resurrect the spirit of Lakeland, the Lakeland that we once knew.' The report recommends compensation for homes lost during urban renewal, free college tuition, and public transportation passes, citing generational wealth loss and displacement caused by urban renewal, with Charkera Ervin noting that some families received as little as $1 for property worth more than $20,000.

key_people: Jim Miller, Parking Enforcement Manager
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember

48748.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering stricter parking enforcement measures, including reducing the number of free permits per household and charging residents to park on their streets. Councilmembers discussed the issue at their Aug. 6 meeting, with Susan Whitney suggesting extending enforcement hours and offering incentives to one-car households, while Maria Mackie noted that 'nobody likes permit parking' but 'we need to realize this is a quality-of-life issue.' The council will continue to discuss the issue, with Mayor Fazlul Kabir asking city staff to prepare talking points for another worksession, as Jacob Hernandez expressed opposition to charging taxpayers to park in front of their homes, saying he is 'vehemently against' it.

key_people: Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
48745.ai_summary: Three College Park City Council members, Llatetra Brown Esters, Susan Whitney, and John Rigg, have announced they will not seek re-election in November. Rigg stated he wants to 'place a much higher priority on … family events, see my friends and neighbors more often and spend time on personal pursuits' and that running for council every other autumn is a 'trade-off that I am no longer willing to accept.' College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir noted that the trio will be missed but their decision also gives an opportunity for new residents to run, saying 'If the same people keep running, those who are interested, they don’t get a chance. It’s very hard to run against incumbents.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Ryna Quiñones, College Park Communications and Events Manager
August Kenner, University of Maryland Police Captain
Rex Powell, College Park Woods Neighborhood Watch Coordinator
48698.ai_summary: College Park residents met with law enforcement officers and city officials at a National Night Out event to foster a safer community. 'The whole goal of National Night Out is to get the community connected with the law enforcement who try to keep us safe,' said College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir. The event, which featured snacks, music, and a bounce house for kids, aimed to build partnerships between the community and law enforcement, with attendees appreciating the chance to build stronger bonds with their community, saying 'It just makes you feel safer to know who’s actually working down there at the fire station, or who’s sitting in the police car around the corner.'

key_people: Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
48651.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmembers Llatetra Brown Esters and Susan Whitney, both representing District 2, announced they will not run for re-election in November due to increasing work and family demands. Esters stated, 'My need to be present for family has become a priority,' and Whitney wrote, 'My work and family responsibilities have increased substantially since the last election, and I no longer have the time and energy to provide the level of service District 2 residents deserve.' Their announcements, along with Councilmember John Rigg's decision not to run for re-election, will leave vacancies on the city council.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember

Stuart Adams, Former College Park City Councilmember
Mike Meadows, Old Town Resident
Gannon Sprinkle, University of Maryland Student
48611.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember John Rigg announced he will not seek re-election in November, citing a desire to prioritize family and personal pursuits. Rigg, who has served for eight years, noted that running a campaign every other year is 'tremendously expensive, mentally taxing and time-consuming', and urged the newly elected council to consider switching to four-year terms for elected officials, a move he said would 'continue a common-sense approach to alleviating the substantial personal burden imposed by our current system of elections every other year'. He endorsed his colleague Ray Ranker for re-election in November, saying 'I feel strongly that the time is right for new leadership in District 3'.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember

48606.ai_summary: The Prince George’s County Board of License Commissioners voted 4-0 to deny a liquor license application for College Park Liquors to reopen next to ZIPS Cleaners on Baltimore Avenue, citing the store's proposed location near the University of Maryland Child Development Center. As College Park City Councilmember John Rigg stated, 'This is unequivocally the wrong location, and the city council stands in unified opposition to it.' College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir testified that 'This is a wonderful community, a historic community that’s very well established, very family-oriented' and that 'the community has voiced very strong opposition and concerns about the introduction of a full-service liquor store so close to the Calvert Hills playground and the University of Maryland Child Development Center.'

key_people: Bob Catlin, Former College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Charlotte Kanner, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
organizations: College Park Vacant and Blighted Properties Committee
48293.ai_summary: Former College Park City Councilmember Bob Catlin remains civically involved, attending council meetings and sharing reflections on the city's history. Catlin serves on the city's Vacant and Blighted Properties Committee and the College Park Housing Authority, and is co-president of the Berwyn District Civic Association. As he stated, 'I've always been interested in history and policies and why things work the way they do,' and he continues to contribute to the community through various roles.

key_people: Oliver Mack, Reporter
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Julie Johnson, University of Maryland Assistant Director for Intramural Sports and the Reckord Armory
Ryna Quiñones, College Park City Communications and Events Manager

48292.ai_summary: The City of College Park has partnered with the University of Maryland's RecWell program to offer social sports, including volleyball and pickleball, to its residents at a discounted rate. 'Anything that gets people out and moving their bodies is a good thing,' said College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, who participates in the program. The program aims to promote community building and social interaction among residents, with the city allocating $30,000 to subsidize the registration fees, as Ryna Quiñones, City Communications and Events Manager, noted, 'We're happy to help reduce the barriers, to encourage our residents to not only meet their neighbors, but kind of engage in these fun and active activities.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Donald Hay, College Park Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee Co-Chair
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Evony Salmeron, Author
organizations: College Park Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee
48291.ai_summary: The College Park City Council will hold a public hearing on a proposal to make it a violation for cars to park or stand in bike lanes, which would affect city-owned streets but not Route 1, owned by the state. According to College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir, the rule would address complaints from bicyclists about cars and delivery trucks blocking bike lanes, making them unsafe for cyclists. Councilmember Susan Whitney stated, 'If we can't do this here, why can't we do this here? I see no reason why we can't accomplish that in College Park,' referencing her experiences with cyclist-friendly cities abroad, such as Montreal and cities in Spain.

key_people: Griffin Limerick, Journalist
Eduoard Haba, College Park City Councilmember
Danny Schaible, College Park City Councilmember

Sean Carlson, Ward 1 Resident
48237.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted 7-1 to purchase a 4.5-acre former parking lot next to Driskell Park for $6.5 million, with Councilmembers Eduoard Haba and Danny Schaible abstaining and Sam Denes providing the lone 'no' vote. The deal has been a point of contention due to the high price and Werrlein Properties' history of developing the land without required permits. As Ward 1 resident Sean Carlson stated, 'I find it galling that we would consider bailing out the developers who have not shown that they are capable of being good stewards of that land,' highlighting concerns about taxpayer funding and developer accountability.

key_people: Sharon O'Malley, College Park Here & Now Managing Editor
Mark Goodson, College Park Here & Now Former Managing Editor
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Chris Currie, Streetcar Suburbs Publishing Former Vice President and St. Jerome Academy Administrator
48233.ai_summary: College Park Here & Now, a monthly newspaper, is celebrating its fifth anniversary, with managing editor Sharon O'Malley stating 'I think that local news, local journalism, is more important now than ever.' The publication's success is attributed to its nonprofit business model and hyperlocal focus, with O'Malley noting 'You can get the big stories about College Park from The [Washington] Post or WTOP radio, but the really local, everyday stuff, they’re not going to cover that. We are.' Former managing editor Mark Goodson credits the community and city support, including a four-page advertising insert in each print edition, for the newspaper's success.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Kristi Noem, U.S. Department of Homeland Security Secretary
Wes Moore, Maryland Governor
48229.ai_summary: The U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS) removed a list of 'sanctuary jurisdictions' from its website, which included College Park and Hyattsville. According to a statement from College Park, 'the news of this designation … is surprising' and the city 'has and will continue to be a welcoming and inclusive community' and 'remains steadfast in our commitment to the safety and well-being of all our residents.' The list was removed after DHS Secretary Kristi Noem said it was in response to blowback from 'some of the cities' that were included.

key_people: Maria Cordone, Yarrow Resident
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Nicole Freeh, Highway Engineer and Yarrow Resident
Sharon O'Malley, Author
47367.ai_summary: Residents of the Yarrow neighborhood in College Park are seeking relief from noise pollution caused by traffic on Kenilworth Avenue, with some residents unable to have conversations in their yards due to the loud noise. As Maria Cordone, a Yarrow resident, stated, 'I can’t hear my neighbor talking to me,' and 'I can’t even sit out in the yard because of the noise.' The residents plan to meet with the city arborist to request the planting of noise-absorbing trees and are also seeking a sound study to determine potential solutions, including better speed enforcement or a barrier wall, although highway engineer Nicole Freeh notes that a barrier wall may not be effective due to the elevation of the homes.

key_people: Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Director
Kathryn Clay, PNC Senior Vice President
Qin Chih, Jumbo Jumbo Cafe Manager
Keith Bouchelion, Laser Essential Co-owner
47353.ai_summary: The City of College Park's Retail Reinforcement Mini-Grant Program, funded by PNC Bank, awarded $75,000 in grants to 15 local businesses to help offset losses from the ongoing Purple Line construction. As noted by Michael Williams, the city's economic development director, 'The money flew out the door' once the grant was announced, and 'It didn’t take long' for the funds to run out. Business owners, such as Qin Chih of Jumbo Jumbo Cafe and Keith Bouchelion of Laser Essential, have expressed gratitude for the grants, with Chih saying 'It’s helpful. It’s better than nothing' and Bouchelion stating it is 'very well appreciated, especially during these turbulent times and uncertainty.'

key_people: Ethan Pham, CP Flea Organizer
Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
Gabrielle Brinkhoff, Shop Made in Maryland Store Manager
Oliver Mack, Journalist

key_issues: unpermitted use of City Hall plaza
47336.ai_summary: City officials shut down the CP Flea market on College Park's City Hall plaza in March after it had operated for nearly a year without permission. According to Kenny Young, College Park City Manager, 'From the city's point of view, it's a pretty simple issue for us: They were operating on the City Hall plaza without permission.' Ethan Pham, the market organizer, was caught off guard by the shutdown, saying 'It just felt completely out of left field, like, I had no idea this was coming' after he had been working with Shop Made in Maryland to host the market for nearly a year with no issues.

key_people: Maxine Gross, Lakeland Community Heritage Project Chair
Mary Anne Hakes, Explorations on Aging Organizer
Oliver Mack, Author
47289.ai_summary: University of Maryland students have been working on senior capstone projects to preserve the history of College Park's Lakeland community and support city-based organizations. Maxine Gross, chair of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project, said 'It is helpful for us to see what [the students are] thinking about, and … it’s helpful for them to see how the things that they learned about in a classroom actually work with real-life projects.' The projects benefit both the students and the community organizations they support, including Explorations on Aging, which is working with students to create a website for the city's senior population.

47278.ai_summary: The College Park City Council will hold a public hearing on May 6 to discuss the city's proposed $29.6 million fiscal year 2026 budget, which would increase the budget by 0.9% over the previous year. The proposal would add three full-time staff members to City Hall but would not raise residential property taxes. The council must adopt a budget resolution later this month.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
47284.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted 5-3 to lower the voting age for city elections from 18 to 16, starting with the November election. Councilmember John Rigg championed the measure, saying it is designed to boost the number of residents who vote in local elections and to instill a sense of civic duty in teenagers. Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell and Councilmembers Maria Mackie and Alan Hew opposed the change, with Mackie suggesting a youth advisory council and Hew proposing a resident vote on the issue.

key_people: Dhruvak Mirani, University of Maryland Student Government Association President and Student Liaison to the College Park City Council
Dhruvak Mirani, University System of Maryland Board of Regents Student Member
organizations: University System of Maryland Board of Regents
47268.ai_summary: Dhruvak Mirani, the 2023-24 University of Maryland student liaison to the College Park City Council, has been elected as the president of the school's Student Government Association. Mirani, a government and politics major, also serves as a student member of the University System of Maryland's Board of Regents. He previously helped create a pilot project for the city to give subsidies to student renters, and his new role may impact College Park governance and student representation.

47339.ai_summary: The College Park Moose Family Center Lodge #453 is preparing to relocate after 75 years due to financial difficulties and the resale of its building to Mekane Hiwot Medhane Alem Tigray Orthodox Tewahedo Church. 'I hate it because we wanted to get back our building,' said Debbie Herbert, the lodge's president. The lodge is struggling to find a new location in College Park, with available buildings being too small or lacking sufficient parking, and has appealed to the College Park City Council for help, with City Councilmember Denise Mitchell working with the group to find a new site.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Ryna Quiñones, College Park Communications and Events Manager
Manel Lacorte, University of Maryland Language Professor
Molly Rubinstein, University of Maryland Graduate and Greenbelt Resident
47186.ai_summary: The 27th annual Maryland Day at the University of Maryland drew thousands of people, with City of College Park officials and staff participating by handing out information about the city. 'It's building community, bringing people together in one place and showcasing how strong we are as a community,' said College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir. The event included over 400 free, family-friendly activities and helped to foster a sense of community between the university and the city, with attendees such as Molly Rubinstein, a 1975 UMD graduate, saying 'Whatever the City of College Park can do to make students feel welcome, not only on campus but throughout the community, is very important.'

key_people: Daniel Gruner, Professor of Entomology at the University of Maryland
Karin Burghardt, Associate Professor of Entomology and Extension Specialist at the University of Maryland
Laura Rost, National Coordinator of Bee City USA
Audrey Bengtson, Author
46919.ai_summary: The City of College Park has waived its 12-inch limit for lawn height in April to support the No Mow April initiative, allowing native pollinators like bees and butterflies to feed and find habitats. According to Daniel Gruner, a professor of entomology at the University of Maryland, 'Pollinators need floral resources in order to get the energy they need to build their broods up, especially early in the season.' Karin Burghardt, an associate professor of entomology, noted that 'No Mow April is a great communication tool to connect in folks' minds that their management strategies in their yards have consequences for insects.'

key_people: Thomas Dernoga, Prince George’s County Councilmember District 1
Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember District 3
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember District 2
organizations: Meals on Wheels of College Park
46928.ai_summary: County Councilmembers Thomas Dernoga and Eric Olson warned of an 'extremely difficult' budget cycle due to uncertainty surrounding federal grants and budget cuts. 'The federal government is doing so much damage,' Olson said, noting the county relies on $267 million in federal grant money. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated that the city is not proposing a property tax increase in its upcoming budget and has attracted 16 new businesses, with a draft budget proposal totaling $29.6 million.

key_people: Ryna Quiñones, College Park Communications and Events Manager
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Jalen Wade, Author
Robert Thurston, Lakeland Civic Association President
Daniel Oates, Calvert Hills Citizens Association President
46925.ai_summary: The City of College Park has started a text alert system to keep residents informed about events, news, and emergencies. According to Ryna Quiñones, College Park Communications and Events Manager, 'We're always looking for new ways to reach our residents,' and using texts will help the city communicate with residents more effectively. Residents can sign up for news and events by texting COLLEGEPARK to 38276 and for emergency alerts by texting CPALERTS to the same number, with alerts able to be geo-targeted to specific areas, as noted by Mayor Fazlul Kabir and community leaders like Robert Thurston and Daniel Oates.

key_people: Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Hua Wang, Owner and Chef of Li Chun Café and Northwest Chinese Food
Xue Ling, Brand Manager of Li Chun Café and Daughter of Hua Wang
Lillian Glaros, Author and Journalist
46916.ai_summary: Li Chun Café, owned by Hua Wang, has opened in College Park City Hall with a ribbon-cutting ceremony attended by College Park City Councilmember Maria Mackie, University of Maryland students, and local politicians. Mackie stated, 'It’s really a … wonderful addition and a really unique place,' and expressed her excitement about the café. The café serves northeastern Chinese cuisine, including chun bing wraps, and has been popular with customers who like the 'vibes' and enjoy 'healthy food and teas in between work and studies'.

key_people: Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Fidel Alfaro, Alfaro Barbershop Owner
46869.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember Jacob Hernandez has been helping Spanish-speaking business owners, including Fidel Alfaro of Alfaro Barbershop, access city information and resources. Hernandez, who learned English as a second language, supports translating city documents into Spanish and has advocated for a bilingual approach to engage both English and Spanish communities. As Alfaro said, 'He always had time to get here,' and with Hernandez's guidance, Alfaro's shop received $14,000 in economic development grants for renovations, demonstrating the impact of Hernandez's efforts on the local business community.

key_people: Sharon O'Malley, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Jatinder Khokhar, College Park Director of Public Services
46866.ai_summary: College Park's city manager has proposed a $29.6 million budget for fiscal year 2026, which does not raise property taxes and includes an 8.5% increase in employee salaries and benefits. As Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated, 'Increasing property taxes has never been popular... This year, thankfully, it’s not happening.' The proposal adds three full-time staff members to city hall, including an economic development coordinator, a deputy city manager, and a public safety manager, aiming to improve compensation, economic growth, and community safety.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor

46822.ai_summary: City officials in College Park are preparing for the economic fallout from recent federal employee layoffs, with approximately 1,100 federal workers living in the city. 'We are not really prepared for it,' said College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir, as the city assesses the potential impact on residents and the city's fiscal year 2026 budget. 'We're going to just see a slowdown in some of the retail activities,' said Economic Development Director Michael Williams, as the city considers options to help displaced federal employees.

key_people: Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember

46529.ai_summary: Ray Ranker, a University of Maryland chaplain, was sworn in as the new District 3 College Park City Councilmember after winning the special election with 344 votes. Ranker stated, 'There are just a lot of amazing people who are, in so many different ways, making our community stronger, more connected, better. I'm excited to build on that.' He plans to focus on city budget planning and aims to be a good steward of the community's resources, saying 'I can really advocate for the kinds of changes that folks in [District 3] want to see reflected in the annual budget that's coming up.'

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember

key_issues: Delayed project timeline and increased costs
46499.ai_summary: College Park officials and community members gathered at Duvall Field for a groundbreaking ceremony, kickstarting the renovation of the field into a seven-acre park for sports, entertainment, and community events. The project, which includes over a dozen amenities such as an amphitheater and sports courts, has been delayed several times due to inadequate funds and increased construction costs, with the estimated cost rising from $5.6 million in 2019 to $7.3 million in 2024. As Councilmember Jacob Hernandez stated, 'I picture a place where seniors can gracefully age in place and just enjoy the largest park in the city and the fresh air that it’ll bring,' and Mayor Fazlul Kabir added, 'I think of the future of Duvall Field … [and] I know times are difficult right now, and you might not feel welcome, but Duvall Field will be a welcoming place for all.'

key_people: Jalen Wade, Author
Matt Hurson, Cape Advisers Representative
Michael Harris, University of Maryland Student
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember (District 2)
46411.ai_summary: The 60-year-old University Club at College Park on Berwyn House Road will undergo a major renovation starting this summer, with Cape Advisers investing $10.5 million into the property. Tenant Michael Harris noted issues with the current state of the building, saying 'I would say sometimes the lighting goes out... sometimes the air conditioning doesn’t work that much.' City Councilmember Susan Whitney expressed concern over potential rent increases, stating 'There is a problem with a lack of affordable housing in general in the city, but also specifically for students,' and that her assumption is the renovations will lead to higher rents.

key_people: Ray Ranker, University of Maryland Chaplain and College Park City Councilmember
Michael Meadow, Lifelong Old Town Resident
Gannon Sprinkle, University of Maryland Student
Stuart Adams, Former College Park City Councilmember
Sharon O’Malley, Author
46379.ai_summary: Ray Ranker, a University of Maryland chaplain, has won the special election for the District 3 College Park City Council seat, receiving 338 out of 504 votes. Ranker stated, 'I’m grateful that people entrusted me with this responsibility' and plans to dive into city budget planning, saying 'It’s one of the best opportunities to help reflect our city’s values and hopes through how we spend our money, [and] to be good stewards of that process.' He will take the oath of office on March 18 and his term will end in November.

key_people: Katelynn Winebrenner, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
Gannon Sprinkle, College Park Tenants Union Co-Founder and former Deputy Student Liaison to the College Park City Council
Dhruvak Mirani, College Park Tenants Union Co-Founder and 2023-24 Student Liaison to the College Park City Council
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember, District 3
Julie Palakovich Carr, Maryland Delegate
Anthony Brown, Maryland Attorney General
46407.ai_summary: The College Park Tenants Union is calling for an investigation into accusations of price-fixing by landlords of six large apartment complexes in College Park, citing the use of RealPage's algorithmic pricing software. As Gannon Sprinkle, a co-founder of the union, stated, 'Local involvement is necessary to protect tenants from predatory pricing, and we would urge the city council to investigate the nature of RealPage.' The average rent in College Park is $1,865 per month, 20% higher than the national average, and renters in buildings using RealPage's algorithm pay an average of $112 more per month in the D.C. metro area, according to a White House Council of Economic Advisers analysis, with Dhruvak Mirani, a co-founder of the tenants union, noting that 'The actual cost of housing is one of the most frequently brought-up concerns.'

key_people: Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Hua Wang, Owner and Chef of Northwest Chinese Food and Li Chun Café
Xue Ling, Brand Manager of Li Chun Café
Lillian Glaros, Author and Journalist
46382.ai_summary: The grand opening of Li Chun Café, owned by Hua Wang, was attended by dozens of community members, University of Maryland students, and local politicians, including College Park City Councilmember Maria Mackie. Mackie stated, 'It’s really a … wonderful addition and a really unique place,' and expressed excitement about the new café. The café, located on the first floor of College Park City Hall, offers traditional northeastern Chinese dishes, including chun bing wraps, and has already gained popularity among customers who enjoy its healthy food and teas.

key_people: Denise Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Anna Owens, Former College Park Mayor
P.J. Brennan, Former College Park City Councilmember
key_issues: Gender representation on the City Council
46437.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmembers Denise Mitchell, Maria Mackie, Susan Whitney, and Llatetra Brown Esters reflect on their journeys as female leaders in a male-dominated field. Mitchell notes that 'This is still a man's world in regard to politics,' and Whitney recalls that 'Some people might be more skeptical of your abilities, perhaps because you're a woman.' The women agree that there is room for more female leaders in government, with Mackie commenting that 'I think that women are really coming up to the plate.'

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Charlotte Kanner, Author
46342.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has proposed an amendment to the city code that would require residents to store tables used for drinking games, such as beer pong, in areas not visible from the street. Councilmember John Rigg stated, 'One thing we’ve always struggled with, one very specific piece of furniture, and that is a beer pong table,' and 'People really don’t appreciate drinking games next door … on their street when they are trying to encourage their children to have healthier approaches to alcohol than is exhibited by such drinking games.' Councilmember Jacob Hernandez questioned the restrictions, saying 'It is a targeted policy to curb [the] behavior of a particular group of people.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
Maria Mackie, College Park Councilmember
Eric Olson, County Councilmember
Mary Anne Hakes, Explorations on Aging project member
Oliver Mack, Author
46348.ai_summary: The College Park Woods Clubhouse has opened to the public after a year of construction, with a ribbon-cutting ceremony attended by city officials and residents. As College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated, 'This is a really, really wonderful time for us to celebrate. This is your second home. Please enjoy it.' The new space is expected to bring a stronger sense of community to broader College Park, as resident Mary Anne Hakes hopes it 'will unite us as a city' and Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell believes it fulfills part of the city's strategic plan to bring College Park together.

key_people: Sherry Felix, High Point High School Title I Coordinator and English Teacher
Audrey Bengtson, Journalist
46388.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has provided a $2,750 grant to support High Point High School's Flying from the HP Nest program, which supplies care packages for students heading to college. According to Sherry Felix, High Point High School Title I Coordinator and English Teacher, 'this is just a small treat' and hopes the program will expand with community support. The grant will help approximately 14 students with care packages and 42 students with registration fees for the nonprofit First Generation College Bound, aiming to increase the number of low-income students who graduate from high school and move on to college.

key_people: Riley Bomgardner, Journalist
Erica Otte, College Park City Council Non-Voting Student Liaison
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember, District 3
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember, District 1
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember, District 2

Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem, District 4

Hamilton Reynolds, Landmark Properties Representative
46331.ai_summary: The College Park City Council pushed back against a proposal by Landmark Properties to demolish the 63-year-old College Park Towers and replace it with luxury high-rise apartment buildings, citing concerns over the lack of affordable housing in the area. As Erica Otte, the council's non-voting student liaison, said, 'Luxury apartments are replacing affordable housing,' and Councilmember John Rigg agreed, stating 'What we have on site right now is an example of pretty good, market-rate affordable housing that would be difficult to replicate and if it is gone, then it is gone.' The councilmembers, including Alan Hew and Llatetra Brown Esters, argued for the inclusion of affordable housing units in the new development, with Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell stating, 'We need to be more collective in letting students find housing that is affordable and that they can afford, not what you’re trying to sell.'

46336.ai_summary: A special election will be held on March 11 to fill the vacant District 3 College Park City Council seat temporarily. The city council has approved the purchase of three drop boxes for voters to mail their ballots at a cost of $9,481.25. A regular city election will be held on November 4 to select a full council, including a mayor and all eight councilmembers.

key_people: Joel Carlson, Volunteer
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Ellen Tappin, Fight Colorectal Cancer Member
46287.ai_summary: The City of College Park partnered with Fight Colorectal Cancer to host an event on March 9 to raise awareness about colon cancer and the importance of getting screened. Councilmember Alan Hew, who has personal experience with the disease, said 'Something about health and personal issues like this aren’t very well versed in minority communities, so we’re trying to build opportunities for them to learn about it, speak about it, without social pressures and things like that.' Ellen Tappin, a colon cancer survivor and Fight Colorectal Cancer member, headed a booth with resources and information on getting screened, aiming to encourage people to get screened as colorectal cancer is the No. 2 cause of cancer deaths in the nation.

key_people: Michael Meadow, Federal Government Worker
Ray Ranker, UMD Lutheran Campus Ministry Chaplain
Gannon Sprinkle, University of Maryland Junior and Former Student Liaison to the College Park City Council
Shawn Anderson, WTOP Anchor
Stuart Adams, Former College Park City Councilmember
Riley Bomgardner, Author
Donald Trump, President of the United States
46182.ai_summary: The three candidates for the vacant District 3 City Council seat, Michael Meadow, Ray Ranker, and Gannon Sprinkle, participated in a 90-minute candidates' forum at city hall, where they discussed their plans to support federal workers who have lost their jobs. The candidates agreed that the city should offer financial assistance, job search resources, and referrals to mental health agencies, with Sprinkle stating, 'I believe that we need a strong safety net, and there have to be city programs in place to both help people find new employment and to stop as much of this bleeding that we can.' They also opposed any effort to dismantle the city's diversity, equity, and inclusion programs, with Meadow saying, 'Absolutely do not scrap' these programs, and Ranker stating he would 'like to have it both ways: continue doing [DEI] while still retaining federal dollars and support.'

key_people: Mike Meadow, College Park City Council Candidate
Ray Ranker, College Park City Council Candidate
Gannon Sprinkle, College Park City Council Candidate
Shawn Anderson, WTOP Radio Moderator
46022.ai_summary: The College Park Here & Now will host a forum for the three candidates for the vacant District 3 City Council seat, including Mike Meadow, Ray Ranker, and Gannon Sprinkle. The forum will take place on Wednesday, Feb. 26, at City Hall in the City Council Chambers, and will be moderated by Shawn Anderson of WTOP radio. The event is open to the public and can also be attended via Zoom.

key_people: Wes Moore, Maryland Governor
Sharon O'Malley, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
Jim Rosapepe, Maryland State Senator for District 21
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Ben Barnes, Maryland Delegate for District 21 and House Appropriations Committee Chair
Joseline Peña-Melnyk, Maryland Delegate for District 21
45972.ai_summary: Maryland Governor Wes Moore's proposed $67.3 billion budget for fiscal year 2026 includes funding for improvements to Route 1 from Greenbelt Road to the Beltway. As noted by state Sen. Jim Rosapepe, 'Route 1 is the front door to the University of Maryland; it’s our flagship university and we want people, when they come, to feel good about the community.' College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir agreed, stating 'Route 1 is our Main Street, plain and simple' and that the project will make it 'very nice.' The upgrades aim to improve traffic safety with dedicated left-turn lanes, bike lanes, and wider sidewalks, and will be guided by community input.

key_people: Gannon Sprinkle, University of Maryland Student Government Association Executive Vice President
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
