organizations: University of Maryland Student Government Association
45947.ai_summary: Gannon Sprinkle, a University of Maryland junior and Student Government Association executive vice president, is running for the District 3 College Park City Council seat. Sprinkle, who has experience as a legislative intern and advocate for students, aims to 'bridge the gap' between student residents and long-time residents of the city, saying 'I do believe that students deserve a voting member of the city council.' If elected, he plans to focus on issues such as affordable housing, food equity, and bicycle and pedestrian safety, stating 'I wish to bring that experience and representation to the city council.'

key_people: Michael Jacobs, Former College Park Mayor
Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember
Andrew Fellows, Former College Park Mayor
Kathy Bryant, College Park Resident
45962.ai_summary: Former College Park Mayor Michael Jacobs, who served as an elected official in the city for two dozen years, died on Jan. 13 at the age of 81. According to Prince George’s County Councilmember Eric Olson, Jacobs was a dedicated public servant who was 'pretty much at almost every meeting' despite his demanding career at the National Security Agency. As mayor, Jacobs played a large part in establishing relations with the University of Maryland and was one of the leaders of the Quality of Life Committee, focused on updating city codes, and was also open to talking with residents one-on-one, as noted by College Park resident Kathy Bryant, who said 'He was never too busy to talk to me, or I’m sure, to anybody else who wanted to talk to him.'

key_people: Josephine Johnson, Author
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Stefan Leggin, former Brentwood Vice Mayor
Tiffany Green, County Fire Chief
organizations: Coalition to Support Prince George’s County Fire and EMS
45951.ai_summary: The College Park City Council agreed to sign a letter to candidates running for Prince George's County executive, seeking increased funding to hire 150 new firefighters and emergency medical service staff. The Coalition to Support Prince George's County Fire and EMS drafted the letter, which also asks for support in reducing barriers to onboarding volunteer firefighters and increasing transparency between the county Fire/EMS Department and local officials. As Stefan Leggin, former Brentwood Vice Mayor, stated, 'We can use this election as leverage to elevate the need for meaningful change, to elevate the conversation... We take it to the top. We want them to know that staffing our fire departments needs to be a top priority.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Donald Trump, President of the United States
SHARON O’MALLEY, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
organizations: Food and Drug Administration’s Center for Food Safety and Applied Nutrition
45903.ai_summary: College Park residents, including federal employees and documented migrants, are feeling the impact of President Donald Trump's executive orders, with one resident saying she is 'shaking like a leaf every day'. The city's mayor, Fazlul Kabir, stated that 'the morale of the community is very low' and acknowledged that 'there are so many jobs of federal employees at stake'. The city has published resources to support those affected and issued a 'One College Park' proclamation, which declares the city's commitment to 'excellence and kindness, with inclusion and hope, with balance and optimism' and promises to 'reject and resolve attitudes of fear, pessimism or cynicism'.

key_people: College Park City Council, College Park City Council
Vote16 representatives, Vote16 National Organization
45978.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has postponed voting on a proposed charter amendment to lower the voting age for city elections to 16 until after the vacant District 3 council seat is filled on March 11. A couple of residents spoke in opposition, while multiple representatives of Vote16 spoke in favor of the change. The council also postponed a vote on a second proposed charter amendment that would allow the mayor and council to appoint a replacement council member if a seat is vacated less than a year before the next election.

45975.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is forming two citizen task forces, one for childcare and one for affordable housing. The Childcare Task Force will monitor state legislation and explore programs to lower the cost of care, while the Affordable Housing Task Force will research the city's housing needs and propose a program to offer affordable housing. The task forces will be appointed by the mayor and council.

key_people: Josephine Johnson, Journalist
Maxine Gross, Founding Chair of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember (District 2)
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember (District 2)
45563.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has approved a contract with Columbia University to develop a plan for reparations to the Lakeland community, which was damaged by urban renewal in the 1970s and 1980s. As Maxine Gross said, 'I think it’s a really great start,' and Councilmember Susan Whitney added, 'It is important to have a well-researched, well-documented, academically sound record of what happened in Lakeland and what parties were involved in that occurring, either by actions or inactions.' The city aims to establish an accurate historical record of Lakeland and provide reparations regarding economic stability, education, and health care.

key_people: Janeen Miller, Age Friendly College Park Program Lead
Chyna Diaz, Habitat for Humanity Metro Maryland Director of Home Preservation
Oliver Mack, Author
45424.ai_summary: The College Park Aging in Place program, part of a nationwide effort by Habitat for Humanity, aims to make homes more accessible for senior citizens with limited incomes. As Janeen Miller, Age Friendly College Park Program Lead, said, 'It’s the home repair and improvements that we can help seniors with that will allow them to stay in their homes as they age.' The program has upgraded half a dozen homes in College Park so far, with funding from the American Rescue Plan Act, and plans to work on at least 10 homes a year, with the goal of becoming a long-term solution for the city's senior citizens.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Robert Day, former College Park City Councilmember
Sharon O'Malley, College Park journalist
key_issues: University of Maryland student housing subsidies
45574.ai_summary: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember, has resigned from his position, citing the demands of the role and the recent birth of his second child. As Adams noted, 'I just knew that my ability to dedicate the time that the council involved … was constrained.' The city will hold a special election to fill his seat on March 11, with two candidates already announcing their intention to run. Adams' resignation is seen as a loss for the council, with Mayor Fazlul Kabir stating 'He's very smart, he has done quite a bit of work, even though it's been only three years now.'

key_people: Michael Meadow, College Park City Council Candidate
Ray Ranker, College Park City Council Candidate
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
45420.ai_summary: Two candidates, Michael Meadow and Ray Ranker, have announced they will run for the District 3 College Park City Council seat vacated by Stuart Adams. Meadow, a lifelong College Park resident and University of Maryland graduate, aims to help the city 'grow and prosper, but at the same time I want to take care of the small business owners.' Ranker, also a University of Maryland graduate, wants to 'think of our government as a way to engage people more broadly' and advocate for a city that is 'livable, walkable, bikeable, a place that can offer support to students, to families, to seniors.'

key_people: Alan Bradford, College Park Volunteer
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Joseph Perry, Son of Late Councilmember John Edward 'Jack' Perry
Casey Glickman, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
John Edward 'Jack' Perry, Late College Park City Councilmember

Martin Klapac, College Park Resident
organizations: College Park Boys and Girls Club
45413.ai_summary: The College Park City Council presented Alan Bradford with the 2024 Jack Perry Award for his 42 years of volunteer service, recognizing his impact on the community. As Mayor Fazlul Kabir wrote, 'Alan's countless acts of service ... have fostered a spirit of community and improved the quality of life for residents, embodying the legacy of Jack Perry himself.' Bradford's volunteer work includes coaching sports, managing the College Park Boys and Girls Club, and participating in community events, earning him recognition as a College Park icon.

key_people: Jalen Wade, Author
Michelle Johnson, College Park Housing Authority Director
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: US Department of Housing and Urban Development
45409.ai_summary: The Attick Towers, a 108-unit apartment building in North College Park, has started its third year of renovation, which could last up to five more years and cost up to $16 million. According to Michelle Johnson, College Park Housing Authority Director, 'The Housing Authority needs around $14 million right now,' and the money so far has come from the city's American Rescue Plan Act funds and Prince George's County grants. As Councilmember Susan Whitney noted, 'It's a building that's, you know, about 50 years old, and there's a lot of deferred maintenance,' due to inadequate funding from the federal Department of Housing and Urban Development over the years.

key_people: Sharon O'Malley, Author
College Park City Councilmembers, College Park City Council
45598.ai_summary: The College Park City Council will hold a public hearing on February 4 to discuss a proposal to change the minimum voting age in College Park from 18 to 16. Councilmembers who support the amendment believe that teenage voters tend to grow up to be civically engaged adults. If the amendment passes, College Park residents who register to vote in Prince George's County and will be at least 16 on Election Day will automatically become registered to vote in the city.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Robert Day, College Park Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park Councilmember
Sharon O'Malley, Author
45174.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember Stuart Adams announced his resignation, effective January 8, due to family commitments. As a young father, Adams stated, 'As a young father, my availability as a city councilmember has become more constrained,' and 'I must reflect, and based on weighing my priorities, I have decided to resign.' The city charter requires a special election to fill his seat within 65 days, with the replacement holding the seat until the next general election in November.

key_people: Josephine Johnson, Journalist
Maj. James Keleti, Hyattsville Division 1 Police
David Mitchell, University of Maryland Police Chief
Lt. Rosanne Hoaas, University of Maryland Police Department
Deputy Chief Stephen Boehm, Metro Transit Police
Yasmin Brown, Maryland-National Capital Park Police Assistant Chief
45050.ai_summary: The University of Maryland Police Department is increasing enforcement of traffic rules for electric scooter riders on campus, with stops involving failure to stop at stop signs, riding on sidewalks, and failure to obey lane directions. According to Lt. Rosanne Hoaas, 'The reality is, oftentimes, they don’t realize that they need to be in the road and that they need to follow the rules of the road, like in a car, truck or SUV.' The police department is also working to address scooter thefts, with at least 70 reported on campus this year. Maj. James Keleti noted that offenders on scooters are hard to pull over, and the Prince George’s County Police Department does not chase speeding scooters due to safety concerns.

key_people: Lisa Eally, Meals on Wheels of College Park Chair
Jalen Wade, Streetcar Suburbs Reporter
Ijeoma Opara, Streetcar Suburbs Reporter
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: Meals on Wheels of College Park
45028.ai_summary: Meals on Wheels of College Park, which moved to Riverdale in 2021, is not scheduled to return to College Park any time soon due to lack of a suitable location. The organization's chair, Lisa Eally, said 'We're doing whatever we can to find a location back in College Park.' The College Park City Council had allocated $800,000 in American Rescue Plan Act funds to support the organization's move, but reallocated the money to other projects after a potential location at Flats at College Park fell through. Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that the city has 'put emphasis [and] substantial dollars' into supporting nonprofit food services, saying 'The support that the city has pushed toward these types of services are magnitudes [more than] what it used to be.'

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember

Rick Hoehn, contractor
Sharon O’Malley, author
45025.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted unanimously to spend $7,354,000 to renovate Duvall Field, a project that has been delayed for 19 years due to funding issues. Councilmembers discussed the high cost of the project, with Councilmember Alan Hew stating 'Making greatness in our city costs, and the longer we wait, the more it’s going to cost' and Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters agreeing that delaying the project would only increase costs. The renovation plans include adding soccer and baseball fields, an event amphitheater, and other amenities to the seven-acre park.

key_people: Jalen Wade, Author
Kahyla Hernandez, College Park Resident
Jacob Hernandez, College Park Councilmember
Mona Lynch, College Park Resident
Michael Williams, College Park Director of Economic Development
44853.ai_summary: A Ross Dress For Less discount store opened in North College Park on October 12, bringing 70 to 110 new jobs to the area. According to College Park Councilmember Jacob Hernandez, 'If you see across the city, we don’t have any major clothing store options... Ross provides, you know, high quality clothing and merchandise for low cost and affordable prices.' The store's opening is expected to be a major economic driver for the city, and residents such as Kahyla Hernandez and Mona Lynch have expressed excitement about the new addition, with Hernandez saying 'I think it’s good... It’s a refresher, considering that we’re [near] campus and there’s a bunch of college students, so I think they would appreciate having something so close' and Lynch saying 'This one is up and it’s new and it’s by a Home Depot and a food store so it brings a lot more to this little shopping plaza here.'

key_people: Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
Keith Hooks, Local Representative of the National Association of Letter Carriers

key_issues: Staffing problems at the North College Park Post Office
Impact on senior residents and retirees
44850.ai_summary: North College Park residents are complaining about inconsistent mail delivery, with some days having no mail at all, and others receiving mail for the wrong address. According to Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem, 'There's maybe two or three days that a resident will not get mail at all,' and many senior residents are not receiving their pension or Social Security checks in a 'timely fashion.' Keith Hooks, a local representative of the National Association of Letter Carriers, stated that the post office has had an 'onslaught' of new, inexperienced employees, following a rash of retirements of long-time mail carriers.

key_people: Yasmin Brown, Assistant Chief of the Maryland-National Capital Park Police
Lt. Brian Wiesemann, Commander of the Maryland State Police College Park Barracks
David Mitchell, UMD Police Chief
Maj. Jim Keleti, Commander of the Prince George’s County Police Hyattsville Division 1
Stephen Boehm, Deputy Chief of the Metro Transit Police
44814.ai_summary: Theft is the most common crime in College Park, according to law enforcement agencies, with thefts from automobiles and thefts of automobiles being most prevalent. As Yasmin Brown, Assistant Chief of the Maryland-National Capital Park Police, stated, “Most of our incidents are theft-related,” and “A lot of that is a crime of opportunity: people leaving something in their car or leaving their car unlocked.” Overall, crime in College Park is down by 11%, with property crime like theft from autos declined by 15%, but violent crime is up by 6%.

key_people: Stephanie Stullich, Former College Park City Councilmember
Rebecca Bailey, College Park Animal Welfare Officer
Patricia Coil, Volunteer with Beltsville Community Cats
organizations: City of College Park Animal Control
44810.ai_summary: Stephanie Stullich, a former College Park City Councilmember, has been fostering stray cats and finding them forever homes, with the help of the city's animal welfare officer and the nonprofit Beltsville Community Cats. Stullich has taken in up to 19 cats at a time and has helped find homes for 120 stray cats in four years. Patricia Coil, another volunteer, has been fostering kittens through Beltsville Community Cats and says 'You bring them in, and you never know what you're going to get, They each develop their own personality. It's just cute to watch them … become cats over the course of, like, two or three months.'

key_people: Jim Rosapepe, Maryland Senator (District 21)
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
William Pines, Maryland State Highway Administrator
Charlie Gischlar, State Highway Administration Deputy Director
44790.ai_summary: Maryland Senator Jim Rosapepe and College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir attended a ribbon-cutting ceremony for the completion of Phase 1 of the U.S. Route 1 Improvement Project. As Rosapepe stated, 'We're hoping that we can get the planning [for the rest of the project] in this coming year's budget,' and he plans to work with the Legislature to secure funding for the next phases. The project, which began in 2020 and cost $56.9 million, aims to improve safety and infrastructure along Route 1, with future phases extending to I-495.

key_people: Joel Biermann, Former University Park Mayor
Patrick Wojahn, Former College Park Mayor
Debi Sandlin, University Park Town Administrator
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Lillian Glaros, Author

organizations: Prince George’s County State’s Attorney’s Office
44763.ai_summary: Former University Park Mayor Joel Biermann was arrested on October 28 and charged with 28 counts relating to child sexual abuse materials. According to court documents, most of the alleged offenses occurred in October 2024, but three date back to October 2021. This arrest comes about 20 months after former College Park Mayor Patrick Wojahn was arrested on 56 counts of child pornography, and is a significant issue for College Park governance as it involves the misconduct of public officials in neighboring jurisdictions.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Hua Wang, Owner of Northwest Chinese Food
Xue Ling, Brand Manager of Northwest Chinese Food
44760.ai_summary: Northwest Chinese Food was named College Park's 2024 Business of the Year by Mayor Fazlul Kabir, who stated that 'Their efforts have made College Park a more vibrant and diverse place to live.' The restaurant, known for its unique Chinese cuisine, has been a staple in the community since 2015 and will be relocating to the city hall building to make way for new student apartments. As Councilmember Stuart Adams noted, the restaurant 'demonstrates the diversity of Chinese cuisine' and is a popular spot among students and residents, like Ashfiha Rahman and Flavyne Tsongwain, who appreciate its consistent quality and cozy atmosphere.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Dhruv Maheshwari, Obvio Co-Founder
Susan Whitney, College Park Councilmember (District 2)

Sharon O’Malley, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
44571.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering the installation of stop sign cameras around the city after a three-week pilot program showed that up to 92% of drivers did not come to a full stop at certain locations. As Councilmember Susan Whitney said, 'I just wanted to make sure there was an opportunity for public comment and really frame this in a clear and public way.' The program's goal is safety, not profit, with Councilmember Maria Mackie stating, 'Our motivation is safety,' and 'The city’s not making big money on this.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
John Carroll, Galena Mayor
Raymond Rodriguez, University of Maryland School of Public Policy
organizations: University of Maryland School of Public Policy
44540.ai_summary: College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir emphasized the importance of resident engagement in building trust in local government, stating 'You are doing it to serve the people. You are no one but the public servant.' Kabir and Galena Mayor John Carroll spoke at a 'Living Democracy' symposium at the University of Maryland, discussing the need for mayors to prioritize local issues over party affiliation. Kabir noted that his daily blog, started in 2009, has helped him connect with residents and increase transparency in city government, saying 'That actually helped me in connecting with the residents and being more transparent about the city government.'

key_people: Andreé Sahakian, Senior Development Manager at LV Collective
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Sam Gauntt, Author
organizations: College Park Department of Planning & Community Development
44458.ai_summary: A Texas-based development company, LV Collective, is planning to build a mixed-use housing complex at the site of the former Campus Village Shoppes on Route 1 in College Park, which will add a privately-owned student housing option with 299 units and approximately 13,000 square feet of retail space. As Andreé Sahakian, Senior Development Manager at LV Collective, stated, 'With each development, we look for creative ways to collaborate with the community, embracing its history and honoring its character and culture.' The project has faced some community backlash, but City Councilmember Susan Whitney noted that the city is 'really, really trying to keep a lot of those beloved businesses here,' and some affected businesses have been invited to return to the space once the building is finished.

key_people: Ijeoma Opara, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
Sydney Nicole, University of Maryland Student
Michael Williams, College Park Director of Economic Development
Dan Zmijewski, Rally House Attorney
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember, District 1
Llatetra Esters, College Park City Councilmember, District 2
organizations: College Park Department of Economic Development
44465.ai_summary: The College Park City Council approved a $130,000 grant for Rally House, a sports apparel store, to move into the former Target storefront on Baltimore Avenue. As Sydney Nicole, a University of Maryland student, said, 'I think it’ll be nice... A lot of people around this area, they wear athleisure, Lululemon, Nike, Adidas; all that’s very popular around College Park, especially with college students.' The city's Director of Economic Development, Michael Williams, noted that the vacancy has been 'economically detrimental to the city' and that Rally House is expected to hire up to 70 employees and commit to staying in College Park for at least seven years.

key_people: Josephine Johnson, Author
Carlos Johnson, Hollywood Elementary School Principal
Lindsay Sullivan, Amplify Associate Vice President
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember (District 4)
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember (District 1)
44455.ai_summary: The College Park City Council approved $40,000 for a tutoring program for Hollywood Elementary School’s Spanish-speaking students. The program, provided by Amplify Tutoring, focuses on students in third to fifth grades and aims to equip them with the literacy skills they need for middle school. As City Councilmember Jacob Hernandez said, 'If I could, I would try to carve [the] entirety of our budget to try and help the schools that we have here in College Park,' and Councilmember Maria Mackie noted she was 'very impressed with how [Amplify does] their tutoring and their success that they had [at Hollywood Elementary] in just four short months of last year, that they brought the students’ reading ability up'.

key_people: Kit Slack, Streetcar Suburbs Executive Director
44445.ai_summary: Kit Slack, Executive Director of Streetcar Suburbs, thanked over 200 readers for taking the first audience survey for College Park Here & Now and its sister publications. Readers expressed their desire for more in-depth city council coverage and comprehensive news, with some suggesting the need for paid reporters. The publication plans to deepen and broaden its conversations with readers and community leaders about local news, with a priority on news and information that gives broad access to civic engagement.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Christina Cross, UMD Child Development Center Director
Ashley Burkett, Author
organizations: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services
locations: University of Maryland Child Development Center
44448.ai_summary: College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir sent a letter to state legislators asking for a review of day care rules that require one staff member per three children under 24 months, citing significant economic burdens on families in College Park. Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that Maryland has one of the most restrictive staff-to-child ratios in the US, and the cost of child care at the University of Maryland Child Development Center is $27,744 a year for infants and toddlers, saying 'it's just so economically impractical for them'. A change in the state's required staff-to-student ratio could take two or three years, according to Adams.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
Anne Healey, State Delegate (District 22)
44437.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has installed cameras on nine stop signs around the city to collect data on traffic and parking. Councilmember Stuart Adams noted the cameras were placed at heavily trafficked intersections, and said 'there could be more review later.' State Delegate Anne Healey, who sponsored legislation authorizing the cameras, said 'I expect that once the cameras are in place and people start getting tickets, they will change their behavior and actually stop at the stop signs where the cameras are,' in response to concerns about people ignoring stop signs and causing near misses and accidents.

key_people: Mike Wells, Senior Director of Franchise Operations for The Greene Turtle
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
SAM GAUNTT, Author
Tiffanii Rogers, College Park Resident
44147.ai_summary: The Greene Turtle, a sports bar and grill, is opening a new location in the College Park Shopping Center, which will bring a new casual dining option to the city. 'We're happy and we're really excited to be in College Park,' said Mike Wells, Senior Director of Franchise Operations for The Greene Turtle, who noted that the restaurant has long partnered with University of Maryland Athletics. City Councilmembers Susan Whitney and Stuart Adams expressed enthusiasm for the new business, with Whitney noting that she has some concern about the restaurant staying within its allowed limit of alcohol sales, which under the business's Prince George's County Liquor Board agreement, can only account for up to 35% of the location's sales.

key_people: Madison Korman, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Jen Holz, Maryland AARP Representative
Gail Lovelace, Age-Friendly College Park Initiative Co-Chair
Mary Anne Hakes, Senior Advisory Committee Co-Chair
key_issues: quality of life for older adults
44115.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is working towards achieving an age-friendly designation from the AARP, which indicates the city offers a good quality of life for older adults. As Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember, stated, 'I think that we need, as a city, to make College Park a place that people want to retire to and people want to continue living here... to make College Park an age-friendly community for all ages.' The city's volunteer Senior Advisory Committee began working towards the designation in 2020, focusing on five of AARP's eight domains of livability, and hopes to earn the designation by next summer.

key_people: Paul Ruffins, Author
Aaron Goldbeck, University of Maryland Bicycle Coordinator
44031.ai_summary: The city of College Park has explored various micromobility options, including bike-sharing programs and dockless e-scooters, to reduce traffic congestion and climate pollution. According to the Union of Concerned Scientists, ride-hailing trips result in an estimated 69% more climate pollution on average than the trips they displace. As Aaron Goldbeck, University of Maryland Bicycle Coordinator, said, 'We are very excited about the growth and success of the system,' referring to the bike-sharing program in College Park.

key_people: Susan Slingluff Hartmann, College Park City-University Partnership Executive Director
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Bode Ramsay, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
43934.ai_summary: The College Park City-University Partnership plans to spend $3 million to build an off-campus bike lane along Campus Drive to connect to the Trolley Trail. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir hopes to extend the trail north to Beltsville, stating 'Maybe in the future we could extend even beyond College Park, because bicycle transportation is not that great once you go past [here]', and citing the danger of biking in Beltsville. The Trolley Trail's recent extension in Hyattsville has added features to enhance pedestrian and cyclist safety, and the partnership is working to install art projects and improve trail safety in College Park.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Sharon O’Malley, Author
43946.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering a proposal to lower the voting age to 16, with Councilmember John Rigg stating that 'the younger they start participating in elections, the more likely they will regularly vote as adults.' The council previously declined to continue an effort to lengthen their terms from two years to four. A straw poll showed that councilmembers decided 5-3 to schedule a future discussion on allowing 16- and 17-year-olds to register to vote in city elections, with Rigg noting that 'as a matter of mathematics and a matter of political economy, there’s only about 300 residents ages 16 and 17 in the city.'

key_people: Katelynn Winebrenner, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
Brian Galbraith, Cherokee Lane Elementary Principal
Kayla Newsome, Cherokee Lane Elementary Assistant Principal
Bernitta Johnson, College Park Academy Signature Programs Coordinator and Business Program Adviser
43931.ai_summary: Eight local schools have received a combined $36,800 in grants from the City of College Park to fund educational projects, including an outdoor classroom, experiential learning opportunities, and a pitch competition for aspiring student entrepreneurs. As Cherokee Lane Elementary Principal Brian Galbraith said, 'They give schools these opportunities to think outside the box.' The grants allow schools to enhance their educational offerings, with Cherokee Lane Elementary using its grant to purchase portable picnic tables and canopies for an outdoor classroom, where 'our children to explore and discover the world around them in ways that a traditional classroom simply cannot,' according to Assistant Principal Kayla Newsome.

key_people: Sam Gauntt, Author
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Varaa Kukreti, University of Maryland Graduate Student Government President
Edward Maginnis Jr., University of Maryland Assistant Vice President for Real Estate
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: University of Maryland Graduate Student Government
43919.ai_summary: The University of Maryland is planning to build a new graduate student housing complex, Leonardtown apartments, near downtown College Park, which is expected to be completed in August 2026. College Park City Councilmember John Rigg said, 'It'll be a vast improvement over the status quo,' and 'It'll provide much needed housing at below market for some of our residents who have the most financial needs.' The development will offer 465 units and create a new street, Graduate Way, and is seen as a positive impact on the local economy, with City Councilmember Stuart Adams saying it's 'absolutely the type of development that we've been asking for in College Park.'

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Edward Gibbs, Property Lawyer
James Woodhouse, Autoville Drive Resident
Sam Gauntt, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
43838.ai_summary: The College Park City Council recommended approval of a four-story, eight-unit apartment building on Autoville Drive, despite concerns from residents about the potential disruption to their quiet neighborhood. Councilmember Alan Hew praised the property owner for being receptive to the city's suggestions, saying 'It’s rare that we have an applicant that’s so cooperative to the conditions put on them.' Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell cast the sole vote against the project, stating 'I will be voting no regarding this project due to the fact that it will change the character of the community, the quality of life of the residents.'

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Julia Bauer, National League of Cities Research and Data Analysis Program Manager
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Department Director
Katelynn Winebrenner, Author
43835.ai_summary: The City of College Park has spent $19 million of the $22 million in federal pandemic funding it received through the American Rescue Plan Act (ARPA) of 2021. The city used the funds to help residents pay bills, subsidize local businesses, and invest in affordable housing, with small businesses receiving $1.7 million in assistance. According to College Park City Councilmember Stuart Adams, 'Without question, that was something unprecedented in the City of College Park,' and the city must use the remaining $3 million by December 31, 2026.

key_people: Janeen Miller, College Park City Clerk
Yvette Allen, College Park Assistant City Clerk
Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Joe Nagro, former College Park City Manager
Lillian Glaros, journalist
43564.ai_summary: Janeen Miller, College Park City Clerk, will retire on August 1 after 18 years in the position and will be succeeded by Assistant City Clerk Yvette Allen. Miller stated, 'More free time to myself to do the things that I put on the back burner and enjoy life without the responsibility of waking up to an alarm clock,' and expressed her readiness for the freedom that comes with retirement. City Manager Kenny Young and Mayor Fazlul Kabir praised Miller's dedication and work ethic, with Kabir saying, 'She reminds us, she keeps us on track. She's very punctual. She gets also very honest.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Tiffany Green, Prince George’s County Fire Chief
Bode Ramsay, Journalist
Sharon O’Malley, Journalist
organizations: Branchville Volunteer Fire Co. & Rescue Squad
Berwyn Heights Volunteer Fire Department & Rescue Squad
43553.ai_summary: The Prince George’s County Circuit Court denied a request by College Park, Greenbelt, and Berwyn Heights to stop the relocation of paid firefighters from local fire stations, which may delay responses to emergencies. As College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'The damage is already done,' and 'The staffing reallocation will be definitely impacting College Park residents.' The relocation of 55 firefighters, including 24 from Greenbelt and six from Berwyn Heights, has raised concerns about the impact on emergency response times in College Park, particularly during the summer when University of Maryland students, who make up the bulk of the city’s unpaid firefighters, are not in the city.

key_people: Hannah Frasure, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Director
Michael Bodaken, Prince George’s County Council’s Rent Stabilization Working Group Member and University of Maryland School of Public Policy Adjunct Instructor
Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember (District 3)
Griffin Benton, Vice President of Government Affairs
organizations: College Park Office of Economic Development
University of Maryland School of Public Policy
43581.ai_summary: The Prince George’s County Council is set to approve a 3% rent cap, which would cap rent hikes at 3% a year, plus the rate of inflation, up to 6%. However, buildings constructed after 2000 would be exempt from the law, which could result in a low impact on College Park, as up to 65% of apartment buildings in the city could be exempt. According to Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Director, 'My takeaway personally, and at the first swipe of it, is that because of all the new construction that really boomed since 2012, that we’re going to have a very small or very little effect on the 3% cap,' and Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember, stated that the council worked 'toward a fair compromise that will achieve all the goals that we want: protecting tenants from, you know, outrageous gouging and that kind of thing, while also making sure that what we do doesn’t inhibit investment in the county.'

key_people: Angela Alsobrooks, Prince George’s County Executive
Tiffany Green, Prince George’s County Fire Chief
Anna Bedford-Dillow, Greenbelt News Review
Matthew Neus, Greenbelt News Review
Randy Chow, Greenbelt News Review
William Snoddy, Judge
43462.ai_summary: The cities of Greenbelt, Berwyn Heights, and College Park filed a joint motion for a Temporary Restraining Order against Prince George’s County Executive Angela Alsobrooks and Fire Chief Tiffany Green to prevent the removal of career firefighters from Greenbelt and Berwyn Heights. The motion argued that the county's summer staffing plan, which relies on volunteers, will result in 'drastic shortages of adequate fire, rescue, and medical personnel' and pose a 'much greater risk of catastrophic injury' to residents. Judge William Snoddy rejected the motion, primarily due to the plaintiffs' failure to demonstrate irreparable harm, as the county argued the plan is for the department's health and safety and in the public interest.

key_people: Katelynn Winebrenner, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
Mike Galvin, SavATree Consultant Group Director
Richard Jones, University of Maryland Campus Arborist
organizations: College Park Department of Public Works
43393.ai_summary: The city of College Park has seen a decrease in tree cover on residential properties between 2009 and 2020, despite an overall growth in tree canopy throughout the rest of the city. According to Mike Galvin, director of SavATree's consultant group, the city's efforts to limit tree removal and encourage planting are 'great proactive steps' toward slowing or stopping the loss of tree canopy in neighborhoods. The report found that 38.7% of College Park is covered by tree canopy, with a net gain of 12.6 acres' worth of cover between 2009 and 2020, and University of Maryland Campus Arborist Richard Jones noted that 'having forest is so critical, not only for the environment, but for our ability to go about our day-to-day lives in a city'.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Ryna Quinones, College Park Communications and Events Manager
Sharon O'Malley, Author
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
43119.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is questioning the future of the annual spring parade, with Councilmember John Rigg suggesting that the city consider getting out of the parade business due to high costs and low participation. The city spent $34,000 on this year's parade and invested approximately 1,000 staff hours worth another $34,000, with Rigg stating that the total funding boils down to approximately $47 per attendee, saying 'That's not good, I can't think of any way to justify that.' The council is considering alternatives to the parade, such as hosting a music festival or a summer concert series, to achieve the objective of community-building and engagement.

key_people: Ken Ulman, President of Terrapin Development Co.
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Kayla Nazaire, Author
Heather Kingry, Representative of Primetime
Sahil Patel, University of Maryland Junior Computer Science Major
organizations: University of Maryland College Park Foundation
43121.ai_summary: Shake Shack, Chopt, and Duck Donuts are set to open in College Park this fall, marking a significant development in the city's downtown area. According to Ken Ulman, President of Terrapin Development Co., 'Shake Shack, you know, frankly, they have been looking for a location in College Park for a number of years' and will be part of the new student housing building, Union on Knox. City Councilmember John Rigg expressed excitement about the new additions, saying 'I have a passion for small businesses, but I know these will be great additions to College Park that members of the community will enjoy.'

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
SAM GAUNTT, Author
43109.ai_summary: The city of College Park offers multiple programs to help homeowners save on property taxes, including the Homestead Property Tax Credit and the Homeowners' Property Tax Credit. According to College Park City Councilmember Stuart Adams, 'I would say without hesitation that College Park has some of the most generous property tax credits of any municipality in Prince George’s County and some of the strongest in the entire state.' These programs can provide significant savings for eligible homeowners, especially with the recent 11% increase in the residential tax rate in College Park.

key_people: Erica Otte, University of Maryland Student Liaison to the College Park City Council
John Fahmy, University of Maryland Deputy Student Liaison to the College Park City Council
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Dhruvak Mirani, former University of Maryland Student Liaison to the College Park City Council

Maryland Office of the Public Defender
43126.ai_summary: Two new University of Maryland student liaisons, Erica Otte and John Fahmy, will start their year-long terms with the College Park City Council in June. Otte, who was selected by a panel including the mayor and councilmembers, said 'I'm very passionate about advocacy' and aims to 'make it easier for students to connect with the broader community'. Councilmember Maria Mackie praised Otte's vision, saying 'The biggest thing was her real desire to build bridges between students and long-term residents,' and that she 'doesn't want to do the 'us and them' thing'.

key_people: Katelynn Winebrenner, Journalist
Teresa Way-Pezzuti, College Park Director of Human Resources
Rick Campbell, Paypoint HR Technical Director
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
43045.ai_summary: The City of College Park is increasing salaries for 74 employees after a study showed they earn less than their peers in other cities in the region. According to the study, city employees earn, on average, 1.2% more than the 50th percentile of the cities surveyed, but half of the city departments fall below the 50th percentile. As Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters said, 'I think it's vitally important for us to bring our employees to a fair compensation and wage, especially if we want to maintain them,' and Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell noted that the city could aim to meet the 60th percentile benchmark in the future.

key_people: Iris Vukmanovic, The Washington Post Reporter
43114.ai_summary: The Washington Post featured the North College Park neighborhoods of Hollywood, Branchville, and Daniels Park in an article about the residents' advocacy efforts. The community's engaged neighbors, who know how to work together, saved the Hollywood Farmers Market through an email-writing campaign that sent nearly 100 messages of support to College Park City Councilmembers. The article notes, “The community benefits from engaged neighbors who know how to work together,” and highlights the community's passion for walking and biking and its sense of community due to its tremendous diversity.

key_people: Gary Fields, College Park Director of Finance
Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
Brian Roan, Daniels Park Resident
Bonny Davis, Long-time College Park Resident
Sharon O'Malley, Author and Journalist
42752.ai_summary: The College Park City Council adopted a $27.4 million operating budget for fiscal year 2025, which includes an 11% residential property tax rate increase. As Gary Fields, Director of the College Park Department of Finance, stated, 'We do not take proposing a property tax increase lightly... We're looking to the future to make sure we stay financially sound.' The increase will raise the residential rate from 30.18 cents to 33.5 cents per $100 of a home's assessed value, with some residents, like Brian Roan and Bonny Davis, objecting to the change, citing concerns about rising costs and the impact on long-time residents and homeowners.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Angela Alsobrooks, U.S. Senate candidate
Glenn Ivey, U.S. Congress representative for Maryland District 4
42023.ai_summary: Councilmember Stuart Adams discussed the city's flood abatement project in the Calvert Hills neighborhood, which has finally entered the contractor selection phase after over 12 years. Adams noted that current codes are not up-to-date with climate change, stating 'We need to acknowledge that this is going to happen a lot more frequently than people want to believe.' The article also covers the results of the May 14 primary elections, including Angela Alsobrooks winning the state Democratic primary for U.S. Senate and Glenn Ivey winning the Democratic primary for his seat in the U.S. Congress.

key_people: Maxine Gross, Chair of the College Park Restorative Justice Commission
Maxine Gross, Chairwoman of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project
Katelynn Winebrenner, Author
Ruth Murphy, Lakeland Civic Association Vice President
Robert Thurston, Lakeland Civic Association President
41839.ai_summary: Maxine Gross, a fifth-generation Lakelander and Chair of the College Park Restorative Justice Commission, has dedicated her time to preserving the history of Lakeland and sharing the stories of its community. As Gross said, 'It looked like nirvana... It looked like paradise,' but the city's urban renewal effort ultimately displaced families and destroyed 104 single-family homes. Gross, described by Ruth Murphy as 'a visionary' and 'an incredible gift,' has played a major role in ensuring a new Lakeland community center will be included in a student housing development, which will also store a physical, historic archive of Lakeland.

key_people: Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
Carson Peters, UMD School of Public Health Doctoral Student
Megan Ravert, Sustainable Terps Liaison
Mark Williams, UMD School of Music Jazz Studies Program Director
Adelia McGuire, Author
41833.ai_summary: The City of College Park participated in Maryland Day, a celebration at the University of Maryland, to offer information about local resources to attendees. As City Manager Kenny Young said, 'I think it’s an important event just to showcase what the University of Maryland offers to the community,' and 'It gives people a chance to come in and really have an experience of the University of Maryland and its sense of community.' The city set up a tent with city staff, including animal control and communications teams, and offered free materials and activities, including a chance to meet the mayor and city councilmembers.

key_people: Rachel Romeo, University of Maryland Professor
Susan Slingluff Hartmann, College Park City-University Partnership Executive Director
MADISON KORMAN, Author
organizations: Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development
41822.ai_summary: The College Park City-University Partnership has ended an eight-year homeownership program that awarded $15,000 to 83 new homebuyers, and has launched a new housing assistance program called Live + Work College Park. The new program offers a forgivable loan of $20,000 to support full-time employees working for any employer in the city, with the goal of supporting homeownership and neighborhood stabilization. As Rachel Romeo, a University of Maryland professor, said, “it was kind of an extra bonus for me, to give me an opportunity to live in the community that I work in and do research with.”

key_people: Eric Olson, Prince George's County Councilmember (District 3)
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
Daniel Oates, Calvert Hills Citizens Association President
Sharon O'Malley, Author
41817.ai_summary: The Calvert Hills stormwater drainage project in College Park is moving forward after over a decade of delay, with contractor selection underway and work expected to begin by mid-summer. As Prince George's County Councilmember Eric Olson (District 3) said, 'It's unfortunate that this project took this long, but I'm glad that we're finally at the stage where it [went] to bid.' The project aims to divert stormwater from the neighborhood into an underground concrete vault at Calvert Park, preventing pooling on streets and lawns and addressing the community's flooding issues, which have resulted in significant damage to homes in the past.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Ryna Quiñones, College Park Communications and Events Manager
Betty Colonomos, College Park Senior Advisory Committee Member
Elena Argueta, 2024 Miss College Park
Rigo Ayala, College Park Resident

41846.ai_summary: The City of College Park held its third annual Star Wars-themed parade on May 4, despite the rain, and it attracted hundreds of residents, including many in costume. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'It's wonderful, I've met quite a few neighbors, and I'm very pleased that they came with their families.' The parade is seen as an important community building event, with Ryna Quiñones, the city's communications and events manager, stating that 'there's something for everybody, for every age, and [our offerings are] very diverse.'

42280.ai_summary: The article lists the board of directors, including Marta McLellan Ross, Melanie Dzwonchyk, Joe Murchison, Michael Walls, Nora Urbieta Eidelman, and Merrill Hartson, who have backgrounds in various fields such as journalism, law, and non-profit work. Nora Urbieta Eidelman serves on the Ethics Commission for the City of College Park, Maryland and the Maryland Cannabis Public Health Advisory Council. The board members have diverse experiences and are involved in different organizations, including the College Park United Methodist Church and the Laurel Historical Society.

key_people: Kelly Jordan, Berwyn District Civic Association Co-President
Bob Catlin, Berwyn District Civic Association Co-President
Edward Najjar, Translator
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
41659.ai_summary: The Berwyn community in College Park has been publishing a regular newsletter for 50 years, and two years ago, the Berwyn District Civic Association started translating it into Spanish. As Kelly Jordan, co-president of the association, said, 'It always had been in English, and then we had enough of a Spanish-speaking population here' that offering the newsletter in two languages made sense. The city has proposed a grant to pay for translators for civic associations' newsletters and a full-time bilingual communications coordinator, which College Park City Councilmember Jacob Hernandez supports, saying 'I’m all about getting folks who speak Spanish to be a part of the larger community.'

key_people: Sharon O'Malley, Journalist
Bill Gardiner, College Park Assistant City Manager
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
41342.ai_summary: The Hollywood Farmers Market in College Park is set to open on April 13, despite concerns from city staff about its cost, with the city considering increasing its budget to $30,000 next year. Assistant City Manager Bill Gardiner noted that 'In the big scheme of our budget, it's not very much money,' but added that 'At the same time, in terms of comparable farmers markets, it's still unusual.' The market's value to the community was debated by city councilmembers, with Mayor Fazlul Kabir stating that 'This place is something that they really love. They want to come and see other community members.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
41366.ai_summary: In his annual State of the City address, College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir highlighted the 'exponential growth' the city experienced in 2023, with 10 new businesses opening and 250 new jobs created. The city is expecting nearly 20 new businesses and restaurants to open in the coming year, and will add two student housing complexes and three affordable housing complexes through grants and partnerships. Kabir also noted the city's efforts to improve sustainability, diversity, and safety, and announced sizable donations to community organizations, including $300,000 to fire companies and $500,000 to local food banks.

key_people: Sharon O'Malley, Journalist
Gary Fields, College Park Finance Director
Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
41323.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering a 13% increase in residential property taxes, which would be the first hike in 10 years, to help fund a $27.4 million budget proposal for fiscal year 2025. According to Gary Fields, the city's finance director, 'It's a reset... We're doing it for the future. … We feel like that we will not have to be looking at [another] rate increase in the near future.' The proposed increase would add $156 a year to the property tax bill of a $390,000 home, and councilmembers have shown little support for alternative money-saving strategies such as cutting city services or laying off staff.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Dhruvak Mirani, University of Maryland Student Liaison to College Park City Council
Gannon Sprinkle, University of Maryland Deputy Student Liaison to College Park City Council
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
41358.ai_summary: The application for student liaison to the College Park City Council is open until April 12, with the student liaison and deputy liaison serving one-year terms and earning stipends of $3,600 and $3,000 respectively. According to College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir, 'The ideal candidate would attend the meetings, work with [city council] in a collaborative fashion and who has a genuine concern about College Park on both sides of Route 1.' Current student liaison Dhruvak Mirani said, 'Our city ought to be responsive to student interests, but sometimes it’s really hard to believe that, see[ing] it play out in reality,' and he would like the city council to give voting status to the student liaisons.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Mary Lehman, Delegate of Prince George's and Anne Arundel
Julie Palakovich Carr, Delegate of Montgomery
Wes Moore, Governor of Maryland
Sam Gauntt, Author
41348.ai_summary: The Maryland General Assembly has passed two bills to increase penalties for street racing and allow Prince George's and Montgomery counties to install noise-sensing traffic cameras. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated that 'It is not unusual for groups of motorcycle and ATV drivers to briefly take over sections of Baltimore Avenue on Route 1' and that 'These actions harm our communities, and these actions waste … resources, especially the police resources.' The new law will increase fines and raise the number of points added to the licenses of drivers found participating in these activities, with Del. Mary Lehman noting that 'The fact is, exhibition driving will continue to disrupt our communities, endanger law enforcement and risk serious bodily harm, or worse, to participants and passersby.'

key_people: Kayla Carter, College Park Racial Equity Officer
Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
41374.ai_summary: The City of College Park is investigating its racial equity officer, Kayla Carter, after news reports revealed she posted remarks on her personal social media accounts that some consider offensive. According to reports from Fox News Digital and the New York Post, Carter used social media to call police 'white supremacists' and post other race-related comments. City Manager Kenny Young stated, 'Ms. Carter’s views expressed on her personal accounts do not reflect the opinions, beliefs and core values of the City of College Park and its mayor and council.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Derek Gunn, Maryland State Highway Administration District 3 Engineer
Larry Hogan, Former Maryland Governor
Gregory Slater, Former Maryland Transportation Secretary
Jonathan Iacovacci, Journalist
Kieran Leonard, Sweetgreen Manager
Kayla Scott, Looney's Pub Manager
41191.ai_summary: The first phase of the Route 1 improvement project in College Park is set to finish this spring after a year-long delay. The project, which began in 2020, will widen the street to make room for bike and turn lanes, and includes adding pedestrian lighting, resurfacing the road, and landscaping the medians. As College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'The changes, once it's happened, will make Route 1 very nice... It's not about making the street pretty, it's more than that... It's for our safety.'

key_people: Danny Copeland, RST Development Vice President
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Kate Kennedy, District 1 resident and former College Park City Councilmember
Kayla Nazaire, journalist
organizations: Meals on Wheels of College Park
41187.ai_summary: Construction on the Flats at College Park, a 317-unit affordable apartment building on Baltimore Avenue, is expected to begin this spring, offering two- and three-bedroom apartments with income-based rents. The project, developed by RST Development, aims to revitalize the community and provide affordable housing for low-income tenants, with monthly rents subsidized by the federal Low-Income Housing Tax Credit Program. As Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember, noted, the project will offer employees of the University of Maryland, the Agricultural Research Center, and NASA affordable housing close to work, but also raises concerns about traffic congestion on Cherokee Street, with Kate Kennedy, a District 1 resident, saying 'the idea is that we want as much traffic on Route 1 as possible and as little traffic in the neighborhoods.'

key_people: Sam Gauntt, Here & Now Reporter
Griffin Limerick, Life & Times Managing Editor
41131.ai_summary: The city of College Park voted to explore imposing a tax on vacant and/or blighted properties, as it can be more profitable for owners and developers to keep a property vacant. The city is hoping to identify the right tax structure and language to make that math work more towards ending vacancies. Griffin Limerick, Life & Times managing editor, also calls for a return of in-person council meetings in Hyattsville, noting that neighboring cities like Takoma Park, Greenbelt, and College Park have already done so.

key_people: Sam Gauntt, Journalist
Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
40635.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted to form a subcommittee to explore imposing a tax on vacant and blighted properties in the city. According to College Park City Manager Kenny Young, 'We’re in a position now that [we] kind of have to force the issue and get people to take more of an interest in making sure properties are maintained in College Park.' Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that a tax on blighted and vacant properties would 'move us in the right direction' and 'makes landlords think twice about what they’re doing with their property and compels them to make a few extra calls on filling up those retail spots.'

key_people: Maxine Gross, Chairwoman of the College Park Restorative Justice Commission
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember (District 2)
Joanne Braxton, CEO and President of the Braxton Institute
Mindy Fullilove, Author and Workshop Leader
Nkechi Taifa, Book Club Guest
Lillian Glaros, Author
40745.ai_summary: The College Park Restorative Justice Commission has partnered with the Braxton Institute to host a series of events in Lakeland about the community's history and restorative justice. Maxine Gross, Chairwoman of the Restorative Justice Commission, stated that 'if you don’t learn your history, you’re destined to repeat it' and emphasized the importance of understanding what happened in Lakeland, where over 100 homes were demolished from the 1960s to the 80s as part of an urban renewal project. The events aim to raise awareness about urban renewal in Lakeland and how restorative justice can be accomplished, as Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember, noted that 'it's different to hear the facts of what happened and to really go into the impact of them and how other communities have felt about that and how it has impacted people psychologically'.

key_people: Katelynn Winebrenner, Journalist
Bonnie McClellan, Vice Chair of the College Park Woods Neighborhood Watch
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember (District 4)
Robert Marsili, College Park Director of Public Works
organizations: College Park Department of Public Works
40680.ai_summary: The College Park City Council approved a $1.3 million contract with Rainbow Construction Co. to build a new community center in College Park Woods, with construction expected to start in March and finish by the end of the year. According to Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember (District 4), 'Many of our activities have been underneath the gazebo in the pouring rain, so it'll be a nice thing to have for the District 4 community, but also for the city.' The new community center will provide indoor space for various gatherings and events, and Bonnie McClellan, Vice Chair of the College Park Woods Neighborhood Watch, said 'This is a dream come true, it will make a very big difference for our community–and the entire community–because it’s going to be a very usable space.'

key_people: Kaya Carter, College Park Racial Equity Officer
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Katelynn Winebrenner, Author
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission
40750.ai_summary: The College Park Restorative Justice Commission proposed changes to park names to honor Lakeland history, including renaming Lake Artemesia Natural Area and the College Park Community Center. The commission also called for the establishment of a scholarship fund for Lakeland residents, which would be an acknowledgment of the 'enduring spirit and resilience of Lakelanders.' Councilmember Jacob Hernandez said, 'I just want to thank you all for the very thoughtful steps to try to address some of the harder parts of our history here in College Park, but also trying to pay homage to the brighter future that we have.'

key_people: Katelynn Winebrenner, College Park Reporter
Christine Johnson, Laurel Councilwoman
Pamela Boozer-Strother, Prince George's County Board of Education Member
organizations: Prince George's County Board of Education
40533.ai_summary: The City of College Park is being criticized for its lackluster celebration of Women's History Month, with only a static exhibit at city hall. Meanwhile, the Washington Commanders' headquarters is relocating to the University of Maryland's Discovery District, which may attract other prominent companies and impact traffic on River Road. The Prince George's County Board of Education is also addressing school transportation issues, with a parent stating that their child's bus has been at least 30 minutes late to school daily, and board member Pamela Boozer-Strother referencing a 2011 audit with similar recommendations, saying 'Let's be honest, we've been talking about the bell times in many task forces on transportation for years.'

key_people: Dhruvak Mirani, University of Maryland Liaison to the College Park City Council
Gannon Sprinkle, University of Maryland Deputy Liaison to the College Park City Council
William Edwards, University of Maryland Senior Studio Art Major
40245.ai_summary: Two University of Maryland student leaders, Dhruvak Mirani and Gannon Sprinkle, proposed establishing a tenants union for students who rent in College Park to lower the cost of rent, help improve living conditions, and address maintenance issues. As Mirani stated, 'We can’t really address housing prices in a vacuum without talking about all of the other issues that go along with it.' The median monthly rent in College Park between 2018 and 2022 was $1,838, according to the U.S. Census Bureau, and Sprinkle emphasized that 'We need something to rally behind, a force to fight for better living conditions and lower rent prices.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem and District 4 Councilmember
Adelia McGuire, Journalist
James Neilis, Berwyn Resident
Brian McAllister, College Park Resident
39395.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has proposed a housing subsidy of $1,500 for 150 low-income University of Maryland undergraduates to rent apartments in high-rise Route 1 buildings. Mayor Fazlul Kabir noted that many questions were raised during the public comment period, with some residents calling the program a 'theft of taxpayer revenue', as Berwyn resident James Neilis wrote, and others questioning why the city is offering the subsidy instead of the university. The proposal is expected to change considerably before the council votes on it, with Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell stating that another public meeting will be held before a vote is scheduled.

key_people: Stephanie Stullich, Author
Todd Larson, College Park Committee for a Better Environment Chair
Alexa Bely, College Park Committee for a Better Environment Member
Brenda Alexander, College Park City Arborist
organizations: College Park Committee for a Better Environment
39385.ai_summary: The College Park Committee for a Better Environment has been leading sustainability efforts in the city for over 50 years. The committee's initiatives include creating storm drain murals and signs to raise awareness about water pollution, as well as a tree identification program to label native trees along the Rhode Island Avenue Trolley Trail. According to Todd Larson, the committee's chair, the storm drain mural project “gives local artists a chance to show their work in a place where everyone can see it, while also providing an educational message to help residents understand why it’s important not to put anything down the storm drain except water.”

key_people: Emely Miranda-Aguilar, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
Brian McAllister, Berwyn Resident
Rakhmel Rafi, College Park Grants Coordinator
Emily Friend, Berwyn Resident and Berwyn District Civic Association Treasurer
Dan Blasberg, Berwyn Resident
Alec Lynde, Berwyn District Civic Association Member
39270.ai_summary: The City of College Park is exploring possible uses for a vacant lot in Berwyn, including designating it as a pocket park or creating a dog park, with Rakhmel Rafi, the city's grants coordinator, presenting options to residents. Some residents, like Emily Friend, expressed concerns about maintenance and suggested a rain garden as an alternative, while others, like Dan Blasberg, questioned the city's ability to handle storm water management projects. The city purchased the property with funds from the Maryland Department of Natural Resources Program Open Space and is considering options that include a permaculture garden, as Rafi said, 'a permaculture garden would preserve much of the existing landscape and augment it with native, pollinator-friendly plants, along with bird and squirrel feeders, to create a largely self-sustaining wildlife habitat.'

key_people: Jimmy Tarlau, Author
Stephanie Stullich, Author
Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember
Joseline Pena-Melnyk, Maryland Delegate
39177.ai_summary: New laws effective January 1, 2024, include a plastic bag ban in Prince George’s and Anne Arundel counties, as well as a statewide law restricting telephone solicitations and increasing the minimum wage to $15 per hour. According to Prince George’s County Councilmember Eric Olson, 'Plastic pollution is a real problem' and the ban will remove over 300 million plastic bags a year from the waste stream. Additionally, Maryland Delegate Joseline Pena-Melnyk stated that the higher minimum wage 'is still too low, but it’s a step in the right direction' and that 'people are barely getting by. Even $15 an hour is not enough; we should have a living wage.'

key_people: Heather Wright, Editor
Lilly, Doctor
Millard House II, Prince George’s County Public Schools Superintendent
NKenge Baker, Northwestern High School Principal
Ashanti Martinez, House of Delegates Member for District 22
Kareem, Hyattsville City Councilmember
39145.ai_summary: The article reviews the year 2023, highlighting tragedies such as the arrest of the City of College Park's then-mayor for possessing and distributing child pornography, and the sentencing to 30 years in prison. The article also covers trials and tribulations including pedestrian safety concerns around county schools, school bus driver shortages, and a cyberattack on Prince George’s County Public Schools. Additionally, the article mentions new developments, such as the opening of the new Hyattsville Middle School and the swearing-in of Ashanti Martinez as a member of the state’s House of Delegates.

key_people: Jacob T. Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Alexandra Alpert, Author
39012.ai_summary: Jacob T. Hernandez has joined the College Park City Council as a District 1 representative, becoming the city's only Hispanic American councilmember. Hernandez aims to enhance communication, improve education, and help residents navigate bureaucratic hurdles, stating 'It's only through our united organized voices that we are going to be able to facilitate the greater projects and programs that I have in store.' He hopes to strengthen relationships between the City of College Park and local public education leaders to improve conditions in the city's public schools.

key_people: Stephanie E. Stullich, Author
Jason Azevedo, Old Town Resident
Matt Tedesco, Attorney for Greenhill Capital Corporation
Adele Ellis, Patron of Pho Tom
Richard Biffl, Chair of the Old Town Historic District Local Advisory Committee
organizations: Old Town Historic District Local Advisory Committee
39027.ai_summary: A proposed redevelopment in downtown College Park, known as Terrapin House, would displace three local restaurants: Pho Tom, Northwest Chinese, and Ritchie’s Colombian Restaurant. The project has been the subject of multiple public meetings, where residents and officials expressed concerns about the impact on existing restaurants, historic character, and stormwater management. Adele Ellis, a patron of Pho Tom, stated, 'We’re losing too many local restaurants,' and hopes the developer will allow the existing restaurants to return to the new building with affordable rent.

key_people: Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Director
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Alexandra Alpert, Author
Kit Slack, Author
39023.ai_summary: The City of College Park is taking steps to help small businesses stay in the city after being displaced by new developments, including a $75,000 business retention fund in the fiscal 2024 budget. According to Michael Williams, College Park's economic development director, 'We wanted to retain some of the businesses who make up the fabric and the character of College Park.' Councilmember Stuart Adams said that around half of the funds raised by a three-cent tax increase on commercial property could reach local businesses, with the city council to make a determination on the allocation in a future meeting.

key_people: Maxine Gross, Graduate Student and Author
H.C. 'Curley' Byrd, University of Maryland President
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC)
39017.ai_summary: The article discusses the history of Lakeland, a neighborhood in College Park, and its transformation over the years. The author, Maxine Gross, shares her personal experience of witnessing the destruction of her community by deliberate government policies, including urban renewal in the 1960s that displaced many residents. The article highlights the importance of understanding this history to determine the prosperity of the entire city, including neighborhoods like Lakeland, Berwyn, and Hollywood.

key_people: Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park Councilmember
Kathy Bryant, Old Town resident
Emily Friend, Berwyn resident
Stephanie Stullich, Author
39032.ai_summary: The City of College Park hosted a Winter Wonderland event at city hall plaza on Dec. 2, featuring holiday music, vendors, photos with Santa, and a petting zoo. Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell and Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters lit up a 20-foot tall holiday tree made of white globes. Old Town resident Kathy Bryant commented that the new city hall was a wonderful venue for the event, saying 'We couldn’t have had something like this before,' and Berwyn resident Emily Friend said her 5-month-old son Miles had a good time, noting 'He was scared of Santa but loved the Grinch,' and added 'It’s cool that there was something fun for every group and every age.'

key_people: Kit Slack, Streetcar Suburbs Publishing Executive Director
39010.ai_summary: The College Park Here & Now is seeking donations to support local news, with a goal of raising $13,000 by the end of the year. As Kit Slack, Streetcar Suburbs Publishing Executive Director, states, 'we hope we can count on you to chip in' to help deliver news to every home in the city. The publication aims to continue covering local events, government, and developments, and possibly add Spanish content, but needs reader support to achieve its goals.

key_people: Emily Strab, College Park City Councilmember
Emily Strab, Office and Enrollment Administrator for St. Jerome Institute
38926.ai_summary: City Councilmember Emily Strab won the inaugural Maryland Catholic Woman of the Year award, presented by the Maryland Catholic Women’s Conference. According to the conference, “Emily Strab’s selection as the 2023 Maryland Catholic Woman of the Year exemplifies the values of faith, hope, and love in action.” Strab was elected to the city council in 2022 and serves as the office and enrollment administrator for St. Jerome Institute in D.C.

key_people: Jaclyn Bruner, Author
Sara Rían, Poet
38992.ai_summary: The author, Jaclyn Bruner, spoke at a College Park City Council meeting in January, urging the city to advocate for crossing guards for Hollywood Elementary School, after witnessing a near-miss accident at Edgewood Road and Rhode Island Avenue. As of December, there are still 83 open crossing guard positions in Prince George’s County, with some being covered by police officers, but without guaranteed coverage. The lack of crossing guards is attributed to staffing shortages, exacerbated by the pandemic, with the author questioning the reasons provided, quoting a poem by Sara Rían that says “what a privilege. / what a gift” to grow up and get old, highlighting the importance of protecting children’s safety.

key_people: Robert "RC" Carter, Head of the ElectionGuard program
Benjamin Hovland, College Park Board of Elections Supervisors and U.S. Election Assistance Commission Commissioner
Robert Stewart, Author
Josh Benaloh, Microsoft Senior Cryptographer
organizations: College Park Board of Elections Supervisors
38704.ai_summary: The City of College Park has implemented new verification technology in its elections, allowing voters to confirm their votes have been counted. According to Robert "RC" Carter, Head of the ElectionGuard program, "Think of it as like FedEx saying your package has arrived". This technology, developed by Microsoft, provides voters with a paper confirmation that can be used to verify their vote was counted, and also creates an encrypted public record of the vote that can be inspected and verified by interested parties.

key_people: Miss Floribunda, Columnist
Mowed and Manicured on Madison Street, Resident
key_issues: city ordinance on grass and weeds
38718.ai_summary: The College Park city ordinance on grass and weeds now permits alternative gardens and the use of native plants, which has sparked aesthetic reservations among some residents, such as Mowed and Manicured on Madison Street. Miss Floribunda, a columnist, shared her perspective on the matter, drawing from her experiences visiting her sister in France, where unmowed and native plant gardens are popular. As Miss Floribunda noted, 'being unmowed is now the mode in France, that most horticulturally elegant Eden.'

key_people: Jacob T. Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Kate Kennedy, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember

38626.ai_summary: Jacob T. Hernandez was elected to the College Park City Council for District 1, running on a platform focused on family and community involvement. Incumbent councilmembers Alan Hew, Stuart M. Adams, and John B. Rigg III were also reelected, with Adams advocating for issues such as a vacant-property tax and rental assistance, and Rigg focusing on affordable housing and aging in place. The election also saw voters weigh in on a nonbinding question about changing councilmember term lengths to staggered 4-year terms, with 710 voters in support and 567 opposed.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
