organizations: The U.S. Biomedical Advanced Research and Development Authority
Prince George’s County Economic Development Corporation
38536.ai_summary: College Park District 3 city council candidates and Mayor Fazlul Kabir discussed economic development, public safety, and property taxes at a forum sponsored by the Yarrow Civic Association. Kabir stated that he has worked closely with business owners and that a city business retention fund could help local owners stay in College Park, saying 'We have been working to take care of the businesses because they are our assets.' The candidates also addressed the displacement of 15 businesses at Campus Village Shops, which will be replaced with an apartment complex with retail space, and the need to support local businesses and ensure fair leases for minority and small businesses.

38531.ai_summary: The College Park Here & Now sponsored a candidate forum for District 1 on October 18, 2023, at City Hall. The forum covered various topics, including government teamwork, small business development, construction, connectivity, and development, as well as climate change and biodiversity. Voting for the College Park election will be on November 5 at the College Park Community Center.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
38455.ai_summary: The City of College Park is holding an election on November 5, with candidate forums scheduled for October 18 and 19. Fazlul Kabir, the current College Park Mayor, is running for re-election and plans to continue leading the city by uniting the community, enhancing transparency, and fostering trust. He aims to improve the quality of life with strong public safety measures, enhanced code enforcement, smart economic development, and support for new small businesses.

key_people: William Whichard, Homeowner in Calvert Hills neighborhood
Daniel Cunningham, Executive Director of the Community Preservation Trust
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Susan Hartman, Executive Director of the College Park City-University Partnership
organizations: Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development
38473.ai_summary: The Community Preservation Trust, an offshoot of the College Park City-University Partnership, celebrated its first purchase and sale of a home in the city with a ribbon cutting ceremony. William Whichard, the new homeowner, said 'Gratitude characterizes what I feel today' and emphasized the importance of strengthened community in creating a stronger city. The program, made possible through $15 million in grants, aims to increase homeownership in the city by making affordable homes available, with Mayor Fazlul Kabir stating it will 'give our residents an opportunity to own a home, build generation wealth and help stabilize the neighborhood'.

key_people: Emily Clipton, Low Impact Design Center
Lesley Riddle, Hyattsville Director of Public Works
Sophie Oriani, Author
38376.ai_summary: The College Park city council is moving forward with plans to renovate King Park, which has been closed for over a year. Emily Clipton from the Low Impact Design Center presented the final design, which took into consideration feedback from residents and returns the playground to its original location. Lesley Riddle, Hyattsville Director of Public Works, noted that the park's reopening is expected in late Spring 2024, saying 'We're in good shape, [but] it took a long time!'

key_people: Kayla Carter, College Park Racial Equity Officer
Hannah Stein, Author
Miriam Bader, Director of Planning and Community Development
38373.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously approved the street name Ancestor’s Lane, which was chosen by the Restorative Justice Commission to honor the local Black community. According to Kayla Carter, College Park Racial Equity Officer, 'The Lakeland community, in particular, was historically Black,' and the commission wanted a name that the entire community could connect with. The commission aims to create committees to work on truth and reconciliation, engagement, and restorative and transformative justice, and hopes to help the Black community of Lakeland redeem a sense of history and shared identity, as Carter said, 'It’s a very large project, and things are still new, but we’re doing the best we can.'

38371.ai_summary: The College Park election will take place on November 5. The article provides resources for voters, including a Voters Guide and links to candidate forums for District 1 and District 3. The article encourages readers to check back for the latest updates on the election.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Aubrey Batten, College Park Resident
Kate Kennedy, College Park City Councilmember
Lydia Hurley, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
38290.ai_summary: Residents and College Park's elected officials are preparing for the November 5 citywide election, which includes a non-binding advisory question about switching from two-year to four-year terms for elected officials. Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that 'If the people vote yes, the city won’t actually change things until the council votes on it. The ballot question’s purpose is to gather information.' Resident Aubrey Batten expressed her opposition, stating 'I understand why this proposal is advantageous to candidates and incumbents. Less campaigning is cheaper and easier... This is not a compelling argument.' The city is polling residents again after a similar question was asked in 2019, which did not support a change.

key_people: Jeremy Rose, FairVote Worker
Alexa Bely, University of Maryland Teacher/Researcher
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
38056.ai_summary: The current voting system in College Park has a flaw, as it ignores voter preferences among candidates if the top candidate does not receive more than 50% of the votes. Jeremy Rose and Alexa Bely suggest adopting ranked choice voting, which considers a voter's top preference and their ranked choices across all candidates, to provide a better approach. They believe this system can increase overall voter satisfaction, decrease polarization among candidates, and draw a larger and more diverse pool of candidates, and are promoting its adoption in College Park for the 2025 city election.

key_people: Lydia Hurley, Journalist
Matthew Kirschebaum, University of Maryland English Professor
Elizabeth Maring, College Park Resident
Christina Grant, College Park Resident
38050.ai_summary: Two new franchise restaurants, The Great Greek Mediterranean Grill and The Little Blue Menu, are coming to College Park, with The Little Blue Menu being a digital-based restaurant and a ghost kitchen. The announcement has sparked community debate, with some residents expressing concerns about Chick-fil-A's association with anti-LGBTQ groups, as stated by Matthew Kirschebaum, an English professor at the University of Maryland, who said 'There is absolutely no dissembling whatsoever. [Chick-fil-A] simply has no interest in the community and isn’t going to even try to pretend otherwise.' The Little Blue Menu is poised to offer competitive-wage employment and give back to the community, with a $25,000 donation to Feeding America on behalf of the new restaurant.

key_people: John Kish, College Park Election Board Chairman
Martin Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember
Brencis Smith, College Park City Councilmember
Keith Sydnor, College Park City Councilmember
Joe Murchison, Journalist
38039.ai_summary: A temporary ban on campaigning by candidates for city office in College Park has caused confusion among three announced mayoral candidates, with one of them, Keith Sydnor, being hit with a $1,000 fine. The candidates, including Martin Mitchell and Brencis Smith, had already begun distributing signs or campaign literature when the letter from John Kish, chairman of the election board, came out, stating that 'no official candidate signs or materials shall be disseminated to the public' until they are officially certified. As Sydnor noted, 'I suspended my campaign, I don’t agree with it, but I’m abiding by it,' while Mitchell contested the rule, stating 'I see no violation of City election law by campaigning for an office prior to being certified by the Board'.

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Marcus Afzali, former College Park City Councilmember
Joel Carlson, friend of Alan Hew
organizations: College Park Committee for a Better Environment
37990.ai_summary: Alan Hew, a long-time resident of College Park, was sworn in as the new District 1 council member. Hew has a strong background in environmental conservation and community service, having served on the College Park Committee for a Better Environment and chaired the Bee City USA Committee. As Hew recalled, former councilmember Marcus Afzali encouraged him to run for the city council, saying 'No, but your heart's there. You understand how the city works, through working through that development and working on your committees. That's what it takes to serve, and you've done that.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem and District 4 Councilmember
Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Manager
Alan Hew, College Park District 1 Councilmember
Stuart Adams, College Park District 3 Councilmember

37973.ai_summary: The City of College Park held its first in-person town hall meeting, where residents discussed the city's budget for fiscal year 2024 with the mayor and city council. Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell explained that the city offers tax breaks to developers, stating 'Yes we did phase a tax credit over five years for developers to come build in the city.' The city's economic development manager, Michael Williams, noted that businesses coming to the city help maintain the low tax rate, and that 'tax credits allow them to be able to operate 12 months of the year, even when school isn’t in session and students go home.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Denise Mitchell, College Park Councilmember (District 4)
Stuart Adams, College Park Councilmember (District 3)
Lydia Hurley, Journalist
Teresa Way-Pezzuti, College Park Director of Human Resources
Bill Gardiner, College Park Assistant City Manager
37732.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted unanimously to raise annual compensation for the mayor and councilmembers, with the mayor's salary increasing from $10,500 to $15,000 and councilmembers' compensation increasing from $7,000 to $10,000. As Mayor Fazlul Kabir wrote in a May 13 blog post, 'Someone seeks office mainly to perform a civic duty and to support their community. However, financial compensation was always a lower-ranking criterion for running for office.' Councilmember Denise Mitchell noted that compensation could be a factor for potential councilmembers, saying 'They're considering things like, 'If I'm giving up my time, how much will I be compensated for that?''.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Julia Kyles, Author
organizations: Bangladesh University of Engineering and Technology
37724.ai_summary: Fazlul Kabir, the new College Park Mayor, has been actively engaging with the community since his inauguration, attending events such as Bike to Work Day. As he stated, 'I'm a very hyper-local person. The thing I enjoy most is talking to a neighbor on his or her porch and talking about some problems or some issues and see[ing] how to solve them or get things done.' Kabir's experience with the North College Park Civic Association and his previous role as a city councilmember have prepared him for his new position, where he aims to rebuild community trust in city leaders.

key_people: Erik Valentine, Community Development Planner for the City of College Park
Kathy Bryant, Author
37734.ai_summary: Biohabitats Inc. has completed an initial survey of the Sentinel Swamp Sanctuary, located behind Columbia Avenue in Old Town College Park, as part of an ecological restoration project. According to Erik Valentine, Community Development Planner for the City of College Park, 'We're still in the very early stages of this project,' and 'there is a lot of potential there.' The project, which has been allocated $200,000 in funding, aims to restore the sanctuary and make it more inviting, with plans for a raised boardwalk, viewing platform, and interpretive signage.

key_people: Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
Violetta Sharps Jones, Vice President of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project
Julie Schablitsky, Chief of Cultural Resources with the Maryland Department of Transportation
Ed Williams, Lakeland Community Member
Rev. Stephen Wright, Spiritual Leader
Rev. Latishia Cokley, Pastor of Embry African Methodist Episcopal Church
Maxine Gross, Author
37619.ai_summary: The Lakeland community gathered to unveil a historic marker, recognizing their legacy and contributions to College Park. As Violetta Sharps Jones stated, 'Lakelanders have been fighting for our recognition, for acknowledgement of our existence, our purpose, our contributions. Hopefully this is just the beginning.' The event, led by Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem, highlighted the importance of legacy and cultural heritage in the community, with Ed Williams noting, 'It's been a long, a long time coming, but I know a change gon' come.'

key_people: Sydnee Singletary, Journalist
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Kennedy Walker, Greenbelt Resident
organizations: College Park Tree and Landscaping Board
37521.ai_summary: The City of College Park has begun a sidewalk installation project on Edmonston Road, which involves cutting down trees to improve mobility and safety for pedestrians and cyclists. According to College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, 'The Complete Streets project is part of our broader initiatives that the city has been involved in. But it’s been about enhancing connectivity and safety for pedestrians and cyclists in our city.' However, the project has created controversy among residents, with some voicing concerns about the removal of trees, as noted by Greenbelt resident Kennedy Walker, who said 'the money used to construct the sidewalk could definitely go towards other aspects of the community like more flowers and less trash in the area.'

key_people: Maria James, Former Managing Editor of The Hyattsville Life & Times
37504.ai_summary: The College Park Here & Now hosted an election forum featuring candidates running in the May 2023 Mayoral Special Election. Maria James, former managing editor of The Hyattsville Life & Times, posed a series of questions to the four candidates, including questions about development, traffic, and affordable housing. The forum was held on April 15 and can be replayed online, with election day scheduled for May 6th.

key_people: Terry Schum, College Park Department of Planning & Community Development Director
Miriam Bader, College Park Department of Planning and Development Director
Dhiru Thadani, Artist and Terry Schum's Husband
Kathy Bryant, Author
organizations: College Park Department of Planning & Community Development
37434.ai_summary: Terry Schum is retiring after 37 years of service as the director of the College Park Department of Planning & Community Development, with Miriam Bader taking over as director. Schum plans to pursue her lifelong interest in drawing and painting, and says 'How am I going to disengage from these things? There's always another grant to explore and project to do.' She will not be in the city to see several projects finalized, including the renovation of Duvall Field and the Route 1 revitalization effort.

key_people: Erik Valentine, Community Development Planner
Jan Carlo Marin, Author
37417.ai_summary: The City of College Park purchased a small parcel of undeveloped land in the Berwyn neighborhood through Program Open Space, a state-funded initiative. Community Development Planner Erik Valentine hopes to use the parcel as open space where people can gather. The city is seeking community input about how to move forward with the Berwyn property, and Valentine indicated that the city council will consider plans for the parcel at upcoming meetings.

key_people: Bryan Haddad, College Park Mayoral Candidate
Maria James, Former Managing Editor with Streetcar Suburbs Publishing
37426.ai_summary: The City of College Park is holding a special mayoral election on May 6, with candidate Bryan Haddad running on a single-issue platform to increase penalties against illegally modified vehicles. Haddad, a small business owner, believes that city code enforcement can be strengthened to more effectively write tickets against these vehicles by citing existing MD laws. A candidate forum will be held on April 15, moderated by Maria James, where residents can submit questions and hear from the candidates, including Haddad, who says 'A vote for me is a vote for a more peaceful community.'

key_people: Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayor
Alice Carlson, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
organizations: National League of Cities University Communities Council
The National Center for Missing and Exploited Children
37262.ai_summary: College Park Mayor Patrick Wojahn resigned after being arrested on charges related to the possession and distribution of child pornography. The National Center for Missing and Exploited Children notified the Prince George’s County Police Department about a social media account distributing child pornography, which was identified as belonging to Wojahn. “While this investigation does not involve any official city business of any kind, it is in the best interests of our community that I step aside and not serve as a distraction,” Wojahn wrote, and Mitchell will serve as pro tem mayor until a special election is held.

key_people: Jenna Bloom, Journalist
Robert Thurston, Lakeland Civic Association President
Todd Alexander, Renewal Housing Associates Representative
Terry Schum, College Park Department of Planning & Community Development Director
organizations: College Park Department of Planning & Community Development
37038.ai_summary: The property on the corner of Route 1 and Melbourne Place in Lakeland was purchased by LV Collective for $36 million, with plans to build a student housing complex with retail on the ground floor. Robert Thurston, Lakeland Civic Association President, emphasized the need for community involvement and multigenerational housing, stating 'It's critical that Lakelanders and the Lakeland diaspora are involved in this redevelopment.' Terry Schum, College Park Department of Planning & Community Development Director, noted that collaboration between property owners and the community is ideal, saying 'And they should listen to the community, of course, all of that is part of the development review process.'

key_people: Dan Behrend, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
Martha Ainsworth, Prince George’s Sierra Club

organizations: College Park Department of Public Works
College Park Committee for a Better Environment
37031.ai_summary: The College Park City Council discussed a proposed ordinance to ban plastic carryout bags and require stores to charge customers a minimum $0.10 fee for paper or new reusable bags. The ordinance aims to promote the city’s sustainability goals by reducing shoppers’ reliance on single-use bags. Councilmember John Rigg expressed support for the bill and requested that the city’s implementation include distribution of reusable bags in targeted locations to ensure support for people who may struggle to afford bag fees.

key_people: Rebecca Bailey, College Park Animal Control Officer
Bob Ryan, College Park Department of Public Services Director
Stephanie Stullich, Author
Aron Bender, College Park Resident
Bryan Draper, College Park Resident
organizations: College Park Department of Public Services
37024.ai_summary: Rebecca Bailey, College Park Animal Control Officer, helps animals in need, saying 'They don’t have a way to advocate for themselves.' Bailey's work involves rescuing stray pets, responding to complaints, and educating the public about animal care. As noted by Bob Ryan, College Park Department of Public Services Director, having an animal control officer who knows the city and can develop relationships with residents is a significant advantage for College Park.

key_people: Anna Latta Owens, College Park Mayor
Dermott Michael Owens
William E. "Brit" Kirwan, University of Maryland President
Kerry Owens
Steve Owens
Mary C. Cook, Author
36789.ai_summary: Anna Latta Owens, College Park's first and only female mayor, was a dedicated public servant who fought against a proposed garbage incinerator and established strong ties with the University of Maryland. She was also a strong advocate for senior and disability housing, as evident from her work with Attick Towers, and published a book about its history. Owens' legacy continues to inspire City Councilmembers, especially women, who sought her insight and guidance on various policy issues.

key_people: Lily Fountain, Author
36796.ai_summary: The City of College Park has introduced a new composting program, making it easier for residents to recycle food waste through inexpensive compost containers and free weekly collections. According to the author, 'composting is one of the easiest and most productive things individuals can do for the environment' and can help combat climate change. The program allows residents to compost a wide range of food scraps, including meat and bones, and paper products, and provides an alternative to sending waste to landfills where it produces methane, a greenhouse gas.

key_people: Will Beltran, Author
Mary King, College Park Resident
Tanya Lervik, College Park Resident
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Denise Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember
Kate Kennedy, College Park City Councilmember
36528.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously approved a new district map, the October Plan 1 map, which keeps the core neighborhoods of the current districts intact. Councilmembers supported this map, with some expressing concerns about the distribution of voters and civic associations, as Councilmember Susan Whitney noted that the alternative Plan 3A map would have 'concentrated' civic associations in District 2, showing 'an imbalance.' Councilmember Maria Mackie explained that losing neighborhoods to another district is 'about all of College Park,' while Councilmember Kate Kennedy expressed concern that District 1 would have a disproportionately high number of active voters, saying 'We're doing a disservice to our [District 1] residents to have so many active voters'.

key_people: Tyrone Wells, Prince George’s County Office of Emergency Management Operations Manager
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember District 3
Denise Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember District 4
Fazlul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember District 1
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember District 4

Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember District 3
organizations: Prince George’s County Office of Emergency Management
36357.ai_summary: The College Park City Council discussed emergency management with Tyrone Wells, the operations manager of the Prince George’s County Office of Emergency Management. Wells described the county's efforts, saying 'Our mission always has to do with life safety, stabilizing situations and property preservation.' Councilmembers raised concerns about the county's communication with residents, with Councilmember John Rigg saying 'Citizens in my community are wondering why they don’t hear from the county more often' and Councilmember Maria Mackie stating that 'a lot of our senior citizens don’t even own a phone,' highlighting the need for extra support during emergencies.

key_people: Gale Mamatova, Vice President of the North College Park Community Association
36359.ai_summary: The city of College Park is undergoing a heated discussion on redistricting, with residents passionately arguing for their own interests. According to Gale Mamatova, the city needs to revisit its approach to bring residents along through access to information and effective communication, and invest in a community engagement tool to facilitate this process. As Mamatova notes, 'Residents understand that redistricting is important to our democratic process. Participation and providing input is vital. We did our part, now it’s up to city leaders to step up and lead us through this change effectively.'

key_people: Kathy Bryant, Old Town Civic Association President
Fazlul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember
Jordan Dewar, Redistricting Commission Vice-Chair
Robert Day, Redistricting Commission Chair
Will Beltran, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
36067.ai_summary: The College Park City Council's redistricting debate continued with residents and councilmembers questioning the Redistricting Commission about their proposals, which would separate core neighborhoods in District 3. Kathy Bryant, Old Town Civic Association President, expressed concern that the commission is disregarding long-term neighborhood connections, saying '[The commission] kept saying ‘we follow the numbers,’ but it’s disregarding long term neighborhood connections that I feel are very important.' Councilmember Fazlul Kabir also emphasized the importance of keeping District 1 neighborhoods together, stating 'People don’t really differentiate between the boundaries of these neighborhoods; they all think as one cohesive community.'

key_people: Denise Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember
Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayor
Auzinea Bacon, Author
Shawn Sharifi, Band leader
36180.ai_summary: College Park Day, the city's signature celebration, is returning on Oct. 15 after a 2-year hiatus. The event will feature about 150 organizations, including food vendors, live entertainment, and more, with around 8,000 residents expected to attend. As band leader Shawn Sharifi stated, 'We can’t wait to bring our boogy to College Park Day,' highlighting the event's goal of providing something for everyone, including entertainment and community engagement opportunities.

key_people: Emily Strab, College Park City Councilmember
Lisbeth M Melendez Rivera, College Park City Council Candidate
Kelly Burello, College Park City Council Candidate
Robert Croslin, College Park Mayor
Kevin Ward, Former College Park Mayor
35987.ai_summary: Emily Strab has won the Ward 2 city council election with 280 votes, edging out Lisbeth M Melendez Rivera and Kelly Burello. The seat was vacant after Robert Croslin was elected mayor in a June special mayoral election following former mayor Kevin Ward's death in January. Strab will take the oath of office in King Park on October 20.

key_people: Lee Sommer, College Park Airport Manager
David Dorsch, College Park Airport Authority Chair
Auzinea Bacon, Author
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission
35792.ai_summary: The College Park City Council discussed a new commuter program run by Tailwind Air, a veteran-owned company, which would launch out of the College Park Airport. The program, which would have four daily flights between College Park and New York City’s Skyport Marina, has raised concerns about increased noise from new air traffic, with David Dorsch noting that 'the noise is generally the helicopters and the ones that are the worst are the military, because they’re the biggest and heaviest' and that the planes in Tailwind’s fleet are quieter than those aircraft. The airport authority has asked the city council for approval of proposed changes to the airport’s operating regulations to allow the program to move forward.

key_people: Robert Croslin, College Park Mayor
Ben Simasek, College Park City Councilmember
Rommel Sandino, College Park City Councilmember
Christopher Hatcher, Attorney for Perseus TDC
35965.ai_summary: A new apartment building, called Residences at the Six, is under construction at 6400 America Boulevard in the Prince George’s Plaza Transit District, with 316 units, including 75 studio, 173 one-bedroom, and 68 two-bedroom apartments. According to Perseus TDC, three-quarters of the apartments will have rents under $2,500, with the developer marketing the project as affordable, though market-rate. As Councilmember Ben Simasek noted, the new building 'is a big opportunity to activate University Town Center, to improve the walkability of that area, and generally making it more attractive.'

key_people: Jon Meltzer, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
Bill Gardiner, College Park Assistant City Manager
Victoria MacDonald, Berwyn Resident
Bob Kuligowski, Meteorologist
Kathy Bryant, College Park Homeowner
organizations: City of College Park Department of Public Works
35659.ai_summary: A devastating bow echo storm hit College Park on July 12, causing widespread damage and power outages, with estimated costs to the city around $750,000. According to Bill Gardiner, College Park Assistant City Manager, 'The department of public works estimates that several hundred trees came down during the storm,' and it took around 10 days to remove them from public rights of way. Victoria MacDonald, a Berwyn resident, said she wished the University of Maryland had used its warning siren to caution residents, saying 'Maybe that has to be looked at, in my opinion, what are the criteria?'

key_people: Calvin Hawkins, Prince George’s County Councilmember
Mel Franklin, Prince George’s County Councilmember
Sophie Gorman Oriani, Journalist
Kit Slack, Journalist
Jennifer Kubit, Hyattsville Resident
35657.ai_summary: The primary election in Prince George's County saw nearly 214,000 voters turn out for the at-large race, with incumbents Calvin Hawkins and Mel Franklin winning. Some Hyattsville residents reported issues with the voting process, including being offered a choice between Republican and Democratic ballots, despite Maryland's closed primary law. As Jennifer Kubit noted, her spouse accidentally selected the wrong ballot, but was able to void it and submit a correct one, highlighting the need for improvements in the voting process, which was discussed by College Park City Councilmembers during a meeting on August 3.

key_people: Jon Meltzer, Journalist
James Crane, Communications and Public Safety Expert with the Columbia Telecommunications Corporation

key_issues: Health risks associated with radio-frequency radiation
35636.ai_summary: The College Park City Council held a meeting on June 28 to discuss the planned installation of 5G antennas around the city, with presentations from James Crane and Shawn Thompson of the Columbia Telecommunications Corporation. According to Suellen Ferguson, attorney for the city, a 2018 FCC rule requires that the city approve eligible 5G Small Wireless Facilities (SWFs) installation requests within 60 days, and the city can only discuss the exact location of proposed arrays, not whether arrays can be installed at all. Residents expressed concerns about health risks to those living and traveling in close proximity to the proposed SWFs, citing a 2020 article in the peer-reviewed medical journal Oncology Letters that noted the evaluation of radio-frequency radiation health risks from 5G technology had not been properly reviewed by unbiased researchers.

key_people: Robert Croslin, Hyattsville Mayor
Kelly Burrello, College Park City Council Candidate
Lisbeth M. Melendez Rivera, College Park City Council Candidate
Emily Strab, College Park City Council Candidate
35721.ai_summary: A special election for the open Ward 2 city council seat in College Park will be held on October 5, with candidates Kelly Burrello, Lisbeth M. Melendez Rivera, and Emily Strab vying for the seat vacated by Robert Croslin, who won the June 7 special mayoral election in Hyattsville. A staffing issue led to a polling place opening an hour and a half late in College Park. The elections are part of a series of voting opportunities in the area, including the November 8 general election, where residents will vote on various issues, including the legalization of recreational marijuana.

35522.ai_summary: Preliminary election results are in for the July 19 primary election in Prince George's County, with many races essentially decided in the Democratic primaries. Wanika Fisher is primed to edge out Victor Ramirez for the Democratic nomination for county councilmember in District 2, while Eric Olson has secured his nomination for county councilmember in District 3. The results are current as of July 20th, but could still change as not all precincts have finished reporting and mail-in and provisional ballots have not yet been counted.

key_people: James Rosapepe, State Senator and Vice Chair of the State Senate’s Budget and Taxation Committee
Sam Draddy, Author
35361.ai_summary: The City of College Park is receiving over $22 million in funding from the State of Maryland for various projects, including $9.7 million for the Route 1 rebuilding project and $5 million for graduate student housing at the University of Maryland. State Senator James Rosapepe notes that the Route 1 project will 'increase safety and create a visually pleasing thoroughfare', which he calls 'the Champs-Élysées of College Park'. The funding will also support the creation of a Community Preservation Trust to preserve affordable single-family homes and a new playground for the Al-Huda School.

35345.ai_summary: The primary election for Prince George's County is scheduled for July 19, with early voting from July 7 to July 14. Candidates such as Eric Olson, Sia Finoh, and Eve Shuman are running for the District 3 county council seat, while Tom Dernoga is running unopposed in District 1. The election will also feature contests for county executive, sheriff, and state legislature seats, with many races likely to be decided in the Democratic primary.

key_people: Claire Worshtil, Prince George’s County Department of Parks and Recreation Lead Strategic Park Planner

35359.ai_summary: The Prince George’s County Department of Parks and Recreation is planning a new community center in North College Park, with a feasibility study expected to conclude by the end of 2022. As NCPCA President Alaina Pitt said, 'I think the community center can serve as a catalyst for bringing the community together and for more interaction between neighbors.' The project has secured $1 million in support from the Maryland state budget, courtesy of State Delegate Ben Barnes, and the City of College Park has also pledged financial support.

34640.ai_summary: The state primary is set for July 19, with early voting from July 7 to July 14, and many local races in College Park will be decided in the Democratic primaries. Candidates such as Wanika Fisher, Victor Ramirez, and Eric Olson are running for various county council seats, while Angela Alsobrooks is running for re-election as Prince George's County Executive. The outcome of these elections will impact College Park governance, particularly in regards to redistricting and county council representation.

35179.ai_summary: The College Park City Council passed a law to protect the city's big, older trees by requiring residents to obtain a permit to cut down trees over 36 inches in circumference and to prune more than 20% of the live branches on large trees. Residents who cut down trees will be required to replace them and may be reimbursed up to $300 for each tree they plant. The law has been met with controversy, with some residents arguing that homeowners should be able to make decisions about trees on their own property without city oversight, while others believe that tree canopy is a public good that the city should regulate to preserve, as noted by resident Robert O'Brien who said that 'trees are a community resource.'

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Jeff DeHan, Prince George's County Department of the Environment Associate Director of Stormwater Management

Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission
35203.ai_summary: The College Park city council is addressing the issue of flooding in the Calvert Hills neighborhood, which has been exacerbated by aging and inadequate stormwater and sewer infrastructure. As Councilmember Stuart Adams noted, 'Unfortunately, with global warming, what were considered 5-year storms when these neighborhoods were built can now happen once or twice a year, and we may see 100-year floods every decade.' The Prince George's County Stormwater Management Division has proposed a $15.5 million project to improve the stormwater management system, which has been delayed and is over a year behind schedule.

key_people: Hannah Marszalek, Journalist
Mike Hunninghake, Sustainable Maryland Program Manager
organizations: University of Maryland’s Environmental Finance Center
35210.ai_summary: Sustainable Maryland, a free certification program, helps Maryland municipalities become more eco-friendly by providing support and access to resources. According to Mike Hunninghake, Sustainable Maryland’s program manager, 'The program operates on the premise that by engaging local governments in sustainability we build a strong foundation for the work that needs to be done with respect to critical issues like climate change, environmental justice, and strong local economies.' College Park has established its own Green Team and has been certified by Sustainable Maryland, and is working to address environmental issues such as stormwater management and waste reduction.

key_people: Katharine Wilson, Author
Carlo Colella, University of Maryland Vice President and Chief Administrative Officer
David Mitchell, University of Maryland Police Department Chief
Llatera Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
35042.ai_summary: The College Park city council has approved funding for live-video monitoring capacity in the fiscal 2023 budget, which will reinstate a program that was previously cut. According to University of Maryland Vice President and Chief Administrative Officer Carlo Colella, 'The university leadership respectfully ask the city to reinstate the monitor and the live monitoring of the city’s closed circuit TV cameras.' University of Maryland Police Department Chief David Mitchell noted that live-monitoring, in addition to police patrols and other safety precautions, can assist with a more proactive response to criminal and safety issues that arise, and Councilmember Llatera Brown Esters agreed that 'Live-monitoring, in addition to police patrols and other safety precautions, can assist with a more proactive response to criminal and safety issues that arise.'

key_people: Robert Marsili, College Park Director of Public Works
Kathy Bryant, Author
Bill Lescure, Calvert Mansion Descendant
Thomas Taltavull, TJT Architects Principal
organizations: Maryland National-Capital Park and Planning Commission
35026.ai_summary: The City of College Park is restoring the historic Old Parish House, a 200-year-old building in the Old Town neighborhood, with the goal of making it a 'showpiece for the public', as stated by Robert Marsili, the city's Director of Public Works. The restoration, which has been ongoing for several years, has involved replacing the roof and ceilings, repairing and replacing windows, and making exterior masonry repairs, with a focus on historical accuracy. The project has been supported by a preservation grant of $38,500 from the Maryland National-Capital Park and Planning Commission, and Marsili noted that 'We worked hard to do this in a historically accurate way'.

key_people: Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayor
Petrella Robinson, North Brentwood Mayor
Isabella Alcañiz, University of Maryland Associate Professor and Director of the Latin American and Caribbean Studies Center
Katharine Wilson, Author
35037.ai_summary: The College Park City Council established the Restorative Justice Commission to address past wrongs, including the city's urban renewal project in the 1970s that destroyed homes in the historic Black community of Lakeland. Mayor Patrick Wojahn said the initiative aims to 'make the community whole for what occurred in the past and putting them as close as possible into the place or even better than they were before the wrong occurred.' Commission member Petrella Robinson, who grew up in another affected community, stated 'I think they need restitution because they, they put them out of their homes, they came in and did what they wanted to do. It was a great, and is a great community.'

key_people: Hayden Renaghan, University of Maryland Student Government Association Deputy Liaison to the College Park City Council
Kislay Parahsar, UMD Student Body President

organizations: University of Maryland Student Government Association

35015.ai_summary: Hayden Renaghan, the University of Maryland Student Government Association's deputy liaison to the College Park City Council, is actively involved in the Clean City Project, a collaborative initiative to clean up the city's streets. As Renaghan explained, 'Students want it known that it's their town, too, not just a pit-stop for them.' She emphasizes the importance of ongoing engagement and encourages leaders to 'show people why they should care' and 'prove there are tangible benefits to respecting their community, to contributing, to leaving it a better place than they found it.'

key_people: Katharine Wilson, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
Mary Cook, North College Park Citizens Association President
Fazul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember (District 1)
