College Park Community Center-Youth Sports Complex
locations: College Park Community Center-Youth Sports Complex
34892.ai_summary: The city of College Park has started a feasibility study for a new community center in the North College Park neighborhood, with the goal of creating a local gathering space for residents. The city will likely pay for a portion of the new facility, while the Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC) would foot most of the bill, and Councilmember Fazul Kabir noted that 'a partnership will help expedite the construction' and give the city a stake in the services provided. The study, led by WXY Studio, will identify residents' priorities and determine the type of facility that best addresses those needs, with Claire Worshtil stating 'we want to make sure that we are complementing services, not duplicating them'.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember
Katharine Wilson, Author
34748.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is encouraging homeownership through new programs, aiming to achieve a better balance of rental and owner-occupied homes in the city. Councilmember Fazlul Kabir noted, 'It’s not against the renters. It’s just the added benefit of stabilizing the neighborhood,' and another person stated, 'Homeowners simply have more skin in the game than renters, who’d have the option to move, were the city to decline.' The council is adjusting the existing New Neighbor Homeowner Grant Program to offer $5,000 to potential home buyers who agree to stay in their home for at least five years.

key_people: A.R. Cabral, Author
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
Fazlul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember (District 1)
Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayor
Kate Kennedy, College Park City Councilmember (District 1)
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember (District 2)
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember (District 2)
D.W. Rowlands, Tenant at 4704 Calvert Rd.
Kenneth Young, College Park City Manager
34737.ai_summary: The College Park City Council approved a contract of sale to purchase a property in the Old Town neighborhood for $1.7 million. Councilmember Stuart Adams said 'The potential purchase of 4704 Calvert Road makes sense... It aligns with our strategic goals. It’s an incredible opportunity that is being brought to us by owners who live in, and love, College Park.' The purchase aims to stabilize affordable rental housing and explore community-focused development, with Mayor Patrick Wojahn stating 'Sometimes opportunities present themselves, and we have to jump on them.'

key_people: Bibi Mohamed, Director of Helpers to Good at Ansaar-ul-Birr Community Services Inc.
Azad Mohamed, Volunteer at Helpers to Good
34755.ai_summary: Helpers to Good, a program under Ansaar-ul-Birr Community Services Inc., hosted a Kids Fun Festival on February 19 and 20, providing free lunches and school supplies to kids. The event was supported by the City of College Park and a national relief organization, with almost 400 meals and 200 gifts given out over two days. As Azad Mohamed noted, the event's success, with a line of cars waiting, underscored the importance of community connections, and Helpers to Good plans to continue supporting the local community with monthly activities.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
Pamela Boozer-Strother, Prince George's County School Board Member (District 3)
Josie Jack, Streetcar Suburbs News Reporter
Kit Slack, Streetcar Suburbs News Reporter
Sarah Christopherson, Hyattsville Middle School PTSO President
34718.ai_summary: Hyattsville Middle School students are experiencing long days due to delayed bus transportation as they attend school at three different locations while their new building is being constructed. According to parent and College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, 'My own child’s bus late arrival means that he misses math pretty much daily.' School officials and Prince George's County School Board Member Pamela Boozer-Strother are working to address the issue, which may be contributing to a decreased attendance rate and declining enrollment at the school. The new school building is expected to be completed by July 2023.

key_people: Denise Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Kate Kennedy, College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Taneen Momeni, Author
34743.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmembers have been adapting to new meeting spaces and formats over the past two years, including a move from the old city hall building to Davis Hall and then to virtual meetings due to the pandemic. Councilmembers Denise Mitchell and Maria Mackie noted that Davis Hall was cramped, but also allowed for a more informal atmosphere, with Mackie saying 'Davis Hall was very close, and your neighbor was right next to you. You could just whisper a question or something like that.' Councilmembers also appreciated the benefits of virtual meetings, including improved work-life balance, with Kate Kennedy saying 'It's a lot easier to have work-life balance when you're at home. It's a lot easier to have dinner with my family [and] then go to the [virtual] meeting.'

key_people: Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Manager
Sophie Gorman Oriani, Author
organizations: Prince George’s County Economic Development Corporation
34775.ai_summary: The College Park City Council discussed a proposal to renew the city’s participation in the Greater College Park Regional Institution Strategic Enterprise (RISE) Zone. According to College Park Economic Development Manager Michael Williams, “What we think is that start up companies and new companies are generally trying to get going and not trying to buy or purchase a building,” and the state has expanded the RISE zone program to provide rental assistance to businesses that don’t own their own buildings. The city will hold a public hearing to review participation in the RISE zone on March 8.

key_people: Sophie Gorman Oriani, Author
Darryll Pines, University of Maryland President
Shubha Punase, M-NCPPC Project Manager
Mary Lehman, Delegate of District 21
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember District 3
Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayor
Alexander Rohlf, 5th Grader
Julie Bringman, Pastor of Hope Lutheran Church
Arthur Horne, Shipley and Horne
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC)
34586.ai_summary: The Adelphi Road Sector Plan, intended to guide development around the Adelphi Road station for the next 25 years, has faced opposition from residents and some officials, including Delegate Mary Lehman and College Park City Councilmember Stuart Adams. Adams urged the county council not to move forward with the plan, stating 'We have enough time to really thoughtfully consider how to add appropriate density, how to make sure we’re looking at our school burdens, how we can preserve our green space and make this absolutely fantastic.' The plan's potential impact on the environmentally sensitive Guilford Woods area has been a major point of concern, with many speaking out against upzoning the woods, including a 5th grader who said he learned about snakes there during the pandemic.

key_people: Katharine Wilson, Journalist
Darryll Pines, University of Maryland President
Bob Baer, College Park Resident
Steve Halpern, College Park City Engineer
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
34607.ai_summary: The City of College Park is addressing the issue of flooding, particularly in the Calvert Hills neighborhood, which has been severely affected by extreme flooding in recent years. As part of the Stormwater Assessment Project, the city plans to conduct a topographical survey to examine water flow in the area, with an estimated cost of $600,000. According to College Park resident Bob Baer, 'When Calvert road stormwater inlets max out, and the street water level rises only an inch or more, the street water goes down our driveway and into our yard and the neighbor’s yard,' highlighting the need for improved stormwater management in the area.

key_people: Maxine Gross, Resident
Robert Craig Baum, Wisdom 1096 Project Creator
34475.ai_summary: Maxine Gross reflected on the pandemic, recalling how College Park leaders passed Resolution 20-R016 in support of Black lives, with the mayor and city council apologizing for the city's past history of oppression and seeking restorative justice. As Gross navigated personal challenges, the city explored racial justice through forums and established a restorative justice commission. The city's efforts towards racial justice were marked by the adoption of a new resolution, with Gross noting the city's responsibility to right its own wrongs and play a role in achieving racial justice, as seen in the story of Lakeland.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Kelsey Ward, Author
Robert Day, Former College Park City Councilmember
34307.ai_summary: Stuart Adams was elected to represent District 3 on the College Park City Council, replacing Robert Day. Adams, who specializes in floodplain management, sustainability, and construction, plans to use his expertise to tackle stormwater management issues in District 3, stating 'That’s been my passion for a while, community resilience. I know what it’s like when your family loses your home and your community gets completely uprooted.' Adams' experience and focus on community resilience will likely impact College Park governance, particularly in regards to preparing for and recovering from natural disasters.

key_people: Susan Whitney, College Park City Council District 2 Representative
Llatetra Esters, College Park City Council District 2 Representative
Monroe Dennis, Former College Park City Council District 2 Representative
Kelsey Ward, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
34310.ai_summary: Susan Whitney is the new District 2 representative on the College Park City Council, replacing Monroe Dennis. Whitney, who has worked in various fields and volunteered extensively in her community, aims to foster more community involvement in District 2. She said, 'I love solving problems, getting things done and helping people. I want to be persistent and see what we can do to make positive change for our neighbors,' and hopes to engage more residents in local politics.

key_people: Derrick Davis, Prince George's County Councilmember District 6
Mel Franklin, Prince George's County Council At-Large Member
Sydney Harrison, Prince George's County Councilmember District 9
Calvin Hawkins, Prince George's County Council At-Large Member and Chair
Deni Taveras, Prince George's County Council Vice Chair and Councilmember District 2
Todd Turner, Prince George's County Councilmember District 4
Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayor
Dannielle Glaros, Prince George's County Councilmember District 3
Eric Olson, College Park Resident and Former Prince George's County Councilmember
Sophie Gorman Oriani, Streetcar Suburbs News Author
34197.ai_summary: The Prince George's County Council voted to accept a new electoral map, despite widespread citizen opposition, which divides the city of College Park and potentially favors some electoral candidates over others. As College Park Mayor Patrick Wojahn stated, 'The current proposed map divides College Park right through its center.' Deni Taveras, the vice chair of the council, defended the map, saying 'This map does more for black and brown communities than any other map ever submitted.' The new map makes some potential candidates, including College Park resident Eric Olson, ineligible to run for office due to the changed district boundaries.

key_people: Mel Franklin, Prince George’s County Councilmember
Derrick Leon Davis, Prince George’s County Councilmember
Thomas Dernoga, Prince George’s County Councilmember
Dannielle M. Glaros, Prince George’s County Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Council Candidate
Nolan Clancy, Author
33953.ai_summary: Prince George’s County Councilmember Mel Franklin proposed a redistricting plan that passed 6 to 4, which redesignates portions of College Park from District 3 to District 1, while keeping the University of Maryland in District 3. The plan was introduced after public outrage in the College Park community, with Councilmember Thomas Dernoga stating, 'With regard to the City of College Park, they’re blindsided.' The redistricting process aims to reflect the increasing diversity in the county and remedy the separation between the University of Maryland and the Discovery District, as stated by Franklin, 'This process is far more transparent than it was 10 years ago.'

key_people: Violetta Sharp-Jones, College Park Restorative Justice Steering Committee Member
Maxine Gross, College Park Restorative Justice Steering Committee Chair
Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayor
Kelly Livingston, Author
organizations: College Park Restorative Justice Steering Committee
34106.ai_summary: The College Park Restorative Justice Steering Committee has presented their suggestions to the city council, aiming to establish a Restorative Justice Commission to address the historical harm done to the Lakeland community. As Mayor Patrick Wojahn said, the establishment of the commission will give people the chance to talk about what happened and "make clear that there was real harm caused to families, caused to the community, caused to people as a result of what happened here." The committee's report includes recommendations for commission activities over a potential six-year timeline, such as seeking and facilitating community engagement and publishing a recorded history of harm.

key_people: Danny Schaible, College Park City Councilmember (Ward 2)
organizations: Town of University Park Common Council
34137.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember Danny Schaible proposed a ban on gas-powered leaf blowers to reduce noise and gas pollution in the city, with the goal of permanently stopping their use by July 2023. Schaible noted that using a gas-powered blower for an hour is equivalent to driving a Ford F-150 for 3,000 miles and can be as loud as 110 decibels, saying '[A gas-powered leaf blower] can be as loud as 110 decibels, 115 decibels in the ear of an operator.' The proposed ban is part of the city's efforts to preserve the local environment, following similar bans in surrounding jurisdictions such as University Park and Montgomery County.

key_people: Ken Ulman, President of Margrave Strategies
Stacey Price, Co-founder and CEO of Shop Made in D.C.
Michael Babin, Co-founder of Shop Made in D.C.
Kelsey Ward, Author
34108.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously approved a one-time grant from the American Rescue Plan Act of 2021 to support the opening of Shop Made in Maryland, a retail store featuring products from local artisans, in the new College Park City Hall. According to Stacey Price, Co-founder and CEO of Shop Made in D.C., 'We like to label ourselves as a modern retail incubator. We provide a place to showcase talent … We provide business assistance so they can become more profitable in what they are doing, from packaging to pricing and all the other things.' The store will offer products from artisans throughout the state, with a focus on Prince George’s County and College Park, and is expected to take six months to curate products.

key_people: Cheryl Harrington, Virtual Cooking Demo Host
Lisa Walker, Hyattsville Aging in Place Board Chair
Miriam Bader, College Park Senior City Planner
Agnes Artemel, Artemel & Associates Inc. President
organizations: University of Baltimore's Jacob France Institute
34100.ai_summary: The City of College Park is working to improve the quality of life for seniors, with efforts such as the Corridor Conversations monthly series, which brings together senior citizens for events like virtual cooking demos. As Miriam Bader, College Park's senior city planner, notes, 'When you can no longer drive, then it is difficult to age in place, due to lack of walkable grocery stores and walkable medical facilities.' The city is also seeking to become a member of AARP's Network of Age-Friendly States and Communities, and is developing an action plan to address issues of aging, with Agnes Artemel, president of Artemel & Associates Inc., stating 'We want to help College Park become an even more age-friendly community than it already is.'

key_people: Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayoral candidate
Kate Kennedy, College Park District 1 candidate
Fazlul Kabir, College Park District 1 candidate
Llatetra Esters, College Park District 2 candidate
Bob Catlin, College Park District 2 candidate
Susan Whitney, College Park District 2 candidate
Maria Mackie, College Park District 4 candidate
Denise Mitchell, College Park District 4 candidate
33771.ai_summary: The College Park Here and Now hosted virtual forums for candidates running in the upcoming election, including mayoral and district candidates. The forums allowed local residents to ask questions and hear from the candidates. Videos of the forums are available to watch, featuring candidates such as Patrick Wojahn, Kate Kennedy, and Llatetra Esters, among others.

key_people: Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayor
Mary Cook, North College Park Neighborhood Association President
Danny Schaible, Hyattsville Councilmember
33836.ai_summary: The Countywide Map Amendment (CMA) process has raised concerns among College Park legislators and community activists, who fear that the new zoning classifications will substantively change the character of their neighborhoods. College Park Mayor Patrick Wojahn sent a letter to the county council expressing concerns that the city council and staff did not have enough input into the zoning decisions, and Mary Cook, president of the North College Park Neighborhood Association, noted that 'I am concerned that these new zoning classifications are going to set developers up so that they can create a new density in our area.' The CMA process has also been criticized for potentially favoring developers and allowing them to 'game the system' to sneak in zoning intensifications.

key_people: Eric Olson, Author
33888.ai_summary: The City of College Park is undergoing significant construction projects, including the development of Tempo, a student housing project, the University of Maryland's new School of Public Policy building, and a new City Hall. These projects are expected to be completed in summer 2022, with the goal of transforming Baltimore Avenue into a college town main street. As stated by the author, 'the good news is that most of these projects are moving along at a steady pace, without delay'.

key_people: Tonia James, Organizer with Embry AME Church
Karen Stewart, Owner of Jesse's Soul Line Dance
Melissa Sites, Director of College Park Arts Exchange
Rev. Carrington Carter, Head Pastor at Embry AME Church
Pierce Panagakos, Author
33883.ai_summary: The Embry AME Church in College Park's historically Black neighborhood of Lakeland hosted a dance event to bring the community together. As Tonia James, one of the organizers, stated, 'We just want to have the community come out and enjoy themselves. … we just want to have a fun time, a fun experience for anybody that’s coming out — little children, the seniors, the adults, the youth — anybody that’s participating, we would love for them to come out and to learn some dance steps.' The event is part of the church's efforts to move forward after the City of College Park passed a resolution renouncing systemic racism and declaring support for Black lives, with Rev. Carrington Carter noting the church's history and its goal to have an impact on the greater College Park community.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Alex Keating, VeoRide Director of Public Policy and Partnerships
Robert Day, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Terry Schum, City Planner
Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember
33773.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering extending VeoRide's micro-mobility pilot program to a three-year contract, despite concerns about mismanagement. Councilmember John Rigg expressed concerns about the company's management, stating 'I've seen [residents] throw them in the stream, I've seen them be hit with cars, I've seen people throw them in the streets.' Councilmembers also raised issues with improperly parked vehicles and the need for accountability from VeoRide managers, as Councilmember Mitchell stated 'Several vehicles are left there for days, I have very great reservations about your product.'

key_people: Stacy Hornstein, Director of Development for Republic Properties
Terry Schum, College Park Director of Planning, Community and Economic Development
Mark Vogel, Founder of Mark Vogel Companies
Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayor
Denise Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember
33651.ai_summary: Developers propose multifamily housing in place of a hotel on the corner of Campus and Corporal Frank Scott drives in College Park. The site's developer, Republic Properties, no longer believes it is feasible to build a hotel due to the pandemic's impact on the hospitality industry, with Stacy Hornstein stating 'The financing community who would participate with us … has evaporated. You can’t get financing for a hotel today.' The proposed housing development would have smaller units and be marketed to young professionals and the 55-and-older community, with Councilmember Denise Mitchell expressing her support for the project.

key_people: James Cirrone, Journalist
Todd Reitzel, Member of the Tree and Landscape Board
Dr. Ian Mather, College Park Resident
Emily Friend, College Park Resident
Bill Gardiner, Interim City Manager of College Park
33577.ai_summary: The College Park City Council delayed a vote on a proposed tree ordinance after a lengthy public hearing, where around 30 residents testified, including Emily Friend, who said 'I believe if we’re going to have a successful tree planting program, it should not be punitive.' The ordinance aims to boost the city's tree canopy, which has been declining, with a focus on homeowners, but some residents argued it puts an undue burden on them. The council will reconsider the ordinance, which includes fines for cutting down trees without replanting, with concerns about the cost of compliance, as Friend noted 'I think a lot of residents are going to choose a fine and then be very bitter about it and never want to plant a tree again.'

key_people: Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Kate Kennedy, College Park City Councilmember
Jax Chaudhry, Literacy Lab Regional Director
33567.ai_summary: The Literacy Lab, a nonprofit organization, is coming to Hollywood Elementary School to provide reading tutors for the 2021-2022 school year. City councilmembers, particularly Councilmember Maria Mackie, were enthusiastic about the pilot program, which will account for no more than $40,000 out of College Park’s FY22 budget. As Councilmember Mackie said, “We saw a problem. We found a solution in the Literacy Lab and we worked on implementing that solution in hopes that this small step could be the first step to helping many students throughout College Park and PG County,” and with the program now moving forward, Councilmember Kate Kennedy noted, “Now everyone’s on board, and we’re moving forward.”

key_people: Kim Seif, Author
Major James Keleti, Prince George’s County Police Department Commander of Special Operations Division
Bob Ryan, College Park Public Services Director
Fazlul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember
Captain Gilbert Bowling, Prince George’s County Police Department Assistant Commander
33505.ai_summary: The College Park City Council and Prince George’s County Police Department are exploring enforcement options to address the growing number of noise complaints from modified exhausts. As Major James Keleti stated, police can issue equipment repair orders and citations to owners of vehicles that make excessive noise, with 29 orders and 66 citations issued between April 16 and July 8. Councilmember Fazlul Kabir said, 'This issue is getting worse,' and emphasized the need for additional enforcement options, citing a joint operation in Montgomery County as a potential model.

key_people: Robert Marsili, College Park Department of Public Works Director
Paul Ruffins, Author
organizations: College Park Department of Public Works
33469.ai_summary: The City of College Park's Department of Public Works Director, Robert Marsili, explained that every city or town in Prince George's County handles its solid waste differently, with local jurisdictions evolving different policies over time. As Marsili noted, 'Every city or town in Prince George's County handles its solid waste a little differently,' and the county has expanded and upgraded its facilities to meet EPA requirements, particularly concerning groundwater pollution. The county's waste disposal history and current practices are significant for College Park governance, as the city's population growth and increasing dependence on single-use plastics and packaging have put pressure on landfill facilities.

key_people: Pablo Regis de Oliveira, EducArte Co-founder and Executive Director
Pablo Regis de Oliveira, Prince George’s County Arts and Humanities Council Program Manager
Melissa Sites, College Park Arts Exchange Executive Director
Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayor
André Coelho, Percussionist
organizations: Prince George’s County Arts and Humanities Council
33507.ai_summary: The College Park Arts Exchange, in collaboration with EducArte, hosted Brazilian percussion workshops at the Old Parish House, led by percussionist André Coelho. Pablo Regis de Oliveira, EducArte’s co-founder and executive director, organized the workshops, stating 'I’ve been driving by this building for so many years, and it’s nice to actually be doing an event here that’s for our community.' The events were supported by Mayor Patrick Wojahn and the College Park City Council, and mark a return to in-person events in College Park after pandemic-related restrictions were lifted.

key_people: Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayor
Fazlul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Bill Gardiner, College Park Interim City Manager
Gary Fields, College Park Director of Finance
James Cirrone, Author
key_issues: grant assistance for small businesses and nonprofits
30398.ai_summary: College Park is expecting $22 million from the American Rescue Plan Act by the end of July, with the first half of the funds scheduled to arrive soon. Mayor Patrick Wojahn and the city council discussed how to use the incoming funds, with interim City Manager Bill Gardiner suggesting using the funds to pay off the city's financial losses and provide grant assistance to small businesses and nonprofits. Councilmember Fazlul Kabir noted that the funds could help residents and businesses recover from the COVID pandemic, saying 'This is a huge opportunity for us to invest' and 'to recover from what we have lost due to the COVID pandemic'.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Shawn Joseph, Interim Superintendent of Prince George’s County Public Schools
Judith White, Prince George’s County Public Schools Chief Academic Officer
Alexandra Tyukavina, Paint Branch PTA Secretary
organizations: Prince George’s County Board of Education
51753.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted to send a letter to the Prince George’s County Board of Education in support of continued funding for the Chinese immersion program at Paint Branch Elementary School. The program has become an important part of the broader educational ecosystem serving College Park residents, and changes that significantly reduce immersion opportunities could undermine years of investment by educators, students and the community. Paint Branch PTA Secretary Alexandra Tyukavina proposed a third alternative to address budget issues, adding an opt-out option and devoting one full-day English-speaking teacher to students not wishing to take lessons in Mandarin, stating 'We, the Paint Branch school community, have found that the board’s proposal to eliminate the program is not the only way to address the budget issues, nor is it ideal for the students and families within the school'.

key_people: Sami Saeed, University of Maryland Public Policy Major
Holly Simmons, College Park Councilmember
David Dorsch, College Park Resident and Landlord
Richard Biffl, Landlord
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Nick DiSpirito, University of Maryland Student Liaison to the College Park City Council
51534.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously approved an ordinance that prevents landlords from asking tenants to renew a lease sooner than 180 days from the end of the current lease. Sami Saeed, a University of Maryland public policy major, shared his story of being approached by his former landlord just four weeks after moving in with a substantial rent increase, saying 'The stress that that caused me … trying to find anyone to live with for next year. I had to call 50 people.' The ordinance, which was amended to count the timeframe from the end of the lease instead of the beginning, aims to protect tenants from aggressive lease renewal practices and will fine landlords $500 for the first offense and $2,500 for subsequent offenses.

key_people: Donald Hays, College Park Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee Subcommittee Chairman
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
51397.ai_summary: A citizen-led committee has resubmitted a report on school bus stop safety to the College Park City Council, recommending upgrades at seven bus stops that scored low on a safety evaluation. Donald Hays, the chairman of the subcommittee, expressed frustration that the city has not acted on the recommendations, saying 'I personally feel that is an outrage and there has been very little movement and very little progress.' The committee's recommendations include adding bike lanes and sidewalks to improve safety for elementary school children waiting for their buses.

key_people: Maxine Gross, Lakeland Community Heritage Project Member
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
51400.ai_summary: The Lakeland neighborhood in College Park is adding 10 murals to highlight its history, as part of a decades-long effort to preserve the community's heritage. According to Maxine Gross, a member of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project, 'It's just a way for folks to be able to see the community that we have been talking about for a number of years.' The project is a joint effort among city officials, community members, and University of Maryland partners, with a total budget of $28,382, and College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir said 'This will be a wonderful and very easy and entertaining way to know about what happened and what used to be the historic Lakeland.'

key_people: Frances Chen, University of Maryland Student
Nicholas Crane, University of Maryland Student
Ashna Pandya, University of Maryland Student
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
51390.ai_summary: Three University of Maryland students, Frances Chen, Nicholas Crane, and Ashna Pandya, presented to the College Park City Council on ways to ease neighborhood tensions near the university. They suggested that the city and university could do more to help undergraduate tenants become better neighbors, citing issues like 'studentification' of established neighborhoods and high rents. As Chen said, 'We found that the most successful communities have used deep university and city cooperation to create a solution that works for both students and [homeowners] and balances affordable housing for the student population [with] enforcing city codes', while Councilmember Alan Hew emphasized that the university should take more responsibility for teaching its students how to be good neighbors.

key_people: Jim Miller, College Park Parking Enforcement Officer
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember, District 2
51376.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering expanding permit parking in residential neighborhoods. Parking Enforcement Officer Jim Miller proposed a study to determine whether to restrict parking on additional streets, stating 'It's a matter of how that restricted parking is utilized.' Councilmembers had mixed reactions to the proposal to charge for permits, with former Councilmember Susan Whitney suggesting the city offer incentives, such as tax breaks, to encourage residents to own fewer cars, saying 'We want people who need their cars to be able to park them, and we want people who can get by with one car to do that and to be encouraged to do that.'

key_people: Matthew Tedesco, Lawyer for Cruz Development Corp.
Kelly Jordan, College Park City Councilmember
Carlos Nunes, Senior Planner for the City of College Park
51441.ai_summary: The College Park City Council agreed to recommend amending a parking ordinance to allow a developer to build a four-story, 87-unit apartment building for older residents on Branchville Road. The project, called Branchville Crossing, will provide affordable housing for residents 62 and older, with rent based on 60% of the area median income, and will be financed using a 4% federal low-income housing tax credit. As Councilmember Kelly Jordan said, 'There is a real need for senior housing in College Park,' and the developer has 'listened to the residents … to see what would fit here, what’s needed here.'

key_people: Wes Moore, Maryland Governor
Joseline Peña-Melnyk, House Speaker of the Maryland House of Delegates, District 21
Jim Rosapepe, Maryland State Senator, District 21
Tom Dernoga, Prince George’s County Councilmember, District 1
51373.ai_summary: Maryland Governor Wes Moore has proposed a bill to change Maryland zoning law to promote construction of more affordable homes by allowing developers to build at least eight houses per acre. House Speaker Joseline Peña-Melnyk, who represents College Park, is a strong advocate of Moore's proposal, stating 'Maryland has a housing shortage of roughly 100,000 homes' and 'We need more housing choices. Smaller homes. Townhouses. Accessory dwelling units.' However, State Sen. Jim Rosapepe and Prince George’s County Councilmember Tom Dernoga have expressed reservations about the proposal, with Rosapepe suggesting the state should focus on practical issues and Dernoga opposing the bill due to concerns about congestion, pollution, and public safety.

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Erica Johnson, Prince George’s County Police Department Sergeant
51366.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember Alan Hew has graduated from three different citizen police academies, including those offered by the Prince George’s County Police Department, University of Maryland Police Department, and Prince George’s County Sheriff’s Office. Hew stated, 'There’s a lot of speculation of how they operate and I wanted to be able to speak confidently in support of the departments that operate within our city.' This training has helped him contribute to council discussions about safety and build trust with police officers, allowing him to share insights and help his constituents better understand police operations.

key_people: Aisha Braveboy, Prince George’s County Executive
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Keith R. Sydnor, Laurel Mayor
51316.ai_summary: A winter storm named Fern brought a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain to the Route 1 corridor, prompting local jurisdictions to mount snow removal and emergency response efforts. Prince George’s County Executive Aisha Braveboy said, 'The county’s snow response operation is fully active. Crews are working around the clock to clear and treat the roadways and public safety remains our top priority.' College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir reminded residents to report icy sidewalks and unsafe conditions to the city, and to check when the streets they live on were last plowed through an interactive map published by the city.

key_people: Tina Nguyen, Owner of Queens Chapel Barbershop
Melvin Franklin (Frank) Key, Customer of Queens Chapel Barbershop
Joshua Solomon, College Park City Councilmember (Ward 5)
Kelson Nisbett, College Park City Councilmember (Ward 5)
Jarod Towers, Hyattsville Police Chief
Laura Lanham, Hyattsville Deputy Police Chief
Merrill Hartson, Retired Editor of AP News
51173.ai_summary: Queens Chapel Barbershop celebrated its 85th anniversary with a party on January 9, attended by customers, city council members Joshua Solomon and Kelson Nisbett, and Hyattsville Police Chief Jarod Towers. Merrill Hartson, a customer and retired editor of AP News, gave poetical remarks, saying: 'For 85 years, the Queens Chapel Barbershop has persevered, never short of sheers, never at a loss for cheers', comparing the shop to the TV show Cheers where 'everyone knows your name'. The event highlights the importance of local businesses and community engagement in College Park.

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Alex Posorske, Veo Policy and Partnerships Manager for the East Coast
51475.ai_summary: The use of electric scooters and bikes in College Park and University Park has increased by 62% between fall 2024 and fall 2025, leading Veo to implement stricter parking rules. Veo now charges riders an extra $4 to $5 for parking outside of designated areas and fines customers up to $60 for repeated violations, as stated by Alex Posorske, Veo Policy and Partnerships Manager for the East Coast. Mayor Fazlul Kabir expressed support for Veo, saying 'We are very happy that we have this micromobility system in College Park because this is in line with our strategic sustainability goal,' and 'Every time someone rides a Veo scooter, that person is not using the car, right? So we’re taking out the cars from the street.'

key_people: Daniel Oates, College Park City Councilmember
Kelly Jordan, College Park City Councilmember
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Holly Simmons, College Park City Councilmember
51408.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has become younger with the election of three newcomers, including Daniel Oates, Kelly Jordan, and Holly Simmons, who join existing members like Jacob Hernandez and Ray Ranker. As Oates noted, 'To me what’s interesting is less [about] the relative ages than the new people coming on the council and bringing different experiences and different perspectives from that vein.' The new councilmembers believe their diverse perspectives will help the city think about its future, particularly on issues like rising utility bills, school quality, parks, and safety, with Jordan saying 'We’ll all have lots of questions and … when you’re used to something, comfortable with something, new questions can bring new ideas for how you’re used to looking at it.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Anthony Brown, Maryland Attorney General
Donald Trump, President of the United States
organizations: U.S. Bureau of City Labor Statistics
51404.ai_summary: A year into President Donald Trump's second term, College Park is grappling with the impact of executive orders, employee layoffs, funding cuts, and new policies. 'We do not know how things will actually go in the future,' said College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir, noting the city will continue to support the community. The city has increased its financial assistance program budget to $100,000 to help residents affected by job losses, including those from the Beltsville Agricultural Research Center and the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration.

key_people: Joseline Peña-Melnyk, Maryland House of Delegates Speaker
Adrienne A. Jones, Baltimore County Delegate and former Maryland House of Delegates Speaker
Jim Rosapepe, state Senator
Ben Barnes, Delegate
Mary Lehman, Delegate and former College Park City Council staffer
organizations: U.S. Attorney’s Office for the District of Columbia
House Health and Government Operations Committee
50899.ai_summary: Joseline Peña-Melnyk, a College Park resident and former College Park City Councilmember, was sworn in as the 109th Speaker of the Maryland House of Delegates. Peña-Melnyk, who replaced Speaker Adrienne A. Jones, called herself an 'inclusive' leader and intends to collaborate with both Democrats and Republicans. She said, 'This moment belongs not just to me, but to every child catching rain in a pot,' and was praised by Delegate Mary Lehman as 'a tireless public servant who has given 100% to the job of delegate for nearly 20 years.'

key_people: Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Edouard Haba, Hyattsville City Council Member and Prince George’s County Municipal Association President
Monica Casañas, Colmar Manor Mayor and Prince George’s County Municipal Association Vice President
50826.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has paused its membership in the Prince George’s County Municipal Association (PGCMA) after a former treasurer allegedly embezzled over $30,000. Councilmember Jacob Hernandez stated, 'It’s our taxpayer money... Why would we give money to an organization that is clearly lacking integrity?' PGCMA President Edouard Haba said the organization will submit to financial reviews and has formed a financial oversight committee to prevent future thefts, noting 'We hope that we’re stronger together' and 'Our towns are stronger when we stand together and speak with one voice.'

key_people: Carly Zapernick, Article Student Living Managing Director of Construction and Development
Julie Bringman, Hope Lutheran Church Pastor
Chris Hatcher, Attorney representing Article
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember (District 2)
Denise Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember (District 4)
Nick DiSpirito, University of Maryland Student
50763.ai_summary: College Park officials pressed Article Student Living, a Chicago-based student housing developer, to include affordable units in a proposed 192-unit complex on Guilford Drive. The project would replace Hope Lutheran Church and include a new church space, with Pastor Julie Bringman noting the new space will be larger, fully accessible, and may have room for offices or affordable housing units. Mayor Fazlul Kabir encouraged Article to use the city's tax incentive program, stating 'The catch is that you must have affordable housing in it,' as the city aims to bring more affordable housing to College Park, with Councilmember Denise Mitchell emphasizing 'We have a vision moving forward, and we're not going to negate that vision.'

key_people: Nisha Kumar, University of Maryland student
Scott Tebbe, University of Maryland student
Richard Biffle, landlord
Lisa Miller, President of the Prince George's Property Owners Association
Nick DiSpirito, UMD student liaison to the College Park City Council
Amira Abujuma, UMD student liaison to the College Park City Council
50602.ai_summary: Nearly 30 people, mostly student tenants of downtown high-rise apartment buildings, asked the College Park City Council to prevent landlords from pressuring tenants to renew leases before they have lived in their units for six months. Students like Nisha Kumar and Scott Tebbe shared their experiences of being pressured to re-sign leases early, with Kumar saying 'We've just moved in for the semester and we already have to figure out next year... Who's going to take my spot? ... Will I have to pay over $1,000 a month for a place I'm not even living in?' and Tebbe stating 'This was a decision I wasn’t ready to make, and I felt trapped.' The proposed ordinance aims to protect students from 'predatory' lease-renewal agreements and is opposed by some landlords, who argue that it would limit their control over tenant selection.

key_people: Samantha McLoughlin, Parent Teacher Organization President of Hollywood Elementary School
Ashley Franceski, Kindergarten Teacher of Hollywood Elementary School
Carl Franceski, General Manager of Sales and Operations for Coca-Cola Consolidated
Carlos Johnson, Principal of Hollywood Elementary School
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember (District 1)
organizations: Parent Teacher Organization of Hollywood Elementary School
50509.ai_summary: The local bottler for Coca-Cola gave each of Hollywood Elementary School’s 301 students new sneakers, socks, and school supplies on Nov. 12, through the Back-to-School with Purpose initiative. According to Principal Carlos Johnson, 'When you can provide for a parent and the things that they need at home, school and home become one.' City Councilmember Jacob Hernandez praised Principal Johnson's efforts, saying 'He’s gone above and beyond to make sure he’s providing meaningful outreach,' and noted his ability to build relationships in the local community. The event showcases the community's support for the school and its students, with the Parent Teacher Organization and University of Maryland also involved in supporting the school.

key_people: Helen Reniere, Resident of Attick Towers
Glenn Reniere, Engineer at Vitro Corp.
Charlotte Davis, Staff Member at Attick Towers
50310.ai_summary: Helen Reniere, a 100-year-old resident of Attick Towers, was congratulated by the College Park mayor and City Council for reaching the milestone birthday. Reniere said, 'I get up in the morning, I take my medicine and … I guess you got to put Jesus first.' She also praised her friendship with Charlotte Davis, a staff member at the apartment complex, saying 'The best thing in my life is Charlotte, she's the best thing that ever happened to me.' This celebration highlights the importance of community engagement and support for seniors in College Park.

key_people: Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Sonja Ewing, M-NCPPC Representative
Bob Patten, M-NCPPC Representative
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission
50276.ai_summary: The College Park City Council endorsed the construction of a bridge connecting Old Calvert Road Park to the Northeast Branch Trail, with Councilmember Ray Ranker stating 'We don’t want to spend another month waiting, we want to get [construction] started … tomorrow.' The estimated cost for the proposed bridge is about $2 million, and the council considered a less-expensive option to modify the Campus Drive bridge. The bridge is a long-awaited project, with Councilmember Ranker noting that the community has been asking for it for at least 10 years, and it aims to address safety concerns with the current route to the trail.

key_people: Gavin Neubauer, University of Maryland Public Policy Student and College Park Child Care Task Force Member
Christina Martin Kenny, College Park Estates Resident and College Park Child Care Task Force Member
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
organizations: College Park Child Care Task Force
50298.ai_summary: The College Park Child Care Task Force, appointed by the city council, has proposed ways to lower child care costs, including grants to home-based child care providers and property tax credits to established centers. As Gavin Neubauer, a University of Maryland public policy student and task force member, said, 'If you’re able to even increase the amount of businesses here that can survive and care for children, we’re going to be a real standout city in this entire metropolitan area.' The task force's report recommends nearly 20 actions to lower child care costs and increase availability in the city, which has 18 licensed child care providers, including 11 day care centers, five home-based providers, and two tax-exempt religious organizations offering after-school care.

key_people: Jatinder Khokhar, College Park Director of Public Services
Stephanie Anderson, College Park City Attorney
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
50304.ai_summary: The College Park City Council introduced an ordinance to increase fines for city code violations, including construction, fire safety, housing, noise, and unruly social gatherings, to align with a Maryland law allowing municipalities to fine up to $5,000. Director of Public Services Jatinder Khokhar and City Attorney Stephanie Anderson presented the proposal, which aims to ensure fairness and consistency of enforcement. As Councilmember John Rigg noted, 'Noise should be bad, but unruly social gatherings almost always include noise and it includes a lot more.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
50292.ai_summary: In the Nov. 4 election, 1,598 people voted, accounting for 9.9% of registered voters in College Park. Six teenagers, aged 16 and 17, cast their ballots, marking the first time this age group was allowed to vote in city elections. Mayor Fazlul Kabir won 1,480 votes, while Jacob Hernandez and Alan Hew were the top vote-getters for council seats, with 536 and 492 votes, respectively.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Marc Lore, Wonder Founder
Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Director
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
50876.ai_summary: The food hall chain Wonder has opened its first Maryland location in College Park on Baltimore Avenue, offering food from over 15 restaurants and brands. According to College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, 'The city has been working hard... to bring an exciting concept here,' and 'I think they hit it out of the park.' Mayor Fazlul Kabir said it's 'a milestone and College Park is very lucky to have them,' and the city's economic development director, Michael Williams, noted that the location is 'the epicenter of downtown College Park' and a great fit for the variety of food options Wonder offers.

Kelly Jordan, College Park City Councilmember-elect and Berwyn District Civic Association Co-President
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember

50191.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has three new members after voters in Districts 2 and 3 elected newcomers to the dais on Nov. 4. Holly Simmons and Kelly Jordan won a three-way election for two District 2 seats, with Jordan beating opponent Chris Gill by three votes after a hand recount. Daniel Oates won the seat in District 3, running unopposed. As Jordan said, 'It's a good feeling... I'm happy to be able to serve the residents and excited about the next two years.' Simmons also expressed her intention to 'listen and learn' as a new councilmember, saying 'I think there's a lot of important work that's underway in the city.'

key_people: Brencis D. Smith, Laurel City Council Member
Christine Johnson, Laurel City Council Member
Holly Simmons, College Park City Council Member
Christopher Gill, College Park City Council Candidate
Kelly Jordan, College Park City Council Candidate
50185.ai_summary: The results of the local elections in College Park and Laurel are uncertain, with tight ballot counts in contested races. In Laurel, Brencis D. Smith may have an edge over incumbent Christine Johnson for the At-Large council seat, while in College Park District 2, Holly Simmons is likely to have secured one of two seats, with Christopher Gill and Kelly Jordan only one vote apart for the second seat. The results will be certified after mail-in and provisional ballots are counted.

key_people: Chris Gill, College Park City Council Candidate
Kelly Jordan, College Park City Council Candidate and Co-President of the Berwyn District Civic Association
Holly Simmons, College Park City Council Candidate and Deputy Zoning Manager for the City of Rockville

key_issues: Restorative justice for the Lakeland community
50017.ai_summary: The three candidates for two vacant College Park City Council seats in District 2, Chris Gill, Kelly Jordan, and Holly Simmons, discussed their priorities at a candidates' forum hosted by College Park Here & Now. They agreed that restorative justice for the Lakeland community and affordable housing are key issues, with Simmons stating that 'reparations are very important' and Jordan suggesting that new housing developments should include affordable units. The candidates are running to fill the seats of former councilmembers Llatetra Brown Esters and Susan Whitney.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
49848.ai_summary: College Park voters will elect a mayor and eight City Councilmembers in November. Fazlul Kabir, the current mayor of College Park, is running for re-election, having previously served six terms on the city council. As mayor, Kabir has led efforts to strengthen the city's finances, open new community spaces, and create jobs, and he is committed to building a stronger, more vibrant College Park by expanding affordable housing.

key_people: Dan Oates, College Park City Council Candidate
Ray Ranker, College Park City Council Candidate
Ray Ranker, Chaplain of the Lutheran Campus Ministry
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
John Rigg, College Park Councilmember
Shawn Anderson, WTOP radio anchor
49703.ai_summary: District 3 City Council candidates Dan Oates and Ray Ranker discussed their plans to improve public safety in College Park without creating a city police force, with Oates stating 'I think jumping to the answer of ‘we need a police force’ is premature' and Ranker noting that measures like adding more security cameras or blue light phones may be more effective. The candidates also emphasized the need for affordable housing and resident involvement in the city. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir, who is running unopposed, highlighted his achievements, including completing the first phase of a major project that improved safety and traffic flow along Baltimore Avenue.

key_people: Christopher Gill, College Park City Council Candidate
Kelly Jordan, College Park City Council Candidate
Holly Simmons, College Park City Council Candidate
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
49536.ai_summary: Three candidates, Christopher Gill, Kelly Jordan, and Holly Simmons, are running for two College Park City Council seats in District 2. Gill, an IT project manager, wants the city to become a place for people in all phases of life and is concerned about housing affordability and owner-occupied homes being flipped into rentals, saying 'I don’t want people to be pushed out of their community.' Jordan, who has been active in civic life, is seeking to extend her service by running for city council, with her campaign rooted in representation, as she says 'I want to make sure that there are lots of different interests that all get heard from and incorporated into the decision-making.'

key_people: Lucas Rigg, son of College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
Lovisa Jordan, daughter of College Park City Council candidate
Kelly Jordan, College Park City Council candidate (District 2)
Lazarus McCarthy, DeMatha Catholic High School student
Dylan Cooney-Barrera, College Park resident
organizations: Prince George's County Board of Elections
49533.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted 5-3 to lower the voting age for city elections from 18 to 16, allowing 16- and 17-year-olds to vote for mayor and city councilmembers. As Lucas Rigg, a 16-year-old resident, stated, 'I think it lets the people who didn’t choose to live here have a say.' Lovisa Jordan, also 16, noted that 'I think it’s cool... I think that it’s an option that we can get our voices and opinions heard' and that preparing to vote could make teenagers more aware of what’s going on in the city. This change makes College Park the ninth Maryland city to allow teens to vote.

key_people: Danny Copeland, Vice President for RST Development
Orlando Velez, Director of Operations and Housing Initiatives for College Park City-University Partnership
Kendra McAbee, Communications Coordinator for College Park City-University Partnership
David Varnado, Senior Program and Operations Manager
organizations: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD)
locations: College Park–University of Maryland Metro Station
49529.ai_summary: The city of College Park offers affordable housing options, including the Flats at College Park, a 317-unit apartment complex with income-restricted units, and the College Park Community Preservation Trust, which sells single-family homes to eligible buyers. According to Danny Copeland, Vice President for RST Development, the Flats at College Park were financed in part through the federal Low-Income Housing Tax Credit program. The city also offers programs like Live+Work College Park, which provides zero-interest loans to full-time employees who purchase a home in the city, aiming to make housing more affordable for residents.

key_people: Maxine Gross, Chair of the Restorative Justice Commission
Ruth Murphy, Restorative Justice Commission Member
Karin Brown, Restorative Justice Commission Member
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
50287.ai_summary: The 20-member Restorative Justice Commission is urging the College Park City Council to provide reparations to the displaced residents of Lakeland, who were affected by urban renewal from 1969 to 1981. As Maxine Gross, the chair of the commission, said, 'When you talk to people who experienced Lakeland before urban renewal, they speak of a vibrant community.' The commission's recommendations include financial compensation, housing priority, and free college tuition for those who lived in Lakeland for at least 10 years or their direct descendants, and the city council will discuss these proposals in a future meeting.

key_people: Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember (District 1)
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
51542.ai_summary: College Park officials are considering annexing multiple properties, including the soon-to-be-vacant Beltsville Agricultural Research Center (BARC) and several other nearby properties. 'We're kind of just barely in the initial stages of talking about it,' said Councilmember Jacob Hernandez, who noted that the city would consider annexing land to protect it, for economic development or to boost population. 'Should a property fall within the city limits, the residents of College Park have much more of a say in what gets built there,' Hernandez said, emphasizing the importance of community involvement in the annexation process.

key_people: Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Teresa Way-Pezzuti, College Park Director of Human Resources
key_issues: mayor and mayor pro tem compensation
49170.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering a proposal to double the part-time salaries of councilmembers from $10,000 to $20,000 a year. 'Everyone on this dais works incredibly hard for the residents that we care for,' said Councilmember Jacob Hernandez, adding that the proposed salary increase 'might not be extraordinarily significant, but it does help cover the costs.' The council is reviewing the compensation and benefits of city officials, comparing them to other Maryland municipalities, with the goal of making the positions more attractive to community members, as suggested by Councilmember Hernandez and agreed upon by Councilmember Ray Ranker, who noted that 'if the pay was $1 million, more people would probably run for office than if the pay were $0 per year.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Miriam Bader, College Park Director of Planning and Community Development
Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Director
Richard Biffle, Property Owner
organizations: College Park Department of Planning and Community Development
49106.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted to require owners of vacant homes and commercial property to register their empty buildings with the city, with owners paying $25 to register and facing fines for non-compliance. According to College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir, 'Vacant properties can negatively impact our community... When left unmonitored, they can become safety hazards, attract vandalism and contribute to blight.' City Councilmember Jacob Hernandez agreed, stating 'It supports improving public safety... It protects property values.' The ordinance aims to track vacant properties and ensure compliance with maintenance standards.

key_people: Maria Cordone, College Park Resident
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
49102.ai_summary: A city-commissioned noise analysis by RK&K found that traffic noise is 'clearly audible in the front yards' on Edmonston Road and warrants a more detailed study and potential noise mitigation by the State Highway Administration. Residents, including Maria Cordone, have complained about the noise, which they say has increased since the city removed trees between Edmonston Road and Kenilworth Avenue. College Park City Councilmember Ray Ranker said the city is preparing to plant trees and shrubs between the two streets to help with sound and beautification, and some residents have suggested the state should install traffic-calming devices to stop speeders.

49098.ai_summary: Five first-time candidates and five incumbents have filed to run for seats on the College Park City Council in the Nov. 4 election. The candidates are competing for seats in Districts 1, 2, 3, and 4, with some incumbents running unopposed. The election will be held at City Hall, with early voting taking place at Davis Hall on Nov. 2.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Christian Shannon, CALS Chief
49093.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, who is also a volunteer paramedic, shared his experience with speed bumps causing pain to patients with long bone fractures, saying 'When you have a long bone fracture, especially like a hip or a pelvis, which happen more often than you think, speed bumps can cause pain.' Rigg's expertise in emergency health care has influenced his decisions as a city official, and he has volunteered for 16 years with Calvert Advanced Life Support (CALS). As a councilmember, Rigg disagreed with his colleagues on installing speed humps on Autoville Drive, instead suggesting the installation of a sidewalk as a solution to traffic calming.

key_people: Nausicaä Sinclair, Former College Park Resident
Michelle Johnson, Executive Director of the Housing Authority of the City of College Park
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: Housing Authority of the City of College Park
U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
49087.ai_summary: Nausicaä Sinclair, a 23-year-old born and raised in College Park's Calvert Hills neighborhood, moved out of her hometown due to soaring rent prices, saying 'I would love to move back to College Park... I see so much additional development, but unfortunately, I’ve not seen that translate to any sort of decrease in prices.' The city has a shortage of affordable housing, with the average rent for a one-bedroom apartment exceeding the recommended 30% of monthly income for a University of Maryland graduate. According to Michelle Johnson, 'Affordable housing is a human right,' and Llatetra Brown Esters emphasized that 'no matter how much money you make, you should be able to come in this community and find housing, housing that is suitable and housing that is quality.'

key_people: Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
49076.ai_summary: Three College Park City Councilmembers, Llatetra Brown Esters, Susan Whitney, and John Rigg, have announced they will not run for re-election, citing the need for more family and personal time. Rigg stated, 'My falls are so busy... Given that it’s fall [and] I’ve got a kid starting college soccer next week, [leaving the council] feels comfortable.' Esters noted that the job of councilmember can be 'taxing' to 'be on' all the time, while Mayor Fazlul Kabir highlighted the importance of being 'realistic about how much time and energy you have' to avoid burnout.

49021.ai_summary: County Executive Aisha Braveboy met with local officials in College Park to discuss issues facing the city and surrounding neighborhoods as part of her 'Elevate Prince George’s' tour. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir asked Braveboy to support a shift in land use authority to cities, and Braveboy expressed her opposition to the shutdown of the Beltsville Agricultural Research Center (BARC), stating 'There are 2,600 people who [work at BARC],... many of them live in College Park and the surrounding communities... and their livelihoods are at stake.' The meeting aimed to increase the commercial tax base responsibly and in coordination with residents and community leaders.

key_people: Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park Councilmember
Jeffrey Ratnow, Cruz Development Corp Consultant

48941.ai_summary: A Boston-based development company, Cruz Development Corp, has proposed an 87-unit affordable senior housing project at Branchville Road and University Boulevard in College Park. The project has received support from city officials, but concerns have been raised about affordability, noise, and parking. As Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell said, 'It seems like the rate of rent is still high for seniors on subsidized incomes,' and Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters asked what the developers plan to do about noise, given that 'University Boulevard is notorious for lots of noise and driving at all types of speed, all during the day and evening.'

key_people: Amira Abujuma, Deputy Student Liaison to the College Park City Council
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember (District 4)
48774.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is urging state legislators to take action against companies like RealPage that offer price-setting features based on algorithms, which can lead to high rent prices. As Amira Abujuma, Deputy Student Liaison to the College Park City Council, stated, 'Housing in College Park is not a luxury. It’s a necessity. Access to affordable housing is essential, and RealPage is a threat to that.' The council agreed to send a letter to state legislators supporting possible legislative action against RealPage and other similar companies, and will discuss potential further action at a future meeting.

key_people: Charkera Ervin, African American Redress Network Legal and Policy Director
Keith Webster, College Park Restorative Justice Commission Member
Kayla Edwards-Scott, Contributing Researcher
organizations: Braxton Institute for Sustainability, Resilience and Joy
48751.ai_summary: Restorative justice advocates presented a report to the College Park City Council urging the city to help the Lakeland community improve education, transportation, and housing. The report, compiled by the African American Redress Network and the Braxton Institute for Sustainability, Resilience and Joy, details the history and impact of urban renewal on Lakeland, which demolished 104 of the neighborhood's 150 homes. As Keith Webster, a commission member and descendant of one of Lakeland's original property owners, said, 'Let's resurrect the spirit of Lakeland, the Lakeland that we once knew.'

key_people: Jim Miller, College Park Parking Enforcement Manager

John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember District 3

Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
48748.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering stricter parking enforcement, including hiring more enforcement officers, reducing the number of free permits per household, and charging residents to park on their streets. Councilmember Maria Mackie noted that 'nobody likes permit parking', but 'we need to realize this is a quality-of-life issue. It's a safety issue.' The council is weighing these options to address growing parking shortages in some neighborhoods, with Councilmember Jacob Hernandez stating he is 'vehemently against' charging taxpayers to park in front of their homes.

key_people: Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
48745.ai_summary: Three College Park City Council members, Llatetra Brown Esters, Susan Whitney, and John Rigg, have announced they will not seek re-election in November. According to Rigg, he wants to 'place a much higher priority on … family events, see my friends and neighbors more often and spend time on personal pursuits.' Mayor Fazlul Kabir noted that their departure will be a loss for the council, but also provides an opportunity for new candidates, saying 'If the same people keep running, those who are interested, they don’t get a chance. It’s very hard to run against incumbents.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor

48698.ai_summary: The city of College Park hosted a National Night Out event on August 5, where residents met with law enforcement officials from the University of Maryland Police Department, Metro Transit Police Department, and Prince George’s County Police Department. As College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'The whole goal of National Night Out is to get the community connected with the law enforcement who try to keep us safe.' The event aimed to build partnerships and engage with community members, and it attracted a diverse crowd of dozens, including residents who appreciate the chance to build stronger bonds with their community, like Ginger Whitaker, who said, 'It just makes you feel safer to know who’s actually working down there at the fire station, or who’s sitting in the police car around the corner.'

key_people: Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
48651.ai_summary: Both College Park City Councilmembers representing District 2, Llatetra Brown Esters and Susan Whitney, announced they will not run for re-election in November, citing increasing demands on their time from their full-time jobs and families. Esters stated, 'My need to be present for family has become a priority,' while Whitney wrote, 'My work and family responsibilities have increased substantially since the last election, and I no longer have the time and energy to provide the level of service District 2 residents deserve.' This decision, along with Councilmember John Rigg's similar announcement for District 3, will impact the upcoming City Council elections in November.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Stuart Adams, former College Park City Councilmember
Mike Meadows, College Park resident
Gannon Sprinkle, University of Maryland student
48611.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember John Rigg announced he will not seek re-election in November, citing a desire to prioritize family and personal pursuits. Rigg stated 'I want to place a much higher priority on … family events, see my friends and neighbors more often and spend time on personal pursuits.' He endorsed his colleague Ray Ranker for re-election and urged the newly elected council to consider switching to four-year terms for elected officials, which he believes would 'continue a common-sense approach to alleviating the substantial personal burden imposed by our current system of elections every other year.'

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Armando Camacho, Prince George’s County Board of License Commissioners Vice Chair
Joshua Patel, College Park Liquors Owner
Rajkumar Patel, College Park Liquors Owner
Daniel Oates, Calvert Hills Citizens Association President
organizations: Prince George’s County Board of License Commissioners
locations: University of Maryland Child Development Center
Calvert Hills Playground and Athletic Field
48606.ai_summary: The Prince George’s County Board of License Commissioners denied a liquor license application for College Park Liquors to reopen on Baltimore Avenue, citing the store’s proposed location near the University of Maryland Child Development Center. College Park City Councilmember John Rigg stated, “This is unequivocally the wrong location, and the city council stands in unified opposition to it.” Mayor Fazlul Kabir testified that the community has voiced strong opposition to the introduction of a full-service liquor store close to the Calvert Hills playground and the University of Maryland Child Development Center, saying “This is a wonderful community, a historic community that’s very well established, very family-oriented.”

key_people: Bob Catlin, Former College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
48293.ai_summary: Former College Park City Councilmember Bob Catlin remains active in civic life, attending council meetings to share historical tidbits and serving on committees such as the Vacant and Blighted Properties Committee. Catlin said, 'I've always been interested in history and policies and why things work the way they do,' and 'I think you need to understand that in order to make your decisions in the future.' He is also co-president of the Berwyn District Civic Association and serves on the College Park Housing Authority, demonstrating his continued commitment to College Park governance and community engagement.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Ryna Quiñones, College Park Communications and Events Manager
Julie Johnson, University of Maryland Assistant Director for Intramural Sports and the Reckord Armory
Cat Peretti, University Park Resident and Volleyball Team Captain
key_issues: residents' access to University of Maryland facilities
48292.ai_summary: The City of College Park has allocated $30,000 to offer 50% discounts to residents who wish to play social sports through the University of Maryland's RecWell program. According to College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, 'Anything that gets people out and moving their bodies is a good thing... Team sports are a great way of building community.' The program focuses on team-based community building and social interaction, and City Communications and Events Manager Ryna Quiñones said, 'We're happy to help reduce the barriers, to encourage our residents to not only meet their neighbors, but kind of engage in these fun and active activities.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Donald Hay, Co-chair of the College Park Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: College Park Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee
48291.ai_summary: The College Park City Council will hold a public hearing on a proposal to make it a violation for cars to park or stand in bike lanes, addressing complaints from bicyclists about cars and delivery trucks blocking bike lanes. As Councilmember Susan Whitney said, 'College Park is about five square miles, if we can't do this here, why can't we do this here? I see no reason why we can't accomplish that in College Park.' The proposed change would mean that drivers of delivery and supply trucks will not be allowed to stop in a bike lane to unload on city-owned streets, although it would not affect Route 1, which is owned and maintained by the state.

key_people: Eduoard Haba, College Park City Councilmember
Danny Schaible, College Park City Councilmember
Sam Denes, College Park City Councilmember
Tracey Douglas, City Administrator of College Park
Robert Croslin, College Park Mayor
Sean Carlson, Ward 1 resident
48237.ai_summary: The College Park city council voted 7-1 to enter into a sales agreement to purchase a 4.5-acre parking lot next to Driskell Park for $6.5 million. The property, known as the lower parcel, has been a subject of debate since 2017 due to its location in the Northwest Branch flood plain and the need for environmental permits. As Ward 1 resident Sean Carlson stated, 'I find it galling that we would consider bailing out the developers who have not shown that they are capable of being good stewards of that land,' highlighting concerns about the city rewarding Werrlein Properties for misusing the property.

key_people: Sharon O'Malley, College Park Here & Now Managing Editor
Mark Goodson, College Park Here & Now Former Managing Editor
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Chris Currie, Streetcar Suburbs Publishing Vice President
48233.ai_summary: College Park Here & Now, a monthly newspaper, is celebrating its fifth anniversary, with Managing Editor Sharon O'Malley saying 'I think that local news, local journalism, is more important now than ever.' The publication's success is attributed to its hyperlocal focus and nonprofit business model, with University of Maryland students contributing as volunteer reporters. According to former Managing Editor Mark Goodson, 'It's [the community] that deserves the credit for [the newspaper's] being what it is because it's a very unique place where a print newspaper could launch during a pandemic.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Wes Moore, Maryland Governor
Kristi Noem, DHS Secretary
48229.ai_summary: The U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS) named College Park and Hyattsville as 'sanctuary jurisdictions' that are 'obstructing the enforcement of U.S. immigration laws.' College Park officials have stated that 'College Park has and will continue to be a welcoming and inclusive community' and that 'The City of College Park complies with all laws and treats all residents justly and equally.' The city has not received any formal notification from DHS about its inclusion on the list, which was later removed from the DHS website.

key_people: Maria Cordone, Yarrow Resident
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Nicole Freeh, Highway Engineer and Yarrow Resident
47367.ai_summary: Residents of the Yarrow neighborhood, including Maria Cordone, are asking the city to plant noise-absorbing trees between Edmonston Road and Kenilworth Avenue to replace the ones removed for a sidewalk. Cordone said, 'I can’t hear my neighbor talking to me,' and 'I can’t even sit out in the yard because of the noise.' The residents plan to meet with the city arborist to discuss tree planting and may also ask the state to conduct a sound study to determine the best solution to reduce the noise, which is loudest between 7 p.m. and 11 p.m.

key_people: Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Director
Kathryn Clay, PNC Senior Vice President
Qin Chih, Jumbo Jumbo Cafe Manager
Keith Bouchelion, Laser Essential Co-owner
47353.ai_summary: The City of College Park awarded $75,000 in grants to 15 local businesses through the Retail Reinforcement Mini-Grant Program, funded by PNC Bank, to help offset losses from the ongoing Purple Line construction. According to Michael Williams, the city's economic development director, 'The money flew out the door' once the grant was announced, and 'It didn’t take long' for the funds to run out.
