Businesses such as Jumbo Jumbo Cafe and Laser Essential have experienced significant losses due to reduced foot and vehicle traffic, with Jumbo Jumbo Cafe's manager Qin Chih stating 'It’s very harmful' and 'It’s really hard for us', but the grant has been 'helpful' in covering rent costs.

key_people: Ethan Pham, CP Flea Organizer
Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
Bill Gardiner, College Park Assistant City Manager
Gabrielle Brinkhoff, Shop Made in Maryland Store Manager
47336.ai_summary: The City of College Park shut down a flea market on City Hall plaza in March after it had operated for nearly a year without permission. According to Kenny Young, College Park City Manager, 'From the city’s point of view, it’s a pretty simple issue for us: They were operating on the City Hall plaza without permission.' The market's organizer, Ethan Pham, had been working with Shop Made in Maryland to host the market and was unaware of any issues until the city evicted it, saying 'It just felt completely out of left field, like, I had no idea this was coming.'

key_people: Maxine Gross, Lakeland Community Heritage Project Chair
Mary Anne Hakes, Explorations on Aging Organizer
47289.ai_summary: University of Maryland students have been working with the Lakeland Community Heritage Project to preserve the history of College Park's Lakeland community. According to Maxine Gross, chair of the heritage project, 'It is helpful for us to see what [the students are] thinking about, and … it’s helpful for them to see how the things that they learned about in a classroom actually work with real-life projects.' The students have also collaborated with Explorations on Aging to create a website for senior citizens, with Mary Anne Hakes noting that 'The intergenerational experience, I think, for both of us, was just wonderful.'

47278.ai_summary: The College Park City Council will hold a public hearing on May 6 to discuss the city's proposed $29.6 million fiscal year 2026 budget, which would increase the budget by 0.9% over the previous year. The proposal includes adding three full-time staff members to City Hall but does not raise residential property taxes, which increased by 11% last year. The council must adopt a budget resolution later this month and may hold a work session on May 13 to discuss possible changes to the proposed budget.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
47284.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted 5-3 to lower the voting age for city elections from 18 to 16, starting with the November election. Councilmember John Rigg championed the measure, saying it is designed to 'instill a sense of civic duty in teenagers' and boost the number of residents who vote in local elections. Councilmembers Denise Mitchell, Maria Mackie, and Alan Hew opposed the change, with Mackie suggesting a youth advisory council and Hew proposing a resident vote on the issue.

key_people: Dhruvak Mirani, University of Maryland Student Government Association President
Dhruvak Mirani, University System of Maryland Board of Regents student member
47268.ai_summary: Dhruvak Mirani, the 2023-24 University of Maryland student liaison to the College Park City Council, has been elected as the president of the school's Student Government Association. Mirani, a government and politics major, also serves as a student member of the University System of Maryland's Board of Regents. He previously helped create a pilot project for the city to give subsidies to student renters, and his new role may impact future initiatives related to College Park governance and student issues.

key_people: Debbie Herbert, College Park Moose Family Center Lodge President
Robert Lucian, College Park Moose Family Center Lodge Junior Past President
Denise Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: College Park Moose Family Center Lodge
Iglesia de Dios Pentecostés, Misión el Buen Samaritano
Mekane Hiwot Medhane Alem Tigray Orthodox Tewahedo Church
47339.ai_summary: The College Park Moose Family Center Lodge is preparing to relocate after 75 years due to financial difficulties and the sale of their building. 'I hate it because we wanted to get back our building,' said Debbie Herbert, the lodge's president. The lodge is working with the College Park City Council to find a new location, and Robert Lucian, the junior past president, has appealed to the council for help, saying 'I'm hoping that sometime very soon, we will find a place to go to and reorganize and keep our members having a place to go to.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Ryna Quiñones, College Park Communications and Events Manager
47186.ai_summary: The 27th annual Maryland Day at the University of Maryland drew thousands of people, where College Park officials and staff participated by handing out information about the city. 'It's building community, bringing people together in one place and showcasing how strong we are as a community,' said College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir. The event featured activities such as a book giveaway, trivia game, and button-making, and city staff attended to share information with current and prospective students and their families.

key_people: Daniel Gruner, University of Maryland Professor of Entomology
Karin Burghardt, University of Maryland Associate Professor of Entomology and Extension Specialist
Laura Rost, Bee City USA National Coordinator
46919.ai_summary: The City of College Park has waived its 12-inch limit for lawn height in April to support No Mow April, allowing native pollinators like bees and butterflies to feed and find habitats. As University of Maryland Professor of Entomology Daniel Gruner said, 'Pollinators need floral resources in order to get the energy they need to build their broods up, especially early in the season.' By not mowing lawns, residents can make floral resources more available for pollinators, which is crucial for their survival, according to Bee City USA National Coordinator Laura Rost, who noted that 'the best thing we can do to help bees is to plant native plants … and native shrubs and native trees that bloom.'

key_people: Thomas Dernoga, Prince George’s County Councilmember (District 1)
Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember (District 3)
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember (District 2)
Wes Moore, Maryland Governor
organizations: Meals on Wheels of College Park
46928.ai_summary: Prince George’s County Councilmembers Thomas Dernoga and Eric Olson warned that the coming budget cycle will be “extremely difficult” due to uncertainty surrounding federal grants and budget cuts. As Olson noted, “The federal government is doing so much damage,” and “Our president is trying to eliminate the Department of Education, where a lot of those grants originate from.” College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated that the city is not proposing a property tax increase in its upcoming budget, and will add four full-time staff members, including a public safety manager, with a draft budget proposal totaling $29.6 million.

key_people: Ryna Quiñones, College Park Communications and Events Manager
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Robert Thurston, Lakeland Civic Association President
Daniel Oates, Calvert Hills Citizens Association President
46925.ai_summary: The City of College Park has started a text alert system to keep residents updated on events, news, and emergencies. According to Ryna Quiñones, College Park Communications and Events Manager, 'We're always looking for new ways to reach our residents,' and the text alert system will help the city communicate with residents more effectively. As Daniel Oates, Calvert Hills Citizens Association President, noted, 'There's right now some people who are affiliated at the university getting text alerts about, if there's a burglary, or, you know, 'Hey, stay away from this area because of XYZ thing,' but there's [previously] not a way that I'm aware of for the city to do the same kind of communication by text.'

key_people: Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Hua Wang, Owner and Chef of Li Chun Café and Northwest Chinese Food
Xue Ling, Brand Manager of Li Chun Café and Daughter of Hua Wang
46916.ai_summary: The Li Chun Café, owned by Hua Wang, has opened in College Park City Hall, with a ribbon-cutting ceremony attended by Councilmember Maria Mackie and other community members. Mackie stated, 'It’s really a … wonderful addition and a really unique place,' and expressed excitement for the café. The café features traditional northeastern Chinese dishes, including chun bing wraps, and has been well-received by customers, with Ling saying, 'They like the vibes, and they like being here to enjoy healthy food and teas in between work and studies.'

key_people: Fidel Alfaro, Owner of Alfaro Barbershop
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
46869.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember Jacob Hernandez has been helping Spanish-speaking business owners, including Fidel Alfaro of Alfaro Barbershop, by providing them with information about the city in Spanish. Hernandez, who has served on the council since 2023, supported the city's hiring of a bilingual staff member to translate city documents into Spanish. As Alfaro said, 'He always had time to get here,' and with Hernandez's guidance, Alfaro's shop received $14,000 in economic development grants for renovations.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Jatinder Khokhar, College Park Director of Public Services
46866.ai_summary: College Park's city manager has proposed a $29.6 million budget for fiscal year 2026, which would not raise property taxes and would add three full-time staff members to city hall. The proposal includes an 8.5% increase over last year for employee salaries and benefits. As Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated, 'Increasing property taxes has never been popular,' and this year, 'thankfully, it's not happening.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Director
Glenn Ivey, U.S. Representative for District 4
John Rigg, College Park Councilmember for District 3
46822.ai_summary: City officials in College Park are preparing for the economic fallout from recent layoffs of federal employees, with approximately 1,100 federal workers living in the city. 'We are not really prepared for it,' said Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor, as rising unemployment could reduce customer traffic at local businesses. The city is assessing the potential impact of job and funding cuts as they consider the city's fiscal year 2026 budget, with Councilmember John Rigg stating 'It's going to be a really tough time over the next couple of years in College Park'.

key_people: Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Stuart Adams, former College Park City Councilmember
Michael Meadow, College Park resident
Gannon Sprinkle, University of Maryland student
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Karin Brown, Ray Ranker's wife
Cindy Ranker, Ray Ranker's mother
46529.ai_summary: Ray Ranker, a University of Maryland chaplain, took the oath of office as the new District 3 College Park City Councilmember after winning the special election with 344 votes. Ranker stated, 'There are just a lot of amazing people who are, in so many different ways, making our community stronger, more connected, better. I'm excited to build on that.' He plans to dive into city budget planning and reach out to his opponents, Michael Meadow and Gannon Sprinkle, to help move College Park forward together, saying 'I can really advocate for the kinds of changes that folks in [District 3] want to see reflected in the annual budget that's coming up.'

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
46499.ai_summary: College Park officials and community members gathered at Duvall Field for a groundbreaking ceremony to kickstart the renovation of the field into a seven-acre park for sports, entertainment and community events. The project, which includes over a dozen amenities such as an amphitheater, pickleball and basketball courts, and rugby and soccer fields, is expected to finish by 2026. As Councilmember Jacob Hernandez said, 'I picture a place where seniors can gracefully age in place and just enjoy the largest park in the city and the fresh air that it’ll bring,' and 'Duvall Field will be a welcoming place for all.'

key_people: Matt Hurson, Cape Advisers
Michael Harris, University of Maryland Student
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember (District 2)
46411.ai_summary: The 60-year-old University Club at College Park on Berwyn House Road will undergo a major renovation starting this summer, which will include upgrades to the water and electrical systems, higher-end furnishings, and outdoor amenities. Matt Hurson of Cape Advisers said, 'This property was of interest to us because of its distress pricing, but the fact that it is a property that needs a lot of work.' City Councilmember Susan Whitney expressed concern about the potential rent increase, stating, 'There is a problem with a lack of affordable housing in general in the city, but also specifically for students.'

key_people: Ray Ranker, University of Maryland Chaplain and College Park City Councilmember
Michael Meadow, Old Town Resident
Gannon Sprinkle, University of Maryland Student
Stuart Adams, Former District 3 Councilmember
46379.ai_summary: Ray Ranker, a University of Maryland chaplain, won the special election for the District 3 College Park City Council seat with 338 out of 504 votes. Ranker stated, 'I’m grateful that people entrusted me with this responsibility' and plans to 'build on' the community's strengths. He will take the oath of office on March 18 and expects to dive into city budget planning, aiming to 'help reflect our city’s values and hopes through how we spend our money'.

key_people: Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Hua Wang, Owner and Chef of Northwest Chinese Food and Li Chun Café
Xue Ling, Brand Manager of Li Chun Café and daughter of Hua Wang
46382.ai_summary: Li Chun Café, owned by Hua Wang, had its grand opening on March 8 with a ribbon-cutting ceremony attended by College Park City Councilmember Maria Mackie and other community members. Mackie said of the café, “It’s really a … wonderful addition and a really unique place,” and she decided to attend the opening because of how proud she is of the café. The café features dishes from northeastern China, including chun bing wraps, and has been popular with customers who like the vibes and the healthy food and teas.

key_people: Denise Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Anna Owens, Former College Park Mayor
P.J. Brennan, Former College Park Councilmember
46437.ai_summary: Female College Park City Councilmembers Denise Mitchell, Maria Mackie, Susan Whitney, and Llatetra Brown Esters reflect on their political journeys, noting that women often work extra hard to prove their competence in a male-dominated field. As Mitchell stated, 'This is still a man's world in regard to politics. A man's, man's world.' The women agree that there is room for more female leaders in government, with Mackie saying, 'I think that women are really coming up to the plate.' Their experiences highlight the importance of increasing female representation in College Park governance.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
key_issues: visibility of tables and surfaces for beer pong
storage of trash and recycling carts
46342.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has scheduled a public hearing for April 1 on a proposed amendment to the city code that will forbid residents from keeping tables used for drinking games in their front and side yards. As Councilmember John Rigg said, 'One thing we’ve always struggled with, one very specific piece of furniture, and that is a beer pong table,' and 'People really don’t appreciate drinking games next door … on their street when they are trying to encourage their children to have healthier approaches to alcohol than is exhibited by such drinking games.' Councilmember Jacob Hernandez questioned the proposed change, stating it 'is a targeted policy to curb [the] behavior of a particular group of people.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
Maria Mackie, College Park Councilmember
Eric Olson, County Councilmember
Mary Anne Hakes, College Park Resident and Explorations on Aging Project Worker
46348.ai_summary: The College Park Woods Clubhouse has opened to the public after a year of construction, with a ribbon-cutting ceremony attended by College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir, Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell, and other local officials. As Kabir said, 'This is your second home. Please enjoy it.' The new community space is expected to bring a stronger sense of community to College Park, with resident Mary Anne Hakes hoping it will 'unite us as a city' and Mitchell stating that 'having this is important, because engagement means inclusivity.'

key_people: Sherry Felix, High Point High School English Teacher and Title I Coordinator
46388.ai_summary: The College Park City Council granted $2,750 to High Point High School's Flying from the HP Nest program, which provides care packages for college-bound students, including blankets, bed linens, and Chromebooks. The program aims to support students, particularly those from low-income backgrounds, in their transition to post-high school education. As Sherry Felix, the school's English teacher and Title I coordinator, said, 'this is just a small treat,' and she hopes the program will expand with community support to help more students graduate and pursue higher education, addressing the school's low graduation rate partly caused by socioeconomic needs, as Felix noted, 'We know that there’s an economic need, there’s a socioeconomic need for our students.'

key_people: Erica Otte, College Park City Council Non-Voting Student Liaison
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember, District 3
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember, District 1
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember, District 2
Anna-Kaye McDonald, College Park Deputy Student Liaison
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem, District 4
Chris Hatcher, Attorney for Landmark Properties
Hamilton Reynolds, Landmark Representative
46331.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voiced objections to a plan by Landmark Properties to demolish the College Park Towers and replace it with luxury high-rise apartment buildings, citing concerns over the lack of affordable housing in the area. Councilmembers, including Erica Otte, John Rigg, Alan Hew, and Llatetra Brown Esters, argued that the new development should include a percentage of affordable housing units, with Otte stating 'Luxury apartments are replacing affordable housing,' and Hew saying 'One thing I will always ask is that there be a percentage of affordable housing built into this.' The council's comments came during a worksession where Landmark Properties was seeking rezoning for the property at 4330 Hartwick Road.

46336.ai_summary: A special election will be held on March 11 to fill the vacant District 3 College Park City Council seat temporarily. The city council has approved the purchase of three drop boxes for voting at a cost of $9,481.25. Voters will also select a full council, including a mayor and all eight councilmembers, during a regular city election on November 4.

key_people: Joel Carlson, Volunteer
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Ellen Tappin, Fight Colorectal Cancer Member
46287.ai_summary: The City of College Park partnered with Fight Colorectal Cancer to host an event on March 9, educating residents about the dangers of colorectal cancer and the importance of getting screened. As Councilmember Alan Hew said, 'Something about health and personal issues like this aren’t very well versed in minority communities, so we’re trying to build opportunities for them to learn about it, speak about it, without social pressures and things like that.' Ellen Tappin, a colon cancer survivor and Fight Colorectal Cancer member, noted that 'I’m hoping that people coming into the event today will become aware that colorectal cancer is the No. 2 cause of cancer deaths in the nation.'

key_people: Michael Meadow, College Park City Council Candidate
Ray Ranker, College Park City Council Candidate
Gannon Sprinkle, College Park City Council Candidate
Stuart Adams, Former College Park City Councilmember
Shawn Anderson, WTOP Anchor
Donald Trump, President of the United States
46182.ai_summary: The three candidates for the vacant District 3 City Council seat, Michael Meadow, Ray Ranker, and Gannon Sprinkle, discussed their plans to support federal workers who have lost their jobs, with Sprinkle stating 'I believe that we need a strong safety net, and there have to be city programs in place to both help people find new employment and to stop as much of this bleeding that we can.' The candidates also agreed to oppose any effort to dismantle the city's diversity, equity and inclusion programs, with Meadow saying 'Absolutely do not scrap' DEI programs, and Ranker stating he would 'like to have it both ways: continue doing [DEI] while still retaining federal dollars and support.' The special election to replace former councilmember Stuart Adams is scheduled for March 11.

key_people: Mike Meadow, College Park City Council Candidate
Ray Ranker, College Park City Council Candidate
Gannon Sprinkle, College Park City Council Candidate
Shawn Anderson, WTOP Radio Moderator
46022.ai_summary: The College Park Here & Now will host a forum for the three candidates for the vacant District 3 City Council seat on Wednesday, Feb. 26. The forum will feature Mike Meadow of Old Town, Ray Ranker of College Park Estates, and Gannon Sprinkle, who lives near the University of Maryland, and will be moderated by Shawn Anderson of WTOP radio. The event will take place at City Hall in the City Council Chambers and attendees can join in person or via Zoom.

key_people: Wes Moore, Maryland Governor
Jim Rosapepe, Maryland State Senator, District 21
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Ben Barnes, Maryland Delegate, District 21
Joseline Peña-Melnyk, Maryland Delegate, District 21
45972.ai_summary: Maryland Gov. Wes Moore's proposed $67.3 billion budget for fiscal year 2026 includes funding to start planning for improvements to Route 1 from Greenbelt Road to the Beltway. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir agreed that 'Route 1 is our Main Street, plain and simple,' and state Sen. Jim Rosapepe noted that 'Route 1 is the front door to the University of Maryland; it’s our flagship university and we want people, when they come, to feel good about the community.' The project aims to improve the safety and appearance of the highway, with updates including dedicated left-turn lanes, bike lanes, and landscaped medians.

key_people: Gannon Sprinkle, University of Maryland Student Government Association Executive Vice President
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Michael Meadow, College Park Engineer
Ray Ranker, University of Maryland Lutheran Chaplain
Eric Olson, County Councilmember, District 3
Adrian Boafo, Maryland Delegate, District 23
organizations: University of Maryland Student Government Association
45947.ai_summary: Gannon Sprinkle, a University of Maryland junior, is running for the District 3 College Park City Council seat, aiming to 'bridge the gap' between student residents and long-time residents. Sprinkle, who is also the executive vice president of UMD's Student Government Association, believes that 'students deserve a voting member of the city council' and plans to focus on issues such as affordable housing, food equity, and bicycle and pedestrian safety. If elected, Sprinkle would become the first undergraduate student to serve as a councilmember, and he has stated that he 'wish[es] to bring that experience and representation to the city council' and is 'very dedicated to spending the next years of my life to what the College Park residents would like to see out of their city council'.

key_people: Michael Jacobs, Former College Park Mayor
Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember
Andrew Fellows, Former College Park Mayor
Kathy Bryant, College Park resident
organizations: National Cyber Security Hall of Fame
45962.ai_summary: Former College Park Mayor Michael Jacobs, who served for two dozen years, has died at the age of 81. According to Prince George’s County Councilmember Eric Olson, Jacobs was a dedicated public servant who balanced his demanding career at the National Security Agency with his duties as a city official. As mayor, Jacobs played a large part in establishing relations with the University of Maryland, and was also one of the leaders of the Quality of Life Committee, focused on updating city codes like noise ordinances, with residents like Kathy Bryant noting that 'He was never too busy to talk to me, or I’m sure, to anybody else who wanted to talk to him.'

key_people: Stefan Leggin, Brentwood Vice Mayor
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Tiffany Green, Prince George's County Fire Chief
organizations: Coalition to Support Prince George’s County Fire and EMS
45951.ai_summary: The College Park City Council agreed to sign a letter to Prince George's County executive candidates asking for funding to hire 150 new firefighters and emergency medical service staff. The Coalition to Support Prince George's County Fire and EMS, which includes government and fire officials, drafted the letter to address staffing concerns and increase transparency. As Stefan Leggin, former Brentwood Vice Mayor, stated, 'We can use this election as leverage to elevate the need for meaningful change, to elevate the conversation' and prioritize staffing for fire departments.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Donald Trump, President of the United States
organizations: Food and Drug Administration’s Center for Food Safety and Applied Nutrition
45903.ai_summary: College Park residents, including federal employees and immigrants, are feeling the impact of President Donald Trump's executive orders, with one resident stating she has been 'shaking like a leaf every day'. The city's mayor, Fazlul Kabir, acknowledged the low morale in the community, saying 'there are so many jobs of federal employees at stake', and announced the city's commitment to diversity, equity, and inclusion through a proclamation called 'One College Park'. The city has also published links to resources for affected residents, including those from Prince George’s County Public Schools and Care Solace.

45978.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has postponed voting on a proposed charter amendment to lower the voting age for city elections to 16, giving residents another month to weigh in. A public hearing was held where a couple of residents spoke against the change, while representatives of Vote16 spoke in favor of it. The council also postponed a vote on a second proposed charter amendment that would allow the mayor and council to appoint a replacement council member if a seat is vacated less than a year before the next election.

key_people: College Park Mayor, College Park City Council
45975.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is forming two citizen task forces, one for childcare and one for affordable housing. The Childcare Task Force will track state legislation and scout out federal, state, county and non-profit programs, and interview childcare providers for input on lowering costs and increasing access. The Affordable Housing Task Force will research the city's housing needs, define 'affordable housing', and propose a program to offer affordable housing to those who qualify.

key_people: Maxine Gross, Founding Chair of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember (District 2)
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember (District 2)
45563.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has approved a contract with Columbia University to develop a plan for reparations to the Lakeland community, which was harmed by urban renewal efforts in the 1970s and 1980s. The research team, including members from Columbia and the city's Restorative Justice Commission, will determine appropriate reparations regarding economic stability, history of access to education and health care, and other effects of losing the neighborhood and social community. As Councilmember Susan Whitney said, 'It is important to have a well-researched, well-documented, academically sound record of what happened in Lakeland and what parties were involved in that occurring, either by actions or inactions,' and Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters added that the city intends to fulfill its promise to commit to restorative justice.

key_people: Janeen Miller, Age Friendly College Park Program Lead
Chyna Diaz, Habitat for Humanity Metro Maryland Director of Home Preservation
45424.ai_summary: The College Park Aging in Place program, part of a nationwide effort by Habitat for Humanity, provides free home repairs and improvements to senior citizens in College Park, aiming to help them stay in their homes as they age. According to Janeen Miller, the program is a key component of College Park's efforts to better support its senior citizens, and the city hopes to be a destination for seniors retiring and looking for a place to age. The initiative has so far upgraded half a dozen homes in the city, with funding from the American Rescue Plan Act, and program coordinators plan to complete 200 projects a year over the next five years throughout Montgomery and Prince George's counties.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Robert Day, College Park City Councilmember
45574.ai_summary: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember for District 3, has resigned, citing the demands of the part-time council job and his full-time engineering work, and the arrival of his second child. As he said, 'I'd rather worry about Facetiming my kids.' The city will hold a special election to fill his seat on March 11. Adams' departure is seen as a loss by his colleagues, with Mayor Fazlul Kabir saying 'He's very smart, he has done quite a bit of work, even though it's been only three years now.'

key_people: Michael Meadow, College Park City Council Candidate
Ray Ranker, College Park City Council Candidate
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
45420.ai_summary: Two candidates, Michael Meadow and Ray Ranker, have announced they will run for the District 3 College Park City Council seat vacated by the resignation of Councilmember Stuart Adams. Meadow, a lifelong resident of College Park and University of Maryland graduate, said he would focus on helping the city 'grow and prosper, but at the same time I want to take care of the small business owners.' Ranker, also a University of Maryland graduate, said he wants to 'keep building on those pieces' to make the city 'livable, walkable, bikeable, a place that can offer support to students, to families, to seniors.'

key_people: Alan Bradford, College Park Volunteer
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
John Edward 'Jack' Perry, Former College Park Councilmember
Joseph Perry, Son of Jack Perry
Janet Bradford, Wife of Alan Bradford
Martin Klapac, College Park Resident
organizations: College Park Boys and Girls Club
45413.ai_summary: The College Park City Council presented Alan Bradford with the 2024 Jack Perry Award, recognizing his 42 years of service as a volunteer in College Park. 'Alan's countless acts of service … have fostered a spirit of community and improved the quality of life for residents, embodying the legacy of Jack Perry himself,' Mayor Fazlul Kabir wrote. Bradford's volunteer work includes coaching softball, managing the equipment for the College Park Boys and Girls Club, and serving on the College Park Recreation Board for 18 years.

key_people: Michelle Johnson, College Park Housing Authority Director
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
45409.ai_summary: The Attick Towers, a 108-unit apartment building for tenants ages 60 and up, is starting its third year of a renovation project that could last up to five more years. According to Michelle Johnson, College Park Housing Authority Director, 'The Housing Authority needs around $14 million right now,' and the city council has allocated $1.25 million in ARPA funds toward the renovations in 2022, with an additional $750,000 allocated on Sept. 24. As Councilmember Susan Whitney noted, 'It’s a building that’s, you know, about 50 years old, and there’s a lot of deferred maintenance,' highlighting the need for continued funding to complete the project.

key_people: Sharon O'Malley, No Specific Title
College Park City Councilmembers, College Park City Council
45598.ai_summary: The College Park City Council will hold a public hearing on a proposal to change the minimum legal voting age in College Park from 18 to 16. Councilmembers who have supported the amendment to the city charter have said that teenage voters tend to grow up to be civically engaged adults. If the amendment passes, College Park residents who register to vote in Prince George’s County and will be at least 16 on Election Day will automatically become registered to vote in the city.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Robert Day, College Park Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park Councilmember
45174.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember Stuart Adams announced his resignation, effective January 8, citing family commitments as the reason. As a young father, Adams, 38, stated 'my availability as a city councilmember has become more constrained' and asked the mayor, councilmembers, and residents to 'continue to push' projects he has championed, including the Calvert Hills stormwater drainage project and affordable childcare. Adams' replacement will be determined through a special election within 65 days, to hold the seat until the next general election in November.

key_people: Maj. James Keleti, Hyattsville Division 1
David Mitchell, University of Maryland Police Chief
Lt. Rosanne Hoaas, University of Maryland Police Department
Deputy Chief Stephen Boehm, Metro Transit Police
Assistant Chief Yasmin Brown, Maryland-National Capital Park Police
45050.ai_summary: The University of Maryland Police Department has stopped numerous e-scooters on campus for traffic violations, including failure to stop at stop signs and riding on sidewalks. According to Lt. Rosanne Hoaas, 'The reality is, oftentimes, they don’t realize that they need to be in the road and that they need to follow the rules of the road, like in a car, truck or SUV.' Police officers are challenged in stopping scooters due to safety concerns, with Maj. James Keleti stating, 'If they decide not to pull over, we have to terminate that vehicle stop.' The issue of e-scooter safety and enforcement is a concern for College Park governance, particularly with the rise in scooter-related crimes on campus.

key_people: Lisa Eally, Meals on Wheels of College Park Chair
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: Meals on Wheels of College Park
45028.ai_summary: Meals on Wheels of College Park will not be returning to the city anytime soon due to lack of suitable space. The organization's chair, Lisa Eally, said 'We’re doing whatever we can to find a location back in College Park,' but they were unable to come to an agreement with the developer of the Flats at College Park. The College Park City Council had allocated $800,000 to support the organization's move, but reallocated the funds after the deal fell through, and instead set aside $400,000 for Meals on Wheels to use if it moves during fiscal year 2025.

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
Rick Hoehn, contractor
45025.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted unanimously to spend $7,354,000 to renovate Duvall Field, a project that has been delayed for 19 years due to funding issues. Councilmembers discussed the high cost of the project, with Councilmember Alan Hew saying 'Making greatness in our city costs, and the longer we wait, the more it’s going to cost.' The renovation plans include adding soccer and baseball fields, an event amphitheater, and other amenities, and Councilmember Susan Whitney compared the potential benefits of the project to the community-gathering space created by the new city hall, saying 'I think the same thing will happen' with the renovated Duvall Field.

key_people: Jacob Hernandez, College Park Councilmember
Michael Williams, College Park Director of Economic Development
Kahyla Hernandez, College Park resident
Mona Lynch, College Park resident
organizations: College Park Department of Economic Development
44853.ai_summary: A Ross Dress For Less discount store opened in North College Park on October 12, filling the vacancy left by Best Buy in the College Park Marketplace Shopping Center. According to College Park Councilmember Jacob Hernandez, 'If you see across the city, we don’t have any major clothing store options,' and the new Ross store will provide 'high quality clothing and merchandise for low cost and affordable prices.' The store is expected to be a major economic driver, hiring 70 to 110 new employees, and College Park resident Mona Lynch said having a Ross in the city was 'in order' to meet community needs.

key_people: Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
Tanya White, North College Park Postmaster
Keith Hooks, Local Representative of the National Association of Letter Carriers
44850.ai_summary: North College Park residents are complaining about mail delivery issues, with some not receiving mail for several days and others getting mail for the wrong address. According to Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem, 'There's maybe two or three days that a resident will not get mail at all,' and 'There's some days where the mail will come, but it will be for another address.' Keith Hooks, a local representative of the National Association of Letter Carriers, attributed the issues to an 'onslaught' of new, inexperienced employees following a rash of retirements of long-time mail carriers, and noted that the union wants to shift its perspective to prioritize the community and customer base.

key_people: Yasmin Brown, Assistant Chief of the Maryland-National Capital Park Police
Lt. Brian Wiesemann, Commander of the Maryland State Police College Park Barracks
David Mitchell, UMD Police Chief
Maj. Jim Keleti, Commander of the Prince George’s County Police Hyattsville Division 1
Stephen Boehm, Deputy Chief of the Metro Transit Police
44814.ai_summary: Theft is the most common crime in College Park, with thefts from automobiles and thefts of automobiles being the most prevalent, according to law enforcement officials. As Yasmin Brown, Assistant Chief of the Maryland-National Capital Park Police, noted, 'Most of our incidents are theft-related,' and 'a lot of that is a crime of opportunity: people leaving something in their car or leaving their car unlocked.' Overall, crime in College Park is down by 11%, despite a 6% increase in violent crime, and police departments are implementing new strategies, such as a 'bike bait' program, to combat theft.

key_people: Stephanie Stullich, Former College Park City Councilmember
Bob Schnabel, Husband of Stephanie Stullich
Rebecca Bailey, College Park Animal Welfare Officer
Patricia Coil, Volunteer with Beltsville Community Cats
Emily Wilkening, Daughter of Patricia Coil
Marina Augoustidis, College Park Resident
44810.ai_summary: Stephanie Stullich, a former College Park city councilmember, has been fostering stray cats and finding them forever homes for four years, with the help of the city's animal welfare officer and the nonprofit Beltsville Community Cats. Stullich has had up to 19 cats in her home at a time and has found homes for 120 cats. As Patricia Coil, a volunteer with Beltsville Community Cats, noted, 'You bring them in, and you never know what you're going to get, They each develop their own personality. It's just cute to watch them … become cats over the course of, like, two or three months.'

key_people: Jim Rosapepe, Maryland Senator (District 21)
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
William Pines, Maryland State Highway Administrator
Charlie Gischlar, State Highway Administration Deputy Director
44790.ai_summary: Maryland Sen. Jim Rosapepe called for swift planning and completion of the U.S. Route 1 Improvement Project during a ribbon-cutting ceremony celebrating the end of Phase 1 in College Park. 'We're hoping that we can get the planning [for the rest of the project] in this coming year's budget,' Rosapepe said, as the first phase, which cost $56.9 million, added bicycle lanes, raised mediums, expanded lanes, and upgraded drainage. The project's completion is expected to improve road safety, and the State Highway Administration remains committed to safety improvements, with plans to pursue grant funding through the US DOT RAISE program to support future costs.

key_people: Joel Biermann, Former University Park Mayor
Patrick Wojahn, Former College Park Mayor
Debi Sandlin, University Park Town Administrator
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Aisha Braveboy, Prince George’s County State’s Attorney
44763.ai_summary: Former University Park Mayor Joel Biermann was arrested on October 28 and charged with 28 counts relating to child sexual abuse materials, including possession and distribution of child pornography. He was denied bond and is being held in custody by the corrections department. The arrest comes about 20 months after former College Park Mayor Patrick Wojahn was arrested on similar charges, highlighting the need for continued vigilance and cooperation between law enforcement agencies in addressing these serious issues.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Hua Wang, Owner of Northwest Chinese Food
Xue Ling, Brand Manager of Northwest Chinese Food
44760.ai_summary: Northwest Chinese Food was named College Park's 2024 Business of the Year by Mayor Fazlul Kabir, who stated 'Their efforts have made College Park a more vibrant and diverse place to live.' The restaurant won the award due to its high reviews and commitment to engaging with and investing in the community. The restaurant will eventually close its current location to make room for new student apartments and will move to the city hall building, where it will reopen as Li Chun Cafe in February.

key_people: Kaelan Wilfred, Volunteer
Josanne Francis, University of Maryland Lead Community Engagement Program Coordinator
Trevor Muñoz, University of Maryland Maryland Institute for Technology in the Humanities Director
Xiomara Bradden, University of Maryland Junior Government and Politics Major
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember District 1
Noelle Showalter, College Park Resident
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission
44704.ai_summary: Hundreds of volunteers, including College Park residents and University of Maryland students, participated in the 13th annual Good Neighbor Day, working on projects such as cleaning up Lake Artemesia, painting a sewer drain, and transcribing oral history about the Lakeland neighborhood. As Josanne Francis, University of Maryland's Lead Community Engagement Program Coordinator, said, 'With Good Neighbor Day, we are ensuring that we are catering to the needs of the City of College Park and environment.' Xiomara Bradden, a University of Maryland junior, emphasized the importance of preserving the history of African Americans in the community, stating 'I feel a lot of people forget that segregation was not that long ago... preserving the history of African Americans, especially in this community who broke barriers, is extremely important.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Dhruv Maheshwari, Obvio Co-Founder
Susan Whitney, College Park Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park Councilmember
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
organizations: College Park Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee
44571.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering installing stop sign cameras around the city after a three-week pilot program showed that up to 92% of drivers did not come to a full stop at the signs. Councilmembers expressed support for the program, but decided to reach out to the community for input before voting, with Councilmember Susan Whitney saying 'I just wanted to make sure there was an opportunity for public comment and really frame this in a clear and public way.' The program's goal is safety, not profit, according to Councilmember Maria Mackie, who said 'Our motivation is safety, the city’s not making big money on this.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
John Carroll, Galena, Maryland, Mayor
Raymond Rodriguez, University of Maryland School of Public Policy
organizations: University of Maryland School of Public Policy
44540.ai_summary: College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir emphasized the importance of resident engagement in building trust in government, stating that mayors should serve the community regardless of party affiliation. At a symposium on civility and democracy, Kabir said, 'You are doing it to serve the people. You are no one but the public servant.' Galena, Maryland, Mayor John Carroll agreed, highlighting the need for transparency and daily engagement with residents, saying 'We live in that community as well... We pay those fees just the same. We pay the taxes just the same, but you've got to walk the walk if you're going to talk the talk.'

key_people: Andreé Sahakian, LV Collective Senior Development Manager
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: College Park Department of Planning & Community Development
44458.ai_summary: A Texas-based development company, LV Collective, is planning to build a mixed-use housing complex at the site of the former Campus Village Shoppes in College Park, expected to open in fall 2027. The seven-story building will have 299 units and 13,000 square feet of retail space, including a community space for the Lakeland neighborhood. As Andreé Sahakian, LV Collective Senior Development Manager, stated, 'With each development, we look for creative ways to collaborate with the community, embracing its history and honoring its character and culture.'

key_people: Sydney Nicole, University of Maryland student
Michael Williams, College Park Director of Economic Development
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Dan Zmijewski, Rally House attorney
44465.ai_summary: A sports apparel store, Rally House, is set to move into the former Target storefront on Baltimore Avenue, with the College Park City Council approving a $130,000 grant for the business. The store is expected to hire up to 70 employees and will sublet the space from Target for the initial two years, committing to stay in College Park for at least seven years. As Sydney Nicole, a University of Maryland student, said, 'I think it'll be nice... A lot of people around this area, they wear athleisure, Lululemon, Nike, Adidas; all that's very popular around College Park, especially with college students. So I think it'll be really good for business.'

key_people: Carlos Johnson, Hollywood Elementary School Principal
Lindsay Sullivan, Amplify Associate Vice President
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
44455.ai_summary: The College Park City Council approved $40,000 for a tutoring program for Hollywood Elementary School’s Spanish-speaking students. The program, provided by Amplify Tutoring, offers reading tutoring to 24 students in third to fifth grades and has shown success in improving reading skills. As Councilmember Jacob Hernandez said, 'If I could, I would try to carve [the] entirety of our budget to try and help the schools that we have here in College Park,' and Councilmember Maria Mackie noted she was 'very impressed with how [Amplify does] their tutoring and their success that they had [at Hollywood Elementary] in just four short months of last year, that they brought the students’ reading ability up.'

44445.ai_summary: The executive director of a local publication thanks over 200 readers who participated in an audience survey, which showed that readers rely on the publication for city government coverage, local events, and hyperlocal housing and development news. The director notes that readers appreciate profiles of local community members and organizations, as well as gardening and nature columns. The publication plans to deepen and broaden its conversations with readers and community leaders about local news, with a priority on news and information that gives broad access to civic engagement.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
Christina Cross, UMD Child Development Center Director
organizations: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services
locations: University of Maryland Child Development Center
44448.ai_summary: College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir sent a letter to state legislators asking for a review of daycare rules that require one staff member per three children under 24 months, citing the high cost of child care as a 'significant economic burden on families in College Park.' Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that Maryland has 'perhaps one of the most restrictive staff/child ratios in the United States for infants and toddlers,' making it difficult for local parents to afford the University of Maryland Child Development Center. Adams said that if the ratio is increased, parents could have more opportunities to find suitable, affordable, high-quality child care.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Anne Healey, State Delegate for District 22
44437.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has installed cameras on nine stop signs around the city to collect data on traffic and parking. Councilmember Stuart Adams stated that the cameras were placed at heavily trafficked intersections, but further review may occur. State Delegate Anne Healey, who sponsored legislation allowing cities in Prince George’s County to install stop sign monitors, said, 'There have been a lot of people that have talked to me about people just ignoring the stop signs, just running right through them, sometimes very fast and not slowing down at all, and near misses and accidents and people getting hurt,' and expects the cameras to change driver behavior.

key_people: Mike Wells, Senior Director of Franchise Operations for The Greene Turtle
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
44147.ai_summary: The Greene Turtle, a sports bar and grill, is opening a new location in the College Park Shopping Center in late September, bringing a new casual dining option to the city. 'We're happy and we're really excited to be in College Park,' said Mike Wells, Senior Director of Franchise Operations for The Greene Turtle, who noted that the restaurant has long partnered with University of Maryland Athletics. College Park City Councilmembers Susan Whitney and Stuart Adams expressed support for the establishment, with Whitney mentioning some concern about the restaurant staying within its allowed limit of alcohol sales, saying 'They definitely have the experience to be successful.'

key_people: Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Jen Holz, Maryland AARP Representative
Gail Lovelace, Age-Friendly College Park Co-Chair
Mary Anne Hakes, Senior Advisory Committee Co-Chair
key_issues: quality of life for older adults
44115.ai_summary: College Park is working toward achieving an age-friendly designation from the AARP, which indicates the city offers a good quality of life for older adults. According to Jen Holz, the Maryland AARP representative, membership in the AARP Network of Age-Friendly States and Communities means elected leaders have worked with residents and local advocates to make their community a friendly place to live for aging residents. As Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember, stated, 'I think that we need, as a city, to make College Park a place that people want to retire to and people want to continue living here,' and Mary Anne Hakes, SAC's co-chair, said the committee's goal is 'to make College Park an age-friendly community for all ages … to allow people to age in place and a secret desire to make College Park a retirement destination.'

key_people: Aaron Goldbeck, University of Maryland Bicycle Coordinator
44031.ai_summary: The city of College Park has explored various forms of micromobility, including bike-sharing programs and e-scooters, as alternatives to traditional transportation methods. According to Aaron Goldbeck, University of Maryland Bicycle Coordinator, 'We are very excited about the growth and success of the system' of mBike, a bike-sharing program introduced in 2016. However, the city's experience with micromobility has also raised concerns about the environmental impact and logistics of these alternatives, with studies suggesting that ride-hailing trips result in an estimated 69% more climate pollution on average than the trips they displace.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Susan Slingluff Hartmann, College Park City-University Partnership Executive Director
43934.ai_summary: The College Park City-University Partnership plans to spend $3 million to build an off-campus bike lane along Campus Drive that will connect to campus and to the Trolley Trail. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir hopes future improvements will extend the trail north to Beltsville, saying 'Beltsville is very dangerous, actually, to bike, so that’s something we’ll be advocating for.' The goal is to encourage more cyclists and pedestrians to use the trail in College Park as an alternative form of transportation.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
43946.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering a proposal to lower the voting age to 16, with Councilmember John Rigg stating that 'the younger they start participating in elections, the more likely they will regularly vote as adults.' The council also discussed the issue of lengthening their terms from two years to four, with Rigg noting that 'we are one of the few municipalities of any size that has two-year terms.' A straw poll showed that five councilmembers supported scheduling a future discussion on allowing 16- and 17-year-olds to register to vote in city elections.

key_people: Brian Galbraith, Cherokee Lane Elementary Principal
Kayla Newsome, Cherokee Lane Elementary Assistant Principal
Bernitta Johnson, College Park Academy Signature Programs Coordinator and Business Program Adviser
43931.ai_summary: The City of College Park has awarded a total of $36,800 in grants to eight local schools to fund educational projects, including an outdoor classroom and experiential learning opportunities. As Cherokee Lane Elementary Principal Brian Galbraith said, 'We are grateful for cities like College Park, they give schools these opportunities to think outside the box.' The grants will support innovative projects, such as a pitch competition for aspiring student entrepreneurs, and will allow schools to 'think outside the box' and provide unique learning experiences for their students.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Varaa Kukreti, University of Maryland Graduate Student Government President
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Edward Maginnis Jr., University of Maryland Assistant Vice President for Real Estate
organizations: University of Maryland Graduate Student Government
43919.ai_summary: The University of Maryland is developing a new graduate student housing complex, Leonardtown apartments, which is expected to be completed in August 2026 and will provide 465 units. College Park City Councilmembers John Rigg and Stuart Adams believe the project will have a positive impact on the city's economy and provide much-needed housing for graduate students, with Rigg stating it will be 'a vast improvement over the status quo' and Adams calling it 'absolutely the type of development that we've been asking for in College Park.' The development will address the high demand for affordable housing, although the rent prices, starting at $1,350 a month for studio apartments, may not be as affordable as some graduate students would like.

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Edward Gibbs, property lawyer
James Woodhouse, Autoville Drive resident
43838.ai_summary: The College Park City Council recommended approval of a four-story, eight-unit apartment building on Autoville Drive, despite concerns from residents about disruption to the quiet neighborhood. Councilmember Alan Hew praised the property owner for being receptive to the city's suggestions, saying 'It's rare that we have an applicant that’s so cooperative to the conditions put on them.' Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell voted against the project, citing its potential to 'change the character of the community, the quality of life of the residents,' while Councilmember John Rigg supported it, urging the council not to be 'blinded by status quo bias.'

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Department Director
Julia Bauer, National League of Cities Research and Data Analysis Program Manager
43835.ai_summary: The City of College Park has spent $19 million of the $22 million in federal pandemic funding it received through the American Rescue Plan Act (ARPA) and must use the remaining $3 million by December 31, 2026. According to College Park City Councilmember Stuart Adams, 'Without question, that was something unprecedented in the City of College Park.' The city used the funds to help residents pay bills, subsidize local businesses, and invest in affordable housing, with small businesses receiving $1.7 million in assistance.

key_people: Janeen Miller, College Park City Clerk
Yvette Allen, Assistant City Clerk of College Park
Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Joe Nagro, former College Park City Manager
43564.ai_summary: College Park City Clerk Janeen Miller will retire on August 1 after 18 years in the position, to be succeeded by Assistant City Clerk Yvette Allen. Miller expressed her readiness for retirement, stating 'I'm just tired of an alarm clock and having to be somewhere on somebody else's schedule, and I'm ready to control my own time.' City Manager Kenny Young and Mayor Fazlul Kabir praised Miller's dedication and service, with Kabir noting 'She reminds us, she keeps us on track. She's very punctual. She gets also very honest.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Tiffany Green, Prince George’s County Fire Chief
organizations: Branchville Volunteer Fire Co. & Rescue Squad
Berwyn Heights Volunteer Fire Department & Rescue Squad
43553.ai_summary: The Prince George’s County Circuit Court denied a request by College Park, Greenbelt, and Berwyn Heights to stop a plan to move paid firefighters from the Greenbelt and Berwyn Heights fire stations to elsewhere in the county. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated that 'The damage is already done' and expressed concern that relying on volunteers, especially during the summer when University of Maryland students are not in the city, puts College Park residents at risk. The relocation of 55 firefighters, including 24 from Greenbelt and six from Berwyn Heights, is expected to impact response times to fire and medical emergencies in College Park.

key_people: Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Director
Michael Bodaken, Prince George’s County Council’s Rent Stabilization Working Group Member and University of Maryland School of Public Policy Adjunct Instructor
Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember (District 3)
organizations: College Park Office of Economic Development
University of Maryland School of Public Policy
43581.ai_summary: A proposed 3% rent cap in Prince George’s County may have a low impact in College Park, as up to 65% of apartment buildings could be exempt due to being constructed after 2000. According to Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Director, 'My takeaway personally, and at the first swipe of it, is that because of all the new construction that really boomed since 2012, that we’re going to have a very small or very little effect on the 3% cap.' The measure aims to protect tenants from 'outrageous gouging' while ensuring it does not inhibit investment in the county, as stated by County Councilmember Eric Olson.

key_people: Angela Alsobrooks, Prince George’s County Executive
Tiffany Green, Prince George’s County Fire Chief
William Snoddy, Judge
43462.ai_summary: The cities of Greenbelt, Berwyn Heights, and College Park filed a joint motion for a Temporary Restraining Order against Prince George’s County Executive Angela Alsobrooks and Fire Chief Tiffany Green to prevent the removal of career firefighters from local fire stations. The motion argued that the county's plan to rely on volunteers would result in 'drastic shortages of adequate fire, rescue, and medical personnel' and pose a 'much greater risk of catastrophic injury' to residents. Judge William Snoddy rejected the motion, primarily due to the plaintiffs' failure to demonstrate irreparable harm.

key_people: Mike Galvin, SavATree Director of Consultant Group
Richard Jones, University of Maryland Campus Arborist
organizations: College Park Department of Public Works
43393.ai_summary: A new assessment found that while tree cover on residential properties in College Park decreased between 2009 and 2020, the city's overall tree canopy has remained relatively stable with a slight increase. According to Mike Galvin, director of SavATree's consultant group, the city's efforts to limit tree removal and encourage planting are 'great proactive steps' toward slowing or stopping the loss of tree canopy in neighborhoods. Richard Jones, University of Maryland Campus Arborist, noted that 'having forest is so critical, not only for the environment, but for our ability to go about our day-to-day lives in a city'.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Ryna Quinones, College Park Communications and Events Manager
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
43119.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is questioning the future of the annual spring parade, with Councilmember John Rigg suggesting the city consider getting out of the parade business due to high costs and low attendance. The city spent $34,000 on this year's parade and invested approximately 1,000 staff hours worth another $34,000, with Rigg noting that the total funding boils down to approximately $47 per attendee, saying 'That's not good, I can't think of any way to justify that.' The council is exploring alternative community events, such as a music festival or a summer concert series, to promote community engagement and building.

key_people: Ken Ulman, President of Terrapin Development Co.
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Sahil Patel, University of Maryland Junior Computer Science Major
Heather Kingry, Primetime Restaurant
organizations: University of Maryland College Park Foundation
43121.ai_summary: Shake Shack, Chopt, and Duck Donuts are set to open in College Park this fall on the first floor of a new student housing building, Union on Knox. According to Ken Ulman, President of Terrapin Development Co., 'Shake Shack, you know, frankly, they have been looking for a location in College Park for a number of years.' City Councilmember John Rigg expressed excitement about the new additions, saying 'I have a passion for small businesses, but I know these will be great additions to College Park that members of the community will enjoy.'

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
43109.ai_summary: The city of College Park offers multiple programs to help homeowners save on property taxes, including the Homestead Property Tax Credit and the Homeowners' Property Tax Credit. According to College Park City Councilmember Stuart Adams, 'I would say without hesitation that College Park has some of the most generous property tax credits of any municipality in Prince George’s County and some of the strongest in the entire state.' These credits can help mitigate the impact of the recent 11% increase in the residential tax rate in College Park.

key_people: Erica Otte, University of Maryland Student Government Association Student Liaison to College Park City Council
John Fahmy, University of Maryland Student Government Association Deputy Student Liaison to College Park City Council
Dhruvak Mirani, Former University of Maryland Student Government Association Student Liaison to College Park City Council
Gannon Sprinkle, Former University of Maryland Student Government Association Deputy Student Liaison to College Park City Council
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Andy Harris, U.S. Representative for Maryland's 1st Congressional District
organizations: University of Maryland Student Government Association
Harford County Parks and Recreation Department
Maryland Office of the Public Defender
43126.ai_summary: Two new University of Maryland students, Erica Otte and John Fahmy, will serve as student liaisons to the College Park City Council. Otte said, 'I'm very passionate about advocacy,' and wants to 'make it easier for students to connect with the broader community, maybe [through] some opportunities or events here on campus, and to make it easier to raise a family in College Park, especially after graduating.' Councilmember Maria Mackie stated that Otte's desire to 'build bridges between students and long-term residents' set her apart from other applicants, and that 'she doesn't want to do the 'us and them' thing.'

key_people: Teresa Way-Pezzuti, College Park Director of Human Resources
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park Councilmember
Rick Campbell, Paypoint HR Technical Director
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
43045.ai_summary: The City of College Park will increase salaries for 74 employees after a study by Paypoint HR showed they earn less than their peers in other cities. 'I think it's vitally important for us to bring our employees to a fair compensation and wage, especially if we want to maintain them,' said Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters. The city aims to reach the 50th percentile of comparable cities, with potential future goals to meet the 60th percentile, with the initial increases estimated to cost $240,784.

key_people: Iris Vukmanovic, The Washington Post Reporter
College Park City Councilmembers, College Park City Council
43114.ai_summary: The Washington Post featured North College Park neighborhoods, including Hollywood, Branchville, and Daniels Park, in an article about residents' advocacy efforts. The community was able to save the Hollywood Farmers Market through an email-writing campaign that sent nearly 100 messages of support to College Park City Councilmembers. As reporter Iris Vukmanovic noted, 'The community benefits from engaged neighbors who know how to work together,' highlighting the area's 'sense of community in part because of its tremendous diversity' and 'passion for walking and biking'.

key_people: Gary Fields, College Park Department of Finance Director
Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
Brian Roan, Daniels Park Resident
Bonny Davis, Long-time College Park Resident
42752.ai_summary: The College Park City Council approved a $27.4 million operating budget for fiscal year 2025, which includes an 11% residential property tax rate increase. According to Gary Fields, College Park Department of Finance Director, 'We do not take proposing a property tax increase lightly... We're looking to the future to make sure we stay financially sound.' The tax increase was met with opposition from some residents, including Brian Roan, who warned that it could lead to a 'massive drain of families and long-time residents' from the city.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Angela Alsobrooks, U.S. Senate candidate
Glenn Ivey, U.S. Congress representative for Maryland District 4
42023.ai_summary: The city of College Park has entered the contractor selection phase for a flood abatement project in the Calvert Hills neighborhood, which aims to bring stormwater systems up to current standards. Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that current codes are not up-to-date with climate change, saying 'We need to acknowledge that this is going to happen a lot more frequently than people want to believe... to acknowledge that the risks are here today, but they continue to increase.' The project is expected to begin mid-summer. Meanwhile, local businesses are preparing for the summer season when most college students from the University of Maryland head home, and recent election results have been announced, with Angela Alsobrooks winning the state Democratic primary for U.S. Senate and Glenn Ivey winning the primary for his seat as a representative in the U.S. Congress for Maryland District 4.

key_people: Maxine Gross, Chair of the College Park Restorative Justice Commission
Maxine Gross, Chairwoman of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project
Ruth Murphy, Lakeland Civic Association Vice President
Robert Thurston, Lakeland Civic Association President
41839.ai_summary: Maxine Gross, a fifth-generation Lakelander and Chair of the College Park Restorative Justice Commission, has committed to preserving the history of Lakeland and sharing the stories of its community. As Gross said, 'It looked like nirvana... It looked like paradise' when she first saw the city plans for Lakeland, but the plans changed, and the city ultimately destroyed 104 single-family homes, displacing families. Gross, along with Ruth Murphy, played a major role in ensuring that a new Lakeland community center will be included in the student housing development that will replace Campus Village Shoppes, with Murphy saying 'Maxine [is] such an incredible gift... Not only to this community but to all of us because she takes the ego out of the work, and she is genuinely committed to the work itself.'

key_people: Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
Carson Peters, UMD School of Public Health doctoral student
Mark Williams, UMD School of Music jazz studies program director
Megan Ravert, Sustainable Terps liaison
41833.ai_summary: The City of College Park participated in the University of Maryland's Maryland Day, offering information about local resources to attendees. 'I think it’s an important event just to showcase what the University of Maryland offers to the community,' said Kenny Young, College Park City Manager. The city set up a tent where attendees could meet the mayor and city councilmembers, and city staff distributed free materials and oversaw lawn games, while the UMD Office of Sustainability and other groups also participated in the event.

key_people: Rachel Romeo, University of Maryland Hearing and Speech Sciences Professor
Susan Slingluff Hartmann, College Park City-University Partnership Executive Director
organizations: Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development
41822.ai_summary: The College Park City-University Partnership has ended an eight-year homeownership program that awarded $15,000 to 83 new homebuyers, and has launched a new program called Live + Work College Park to support full-time employees in the city. According to Susan Slingluff Hartmann, the new program offers a forgivable loan of $20,000 to help with down payments and closing costs. As Rachel Romeo, a University of Maryland professor, said, the funding 'did make it possible for us to buy a house we might not otherwise have been able to' and allowed her to 'live in the community that I work in and do research with.'

key_people: Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember (District 3)
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
Daniel Oates, President of the Calvert Hills Citizens Association
41817.ai_summary: The Calvert Hills stormwater drainage project in College Park is moving forward after over a decade, with contractor selection underway and work expected to begin by mid-summer. Prince George’s County Councilmember Eric Olson said, 'The sooner we get construction started, the sooner it will finish.' The project aims to prevent pooling on streets and lawns in the area around Calvert Park by diverting stormwater into an underground concrete vault, addressing flooding issues that have plagued the neighborhood for years, with College Park City Councilmember Stuart Adams noting that 'we need to acknowledge that this is going to happen a lot more frequently than people want to believe.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Ryna Quiñones, College Park Communications and Events Manager
Betty Colonomos, College Park Senior Advisory Committee member
Elena Argueta, 2024 Miss College Park
Rigo Ayala, College Park resident
41846.ai_summary: The City of College Park held its third annual parade, this time with a Star Wars theme, on May 4, attracting hundreds of residents despite the rain. According to College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir, 'It's wonderful, I've met quite a few neighbors, and I'm very pleased that they came with their families.' The parade, which included participants from neighborhood organizations and local schools, is seen as an opportunity for community building, with Ryna Quiñones, the city's communications and events manager, stating that 'There's something for everybody, for every age, and [our offerings are] very diverse.'

key_people: Marta McLellan Ross, NPR Vice President of Government and External Affairs
Melanie Dzwonchyk, Laurel Historical Society Board Member
Joe Murchison, Side by Side Founder and Executive Director
Michael Walls, C. Michael Walls, Attorney at Law, LLC Founder
Nora Urbieta Eidelman, College Park United Methodist Church Administrator & Outreach Director
Nora Urbieta Eidelman, City of College Park Ethics Commission Member
Nora Urbieta Eidelman, Maryland Cannabis Public Health Advisory Council Member
Merrill Hartson, Associated Press Reporter, Editor and Manager
organizations: C. Michael Walls, Attorney at Law, LLC
University of Baltimore School of Law
Maryland Cannabis Public Health Advisory Council
Community Legal Services of Prince George’s County, Inc.
Human Rights Commission for Prince Georges’ County
42280.ai_summary: The article lists the board of directors for an unspecified organization, which includes Marta McLellan Ross, Melanie Dzwonchyk, Joe Murchison, Michael Walls, Nora Urbieta Eidelman, and Merrill Hartson. These individuals have diverse backgrounds in journalism, non-profit leadership, and public administration, with connections to various organizations such as NPR, the Laurel Historical Society, and the City of College Park. The article provides information on the board members' professional experience and community involvement, but does not specifically address any governance issues in College Park.

key_people: Kelly Jordan, Berwyn District Civic Association Co-President
Bob Catlin, Berwyn District Civic Association Co-President
Edward Najjar, Translator
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
41659.ai_summary: The Berwyn District Civic Association has been publishing a bilingual newsletter for the past two years, with the help of a translator, Edward Najjar, who was funded by a grant from the city. 'I'm all about getting folks who speak Spanish to be part of the larger community,' said Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember, in support of the proposed budget item to provide $7,500 for neighborhood associations to pay for translators. The city's proposed operating budget for fiscal year 2025 also includes $65,000 to add a full-time bilingual communications coordinator to the city staff, which Hernandez and the civic association co-presidents, Kelly Jordan and Bob Catlin, see as a valuable resource for community outreach.

key_people: Bill Gardiner, College Park Assistant City Manager
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
41342.ai_summary: The Hollywood Farmers Market in North College Park will open for the season on April 13, despite concerns from city staff about its cost, with Assistant City Manager Bill Gardiner suggesting the city council consider whether spending over $20,000 per season is worth it. As Mayor Fazlul Kabir noted, 'This place is something that they really love. They want to come and see other community members,' after receiving 97 letters of support from community members. The city council is debating the market's value and considering alternatives, including combining it with the College Park Farmers Market or recruiting a produce vendor to supply a more robust selection of fresh fruits and vegetables.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
41366.ai_summary: In his State of the City address, College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir highlighted the city's "exponential growth" in 2023, citing the opening of 10 new businesses that will bring 250 new jobs. The city is also expecting nearly 20 new businesses and restaurants to open in the coming year, and will add two student housing complexes and three affordable housing complexes through grants and partnerships. The city has made efforts to improve sustainability, diversity, and safety, including donations to community organizations and grants to local businesses and households.

key_people: Gary Fields, College Park Finance Director
Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
41323.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering a 13% increase in residential property taxes, which would be the first hike in 10 years, to help fund a $27.4 million budget proposal for fiscal year 2025. According to Gary Fields, the city's finance director, 'It's a reset, we're doing it for the future. … We feel like that we will not have to be looking at [another] rate increase in the near future.' The proposed increase would raise the tax rate from 30.18 cents to 34.18 cents per $100 of assessed value, and is part of a larger budget plan that also includes a commercial property tax hike.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Dhruvak Mirani, University of Maryland Student Liaison to College Park City Council
