Gannon Sprinkle, University of Maryland Deputy Student Liaison to College Park City Council
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
41358.ai_summary: The University of Maryland students can apply to be student liaisons to the College Park City Council until April 12. The student liaison and deputy liaison serve one-year terms, participating in weekly council meetings, conducting research, and finding experts to speak to the council. As Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'It is important to bridge the gap as much as we can... Anything we do at the city council level that can potentially impact the campus community in any manner … it is important for [students] to learn that as well and get their input on those issues.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Mary Lehman, Delegate of Prince George's and Anne Arundel
Julie Palakovich Carr, Delegate of Montgomery
Wes Moore, Governor
41348.ai_summary: The Maryland General Assembly passed two bills to increase penalties for street racing and allow Prince George's and Montgomery counties to install noise-sensing traffic cameras. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated that 'it is not unusual for groups of motorcycle and ATV drivers to briefly take over sections of Baltimore Avenue on Route 1' and that 'these actions harm our communities, and these actions waste … resources, especially the police resources.' If signed by Governor Wes Moore, the new law will increase fines and raise the number of points added to the licenses of drivers found participating in these activities, with Delegate Mary Lehman saying 'exhibition driving will continue to disrupt our communities, endanger law enforcement and risk serious bodily harm, or worse, to participants and passersby.'

key_people: Kayla Carter, College Park Racial Equity Officer
Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
41374.ai_summary: The City of College Park is investigating its racial equity officer, Kayla Carter, after news reports revealed she posted comments on her personal social media accounts that some might consider offensive. According to City Manager Kenny Young, 'Ms. Carter’s views expressed on her personal accounts do not reflect the opinions, beliefs and core values of the City of College Park and its mayor and council.' Carter was hired as the city's first racial equity officer in 2022 to examine city policies and programs for barriers to equal participation and to identify opportunities for Black residents.

key_people: Derek Gunn, Maryland State Highway Administration District 3 Engineer
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Larry Hogan, Former Governor of Maryland
Gregory Slater, Former Maryland Transportation Secretary
Kieran Leonard, Sweetgreen Manager
Kayla Scott, Looney's Pub Manager
41191.ai_summary: The Maryland State Highway Administration is set to finish the first phase of Route 1 improvements, which include adding bike lanes, raised medians, and turn lanes, after a year-long delay. The project, which began in 2020, is more than 74% complete, with a price tag of $28.7 million. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated, 'The changes, once it's happened, will make Route 1 very nice... It's not about making the street pretty... It's for our safety.'

key_people: Danny Copeland, RST Development Vice President
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Kate Kennedy, District 1 resident and former College Park City Councilmember
organizations: Meals on Wheels of College Park
41187.ai_summary: Developers of the 317-unit affordable apartment building, Flats at College Park, expect to start construction as soon as April. The project will offer two- and three-bedroom apartments with monthly rents based on income, targeting households that make between 50% to 80% of the area median income. As RST Development Vice President Danny Copeland said, 'It’s a redevelopment of some motels that weren’t necessarily a shining light along the Route 1 corridor,' and the new property will revitalize the community while putting the space to better use with affordable housing for low-income tenants.

key_people: Sam Gauntt, The Here & Now Reporter
Griffin Limerick, Life & Times Managing Editor
41131.ai_summary: The city of College Park voted to explore imposing a tax on vacant and/or blighted properties, as it can be more profitable for owners and developers to keep a property vacant. The city is hoping to identify the right tax structure and language to make that math work more towards ending vacancies. Griffin Limerick, Life & Times managing editor, is calling for a return of in-person council meetings in Hyattsville, noting that nearby cities like Takoma Park, Greenbelt, College Park, and Laurel have already done so.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
40635.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted to form a subcommittee to explore taxing blighted and vacant properties in the city. Councilmember Stuart Adams has advocated for this tax since 2019, stating that it would 'move us in the right direction' and 'compels them to make a few extra calls on filling up those retail spots.' According to City Manager Kenny Young, the subcommittee will define what properties count as blighted or vacant and determine how to impose a potential tax, studying how other cities like Frederick and Washington have done so.

key_people: Maxine Gross, College Park Restorative Justice Commission Chairwoman
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
The Rev. Joanne Braxton, Braxton Institute CEO and President
Dr. Mindy Fullilove, Author
Nkechi Taifa, Book Club Guest
40745.ai_summary: The College Park Restorative Justice Commission has partnered with the Braxton Institute to host a series of events in Lakeland, including lectures and workshops, to raise awareness about the community's history and the impact of urban renewal. As Maxine Gross, Chairwoman of the Restorative Justice Commission, said, 'if you don’t learn your history, you’re destined to repeat it' and 'it’s important to not just recognize that thing happened, but also to take positive action on assisting that community and heal it.' The events aim to help city residents understand the impact of urban renewal on Lakeland and how restorative justice can be accomplished.

key_people: Bonnie McClellan, College Park Woods Neighborhood Watch Vice Chair
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Robert Marsili, College Park Director of Public Works
organizations: College Park Department of Public Works
40680.ai_summary: Construction on the College Park Woods Clubhouse is set to begin, with a projected completion date by the end of the year, according to city projections. The College Park City Council approved a $1.3 million contract with Rainbow Construction Co. to bring the project to fruition, which has been in the works since July 2018. As Bonnie McClellan, Vice Chair of the College Park Woods Neighborhood Watch, said, 'This is a dream come true,' and Councilmember Maria Mackie added, 'Many of our activities have been underneath the gazebo in the pouring rain, so it'll be a nice thing to have for the District 4 community, but also for the city.'

key_people: Kaya Carter, College Park Racial Equity Officer
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission
40750.ai_summary: The College Park Restorative Justice Commission has proposed changes to park names to honor Lakeland history, including renaming Lake Artemesia Natural Area and the College Park Community Center. The commission's report also calls for the city council to establish a scholarship fund for Lakeland residents, as stated by Kaya Carter, College Park Racial Equity Officer, who said the changes would 'not only preserve [the Lakeland] legacy but also educate future generations about the important role that this area has played in shaping the city.' Councilmember Jacob Hernandez expressed appreciation for the commission's efforts, saying 'I just want to thank you all for the very thoughtful steps to try to address some of the harder parts of our history here in College Park, but also trying to pay homage to the brighter future that we have.'

key_people: Katelynn Winebrenner, College Park Reporter
Christine Johnson, City of Laurel Councilwoman
Pamela Boozer-Strother, Prince George's County Board of Education Member
organizations: Prince George's County Board of Education
40533.ai_summary: The Washington Commanders' headquarters is moving to the University of Maryland's Discovery District on River Road, which may attract other prominent companies and impact daily traffic. The recent Prince George's County Board of Education meeting focused on the proposed FY 2025 operating budget, but bus problems dominated the public comment period, with one parent stating their child's bus had been at least 30 minutes late to school daily. Board member Pamela Boozer-Strother referenced a 2011 audit, saying 'Let's be honest, we've been talking about the bell times in many task forces on transportation for years. This district has been unwilling to change them because every school community has a reason why they think their bell time shouldn't change.'

key_people: Dhruvak Mirani, University of Maryland Liaison to the College Park City Council
Gannon Sprinkle, University of Maryland Deputy Liaison to the College Park City Council
William Edwards, University of Maryland Student
40245.ai_summary: Two University of Maryland student leaders, Dhruvak Mirani and Gannon Sprinkle, proposed establishing a tenants union for students who rent in College Park to lower the cost of rent, help improve living conditions and address maintenance issues. As Mirani said, 'We can’t really address housing prices in a vacuum without talking about all of the other issues that go along with it.' The students plan to start the union as a 'soft launch' and have been meeting with similar groups, such as Stomp Out Slumlords, to learn how to proceed.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem and City Councilmember for District 4
James Neilis, Berwyn resident
Brian McAllister, College Park resident
39395.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has proposed a housing subsidy of $1,500 for 150 low-income University of Maryland undergraduates to help them rent apartments in high-rise Route 1 buildings. As Mayor Fazlul Kabir noted, 'I’m not quite sure what is going to happen with this proposal at the end, given all the pushback and questions.' The proposal has received pushback from residents, with one resident, James Neilis, calling the program a 'theft of taxpayer revenue' and questioning why the city and not the university is offering the subsidy, as resident Brian McAllister asked, 'Why does the City Council feel it is their responsibility to pay these subsidies when the university and the business developers aren’t willing to do it for themselves?'

key_people: Todd Larson, College Park Committee for a Better Environment Chair
Alexa Bely, College Park Committee for a Better Environment Member
Brenda Alexander, College Park City Arborist
organizations: College Park Committee for a Better Environment
39385.ai_summary: The College Park Committee for a Better Environment (CBE) has been leading sustainability efforts in the city for over 50 years, with initiatives such as painting murals on storm drains to raise awareness about environmental impact. CBE Chair Todd Larson said, 'This project gives local artists a chance to show their work in a place where everyone can see it, while also providing an educational message to help residents understand why it’s important not to put anything down the storm drain except water.' The committee has also worked on labeling native trees along the Rhode Island Avenue Trolley Trail and issuing Green Awards to recognize environmentally responsible practices in the city.

key_people: Rakhmel Rafi, College Park Grants Coordinator
Brian McAllister, Berwyn Resident
Emily Friend, Berwyn Resident and Berwyn District Civic Association Treasurer
Dan Blasberg, Berwyn Resident
Alec Lynde, Berwyn District Civic Association Member
39270.ai_summary: The City of College Park is exploring possible uses for a vacant lot in Berwyn, including designating it as a pocket park, creating a dog park, or installing fitness equipment. Rakhmel Rafi, the city's grants coordinator, presented options to residents, who expressed enthusiasm for a permaculture garden, but also concerns about maintenance, with Emily Friend suggesting a rain garden as an alternative, saying it could be used as a teaching tool. Dan Blasberg doubted the city's ability to handle storm water management projects, citing the city's track record as 'an abject failure' and pointing to the soft runoff at the end of Rhode Island Avenue and Greenbelt Road, which 'collects more mosquitoes now than it did before they played with it'.

key_people: Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember
Joseline Pena-Melnyk, Maryland Delegate
39177.ai_summary: New laws are effective in Maryland as of January 1, 2024, including a plastic bag ban in Prince George’s and Anne Arundel counties, similar to the one adopted by the City of College Park last year. The state minimum wage will increase to $15 per hour, affecting over 160,000 workers, with Maryland Del. Joseline Pena-Melnyk stating that 'people are barely getting by. Even $15 an hour is not enough; we should have a living wage.' Prince George’s County Councilmember Eric Olson noted that the plastic bag ban will 'remove over 300 million plastic bags a year from the waste stream in Prince George’s County alone.'

key_people: Millard House II, Prince George’s County Public Schools Superintendent
NKenge Baker, Northwestern High School Principal
Ashanti Martinez, Maryland House of Delegates Member for District 22
key_issues: cyberattack on Prince George’s County Public Schools
39145.ai_summary: The article reviews tragic events and trials in 2023, including the arrest of the then-mayor of College Park and the sentencing to 30 years in prison. Two Riverdale Elementary School students were struck and killed by a van, prompting concerns about unfilled crossing guard positions and pedestrian safety around county schools. The article also mentions the start of new journeys for Superintendent Millard House II and Principal NKenge Baker, as well as the opening of the new Hyattsville Middle School, which somewhat eased the strain of the previously mentioned bus driver shortage.

key_people: Jacob T. Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
39012.ai_summary: Jacob T. Hernandez has joined the College Park City Council as a District 1 representative, aiming to enhance communication, improve education, and help residents navigate bureaucratic hurdles. Hernandez, the city's only Hispanic American councilmember, said 'It's only through our united organized voices that we are going to be able to facilitate the greater projects and programs that I have in store' and plans to strengthen relationships between the City of College Park and local public education leaders. Hernandez's goals and leadership style are expected to have a positive impact on College Park governance, particularly in addressing the needs of the city's Hispanic or Latino population, which makes up 18% of the population.

key_people: Jason Azevedo, Old Town Resident
Matt Tedesco, Attorney for Greenhill Capital Corporation
Adele Ellis, Patron of Pho Tom
Richard Biffl, Chair of the Old Town Historic District Local Advisory Committee
organizations: Old Town Historic District Local Advisory Committee
39027.ai_summary: A proposed redevelopment project, Terrapin House, would displace three local restaurants in downtown College Park, including Pho Tom, Northwest Chinese, and Ritchie's Colombian Restaurant. The project has raised concerns among residents and local officials about the impact on existing businesses, historic character, and stormwater management. As Adele Ellis, a patron of Pho Tom, said, 'We're losing too many local restaurants,' and expressed hope that the developer would make the new building's rent affordable for the existing restaurants to return.

key_people: Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Director
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
39023.ai_summary: The City of College Park is responding to small business losses due to new developments, with a $75,000 business retention fund and a three-cent tax increase on commercial property and apartment buildings to generate $515,000 in additional revenue. As Michael Williams, College Park's economic development director, said, 'We wanted to retain some of the businesses who make up the fabric and the character of College Park.' Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that around half of the funds raised by the tax increase could reach local businesses, with the city already approving $23,000 in relocation assistance for Laser Essential.

key_people: H.C. "Curley" Byrd, University of Maryland President
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission
39017.ai_summary: The history of Lakeland, a neighborhood in College Park, is marked by the displacement of its residents due to urban renewal efforts in the 1960s and 1970s. The University of Maryland's president, H.C. "Curley" Byrd, initiated a plan to create a new municipality, College Park, which included Lakeland despite opposition from its residents. The prosperity of neighborhoods like Lakeland, Berwyn, and Hollywood determines the overall thriving of the city.

key_people: Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park Councilmember
39032.ai_summary: The Winter Wonderland event was held at city hall plaza on Dec. 2, featuring holiday music, food, and activities for all ages. Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell and Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters participated in the event, which included a tree lighting ceremony. According to Old Town resident Kathy Bryant, the new city hall was a wonderful venue for the event, and Berwyn resident Emily Friend said her 5-month-old son had a good time, noting 'He was scared of Santa but loved the Grinch,' and 'It’s cool that there was something fun for every group and every age.'

key_people: Kit Slack, Streetcar Suburbs Publishing Executive Director
39010.ai_summary: The College Park Here & Now is seeking donations to support local news, with a goal of raising $13,000 by the end of the year. Kit Slack, Executive Director of Streetcar Suburbs Publishing, states that the fundraiser is necessary to continue covering the city's government, county schools, and local developments. If the goal is met, Newsmatch has pledged to match donations dollar for dollar, and the publication hopes to add Spanish content in the coming year.

key_people: Emily Strab, College Park City Councilmember
38926.ai_summary: City Councilmember Emily Strab won the first-ever Maryland Catholic Woman of the Year award, presented by the Maryland Catholic Women’s Conference, which drew over 600 participants. According to the conference, “Emily Strab’s selection as the 2023 Maryland Catholic Woman of the Year exemplifies the values of faith, hope, and love in action.” Strab was elected to the city council in 2022 and serves as the office and enrollment administrator for St. Jerome Institute in D.C.

key_people: Sara Rían, Poet
Jaclyn Bruner is excluded as per the format, no other people mentioned with specific titles are present
38992.ai_summary: The author is calling for the College Park City Council to address the lack of crossing guards, particularly after two Riverdale Elementary School students were hit by a car. The author had previously spoken at a College Park City Council meeting in January, urging the city to help advocate for crossing guards for Hollywood Elementary School. As Sara Rían writes, “don’t you grow up / we say to babies. / don’t you get old / we say to adults. / but please do. / grow up. get old. / what a privilege. / what a gift,” highlighting the importance of ensuring children’s safety to allow them to grow up.

key_people: Robert "RC" Carter, Head of the ElectionGuard program
Benjamin Hovland, College Park Board of Elections Supervisors and U.S. Election Assistance Commission Commissioner
Josh Benaloh, Microsoft Senior Cryptographer
38704.ai_summary: The City of College Park has implemented new verification technology in its elections, allowing voters to confirm that their votes have been counted. According to Robert "RC" Carter, Head of the ElectionGuard program, "Think of it as like FedEx saying your package has arrived". This technology, developed by Microsoft, provides voters with a paper confirmation that they can use to verify their vote, and also provides an encrypted public record of the vote that can be inspected and verified by interested parties.

key_people: Miss Floribunda, Columnist
Polyantha, Miss Floribunda's Sister
Bonhomme Boutonnier, Polyantha's Husband
Noisette, Polyantha's Daughter
Joe Fox-Glover, Gardener
The Minnowhavens, Garden Owners
key_issues: city ordinance on grass and weeds
38718.ai_summary: The College Park city ordinance on grass and weeds now permits alternative gardens, including the use of native plants and tall grasses, which has sparked aesthetic concerns among some residents. Miss Floribunda, a columnist, weighs in on the issue, citing her experience visiting her sister in France, where she observed the use of native plants and grasses in gardens, including the Jardin Botanique d'Evreux. The ordinance is an attempt to promote ecologically friendly gardening practices, but some residents, like 'Mowed and Manicured on Madison Street', are concerned that these gardens will look 'messy and unkempt'.

Kate Kennedy, College Park City Councilmember

38626.ai_summary: In the recent College Park City Council election, Jacob T. Hernandez was elected to District 1 with 266 votes, while Alan Hew was reelected with 322 votes. Incumbent Mayor Fazlul Kabir ran unopposed and was reelected with 1,217 votes. The election also saw the reelection of several councilmembers, including Stuart M. Adams and John B. Rigg III in District 3. Voters also weighed in on a nonbinding question about changing councilmember term lengths to staggered 4-year terms, with 710 voters in support and 567 opposed.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Perez Abbott, College Park City Council candidate
Shawn Anderson, WTOP news anchor
organizations: The U.S. Biomedical Advanced Research and Development Authority
Prince George’s County Economic Development Corporation
38536.ai_summary: College Park District 3 city council candidates and the mayor discussed economic development, public safety, and property taxes at a forum sponsored by the Yarrow Civic Association. The candidates and Mayor Fazlul Kabir informed voters about their plans to address the city’s challenges, including supporting local businesses and the displacement of 15 businesses at Campus Village Shops. Mayor Kabir stated, 'We have been working to take care of the businesses because they are our assets,' and the candidates proposed various solutions to support small local businesses and ensure public safety in the city.

38531.ai_summary: A candidate forum for District 1 in College Park was sponsored by College Park Here & Now on October 18, 2023, at City Hall. The forum covered various topics including government teamwork, small business development, construction, connectivity, climate change, biodiversity, and road dangers. Voting for the College Park election is scheduled to take place on November 5 at the College Park Community Center.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
38455.ai_summary: The City of College Park is preparing for its November election, with candidate forums scheduled for October 18 and 19. Fazlul Kabir, the current College Park Mayor, is running for re-election and has outlined his plans to continue leading the city by uniting the community, enhancing transparency and fostering trust. According to Kabir, his advocacy focuses on improving the quality of life with strong public safety measures, enhanced code enforcement, smart economic development, support for new small businesses, sustainable and green programs, and solutions for aging-in-place seniors.

key_people: William Whichard, Homeowner in Calvert Hills neighborhood
Daniel Cunningham, Executive Director of the Community Preservation Trust
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Susan Hartman, Executive Director of the College Park City-University Partnership
organizations: Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development
38473.ai_summary: The Community Preservation Trust, an offshoot of the College Park City-University Partnership, celebrated its first home purchase and sale with a ribbon cutting ceremony. William Whichard, the new homeowner, said 'Gratitude characterizes what I feel today' and expressed his hope for a strengthened community. The program aims to increase home ownership in the city by making affordable homes available, with Mayor Fazlul Kabir praising the program's accomplishment and hoping it will become sustainable to offer similar opportunities to others in the future.

key_people: Emily Clipton, Low Impact Design Center
Lesley Riddle, Hyattsville Director of Public Works
organizations: City of Hyattsville Department of Public Works
38376.ai_summary: The Low Impact Design Center presented a plan for the new King Park at the September 18 city council meeting, which includes a nature-inspired playground and other amenities. Emily Clipton noted that the final design took into consideration feedback from residents, who wanted the playground to remain on the edge of the park to allow for green space. Lesley Riddle, Hyattsville Director of Public Works, stated that the park can reopen in late Spring 2024, saying 'We're in good shape, [but] it took a long time!' after ensuring the proper remediation of an underground heating oil tank.

key_people: Kayla Carter, College Park Racial Equity Officer
Miriam Bader, College Park Director of Planning and Community Development
38373.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously approved the street name Ancestor’s Lane, which was chosen by the Restorative Justice Commission to honor the local Black community. According to Kayla Carter, College Park Racial Equity Officer, 'It’s a very large project, and things are still new, but we’re doing the best we can.' The commission worked to include the community in the decision and aimed to give the street a name that an entire community could connect with, rather than naming it after an individual.

38371.ai_summary: The College Park election will be held on November 5. The article provides resources for voters, including a Voters Guide and links to candidate forums for District 1 and District 3. The article invites readers to check back for the latest updates on the election.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Kate Kennedy, College Park City Councilmember
Aubrey Batten, College Park Resident
38290.ai_summary: The College Park city council is preparing for a November 5 election, where voters will weigh in on a non-binding advisory question about switching from two-year to four-year terms for elected officials. Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that 'If the people vote yes, the city won’t actually change things until the council votes on it. The ballot question’s purpose is to gather information.' Resident Aubrey Batten expressed her opinion, stating 'I take exception to the assertion by certain councilmembers that residents need to be educated about this issue so that we change our minds. Our voice was made clear by the 2019 vote that we prioritize accountability over making it easy to campaign.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Jeremy Rose, FairVote
Alexa Bely, University of Maryland teacher/researcher
38056.ai_summary: The current voting system in College Park has a flaw where voter preferences among candidates are ignored if the top candidate receives more than 50% of the votes. Jeremy Rose and Alexa Bely suggest adopting ranked choice voting, which considers a voter's top preference and their ranked choices across all candidates, to increase overall voter satisfaction and decrease polarization among candidates. They propose that College Park consider adopting ranked choice voting for the 2025 city election, and encourage interested individuals to email the city's chapter of Ranked Choice Voting Maryland at rcvcollegepark@gmail.com.

key_people: Matthew Kirschebaum, University of Maryland English Professor
Elizabeth Maring, College Park Resident
Christina Grant, College Park Resident
38050.ai_summary: Two new franchise restaurants, The Great Greek Mediterranean Grill and The Little Blue Menu, are coming to College Park, with The Little Blue Menu being a digital-based restaurant or ghost kitchen. The announcement has sparked community debate, with some residents expressing concerns about Chick-fil-A's association with anti-LGBTQ groups, as stated by Matthew Kirschebaum, 'There is absolutely no dissembling whatsoever. [Chick-fil-A] simply has no interest in the community and isn’t going to even try to pretend otherwise.' The Little Blue Menu plans to offer competitive-wage employment and donate $25,000 to Feeding America on behalf of the new restaurant, with Elizabeth Maring saying 'I have no more questions, but sadness and frustration' and Christina Grant noting that ordering through an app might be challenging for some.

key_people: John Kish, College Park Election Board Chairman
Martin Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember
Brencis Smith, College Park City Councilmember
Keith Sydnor, College Park City Councilmember
38039.ai_summary: A temporary ban on campaigning by candidates for city office in College Park has caused confusion among three announced mayoral candidates. The ban, announced by John Kish, Chairman of the College Park Election Board, prohibits official candidate signs or materials from being disseminated until a complete candidate packet has been filed and the person has been certified as a candidate. Keith Sydnor noted that he began campaigning on May 1, the first day candidate packets were available, and said 'When I received that letter, I suspended my campaign, I don’t agree with it, but I’m abiding by it,' while Martin Mitchell contested the no-campaign rule, stating 'I see no violation of City election law by campaigning for an office prior to being certified by the Board.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Joel Carlson, friend and community member
Marcus Afzali, former College Park City Councilmember
organizations: College Park Committee for a Better Environment
37990.ai_summary: Alan Hew was sworn in as the new College Park City Councilmember for District 1, bringing his experience in environmental education and community engagement. Hew has served on the College Park Committee for a Better Environment and chaired the Bee City USA Committee, working to create pollinator gardens and educate the public. As he recalled, former councilmember Marcus Afzali encouraged him to run for the council, saying 'No, but your heart's there. You understand how the city works, through working through that development and working on your committees. That's what it takes to serve, and you've done that.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Manager
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
37973.ai_summary: The City of College Park held its first in-person town hall meeting, where City Manager Kenny Young gave an overview of the city's budget for fiscal year 2024, designating $24.8 million for the general fund. Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell explained that the city offers tax breaks to developers, stating 'Yes we did phase a tax credit over five years for developers to come build in the city.' The meeting also covered concerns about student housing, with Councilmember Alan Hew suggesting an off-campus orientation guide for students, and economic development, with Michael Williams noting that ARPA grants might help small businesses remain in the city.

key_people: Lydia Hurley is the author and should not be included, but the following are: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem and Councilmember
Stuart Adams, College Park Councilmember
Teresa Way-Pezzuti, College Park Director of Human Resources
Bill Gardiner, College Park Assistant City Manager
organizations: City of College Park Human Resources Department
37732.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted unanimously to raise annual compensation for the mayor and councilmembers, with the mayor's salary increasing from $10,500 to $15,000 and councilmembers' compensation increasing from $7,000 to $10,000. Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated that 'Someone seeks office mainly to perform a civic duty and to support their community. However, financial compensation was always a lower-ranking criterion for running for office.' The salary increase is intended to make the positions more competitive and prevent low compensation from being a barrier to running for office.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park Mayor
Fazlul Kabir, U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission
organizations: Bangladesh University of Engineering and Technology
37724.ai_summary: Fazlul Kabir, the newly inaugurated College Park Mayor, has been actively engaging with the community since joining the North College Park Civic Association in 2006. Kabir said, 'I'm a very hyper-local person. The thing I enjoy most is talking to a neighbor on his or her porch and talking about some problems or some issues and see[ing] how to solve them or get things done.' As mayor, Kabir aims to rebuild community trust in city leaders and has been connecting with residents through various community events and initiatives.

key_people: Erik Valentine, Community Development Planner for the City of College Park
37734.ai_summary: Biohabitats Inc. has completed an initial survey of the Sentinel Swamp Sanctuary, identifying two nontidal wetlands and recommending a raised boardwalk and viewing platform. 'We're still in the very early stages of this project,' said Erik Valentine, Community Development Planner for the City of College Park. The city has allocated $200,000 for the project, with ongoing funding available through Program Open Space, to restore the sanctuary and remove invasive species, including 13 species that still need to be removed, as noted by Valentine.

key_people: Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
Violetta Sharps Jones, Vice President of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project
Julie Schablitsky, Chief of Cultural Resources with the Maryland Department of Transportation
Ed Williams, Lakeland Community Member
Rev. Stephen Wright, Spiritual Leader
Rev. Latishia Cokley, Pastor of Embry African Methodist Episcopal Church
37619.ai_summary: The Lakeland community gathered to unveil a historic marker, led by Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem, and Violetta Sharps Jones, Vice President of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project. The event honored the community's history and legacy, with Jones stating, 'We pour this libation to give honor, reverence, respect, and recognition to our ancestors whose shoulders we stand on and whose shadows we walk in.' The unveiling of the marker is seen as a step towards recognition and acknowledgment of the community's existence and contributions, with Ed Williams noting, 'It's been a long, a long time coming, but I know a change gon' come.'

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Kennedy Walker, Greenbelt resident
organizations: College Park Tree and Landscaping Board
37521.ai_summary: The City of College Park has begun a sidewalk installation project on Edmonston Road, which involves cutting down a large portion of trees, sparking controversy among residents. According to College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, 'The Complete Streets project is part of our broader initiatives that the city has been involved in. But it’s been about enhancing connectivity and safety for pedestrians and cyclists in our city.' However, some residents, like Greenbelt resident Kennedy Walker, believe the money could be spent on other aspects of the community, saying 'the money used to construct the sidewalk could definitely go towards other aspects of the community like more flowers and less trash in the area.'

key_people: Maria James, former managing editor of The Hyattsville Life & Times
37504.ai_summary: The College Park Here & Now hosted an election forum featuring candidates running in the May 2023 Mayoral Special Election, where they discussed various issues such as development, traffic, and affordable housing. Maria James, former managing editor of The Hyattsville Life & Times, posed a series of questions to the four candidates. The event aimed to inform the community about the candidates' stances on key issues affecting College Park governance.

key_people: Terry Schum, College Park Department of Planning & Community Development Director
Miriam Bader, College Park Department of Planning and Development Director
Dhiru Thadani, Husband of Terry Schum
organizations: College Park Department of Planning & Community Development
37434.ai_summary: Terry Schum, the director of the College Park Department of Planning & Community Development, is retiring after 37 years of service. Schum plans to pursue her lifelong interest in drawing and painting, saying 'Maybe I will carry around a sketchbook.' Her retirement marks a significant change in the city's leadership, with Miriam Bader taking over as director of planning and development, and Schum will not be present to see several projects finalized, including the renovation of Duvall Field and the Route 1 revitalization effort.

key_people: Erik Valentine, Community Development Planner
37417.ai_summary: The City of College Park purchased a small parcel of undeveloped land in the Berwyn neighborhood through the state-funded Program Open Space initiative. Community Development Planner Erik Valentine hopes to use the parcel as open space where people can gather, and the city is seeking community input on how to move forward with the property. Valentine said that by preserving the nearby Sentinel Swamp Sanctuary, the city “hope[s] to incorporate educational elements because of the site’s unique natural habitat.”

key_people: Bryan Haddad, College Park Mayoral Candidate
Maria James, Former Managing Editor with Streetcar Suburbs Publishing
37426.ai_summary: The City of College Park is holding a mayoral election on May 6, with early voting on May 2. Bryan Haddad, a small business owner and College Park mayoral candidate, is running on a single issue to increase penalties against illegally modified vehicles, stating 'A vote for me is a vote for a more peaceful community.' A candidate forum will be held on April 15, moderated by Maria James, a former managing editor with Streetcar Suburbs Publishing.

key_people: Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayor
Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
organizations: National League of Cities University Communities Council
National Center for Missing and Exploited Children
37262.ai_summary: College Park Mayor Patrick Wojahn resigned after being arrested on charges related to the possession and distribution of child pornography. As Wojahn stated, 'While this investigation does not involve any official city business of any kind, it is in the best interests of our community that I step aside and not serve as a distraction.' The city council held an emergency meeting and voted to remove Wojahn from all boards and committees, and Mayor Pro Tem Mitchell will serve as pro tem mayor until a special election is held.

key_people: Robert Thurston, Lakeland Civic Association President
Terry Schum, College Park Department of Planning & Community Development Director
Todd Alexander, Renewal Housing Associates Representative
organizations: College Park Department of Planning & Community Development
37038.ai_summary: LV Collective, a Texas-based real estate developer, has purchased a property in Lakeland for $36 million and plans to build a student housing complex with retail on the ground floor. Robert Thurston, president of the Lakeland Civic Association, wants to see multigenerational housing on the adjacent Alden-Berkley site and emphasizes the importance of community involvement, stating 'It's critical that Lakelanders and the Lakeland diaspora are involved in this redevelopment.' The College Park Department of Planning & Community Development is overseeing the development review process, with Director Terry Schum noting that collaboration between property owners and community consideration is ideal.

key_people: Rebecca Bailey, College Park Animal Control Officer
Bob Ryan, College Park Department of Public Services Director
organizations: College Park Department of Public Services
37024.ai_summary: College Park Animal Control Officer Rebecca Bailey helps animals in need, from rescuing wildlife to reuniting pets with their owners. Bailey said, 'They don’t have a way to advocate for themselves', and she knows she's meant to do this job. The city's animal control service, which has been in place for at least 30 years, focuses on animal welfare and has a significant advantage in being able to respond to calls quickly due to its local knowledge and relationships with residents.

key_people: Anna Latta Owens, College Park Mayor
Dermott Michael Owens, no specific title
William E. “Brit” Kirwan, University of Maryland Leader
36789.ai_summary: Anna Latta Owens, College Park’s first and only female mayor, played a significant role in the city’s governance from 1981 to 1993. She led a fight against a proposed garbage incinerator, established strong ties with the University of Maryland under William E. “Brit” Kirwan, and advocated for senior independent living facilities like Attick Towers. Anna’s legacy continues to be remembered fondly by those who knew her, as she touched many lives with her measures and provided insight to City Councilmembers, especially women, seeking her knowledge of political affairs.

36796.ai_summary: The City of College Park has introduced a new program to make recycling food waste easier through inexpensive compost containers and free weekly collections. According to a recent study by Prince George’s County officials, more than a third of the food that ends up in the county landfill could have been composted instead. Composting is a simple process that can help combat climate change by reducing methane production in landfills, and College Park residents can participate by purchasing compost containers and signing up for the program.

key_people: Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Denise Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember
Kate Kennedy, College Park City Councilmember
Mary King, College Park Resident
Tanya Lervik, College Park Resident
36528.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously approved a new district map, the October Plan 1 map, which keeps the core neighborhoods of the current districts intact. Councilmembers supported this map over the Plan 3A map, which would have unevenly distributed civic associations and student population. As Councilmember Maria Mackie noted, 'One of the reasons that Autoville and Cherry Hill will no longer be in District 4 is because they're the means to allowing District 1 to come down into [areas with student housing].'

key_people: Tyrone Wells, Prince George’s County Office of Emergency Management Operations Manager
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Denise Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember

organizations: Prince George’s County Office of Emergency Management
36357.ai_summary: Tyrone Wells, the operations manager of the Prince George’s County Office of Emergency Management, presented the recently updated County Emergency Operations Plan to the College Park City Council. Councilmembers expressed concerns about the county's efforts to communicate with residents during emergencies, with Councilmember John Rigg stating 'Citizens in my community are wondering why they don’t hear from the county more often.' The council discussed the need for more effective communication and emergency preparedness, particularly for senior citizens and homebound individuals, with Councilmember Maria Mackie noting 'A lot of our senior citizens don’t even own a phone.'

key_people: Gale Mamatova, North College Park Community Association Vice President
36359.ai_summary: The City of College Park is facing challenges in managing change due to redistricting, with residents passionately arguing for their own interests. According to Gale Mamatova, the city needs to revisit its approach and invest in a community engagement tool to bring diverse stakeholders along, as well as hire multilingual staff to address inclusivity. The city's leaders are urged to learn to listen to residents with intent, as residents feel frustrated and do not feel heard, and it is up to city leaders to step up and lead them through this change effectively.

key_people: Kathy Bryant, Old Town Civic Association President
Fazlul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember
Jordan Dewar, Redistricting Commission Vice-Chair
Robert Day, Redistricting Commission Chair
36067.ai_summary: The College Park City Council's redistricting debate continued with residents and councilmembers questioning the Redistricting Commission about their proposals, which would separate core neighborhoods such as Old Town, Calvert Hills, College Park Estates, and Yarrow. Kathy Bryant, Old Town Civic Association President, expressed concern that the commission's approach disregards long-term neighborhood connections, saying '[The commission] kept saying ‘we follow the numbers,’ but it’s disregarding long term neighborhood connections that I feel are very important.' Councilmember Fazlul Kabir emphasized the importance of keeping neighborhoods intact, stating 'People don’t really differentiate between the boundaries of these neighborhoods; they all think as one cohesive community.'

key_people: Denise Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember
Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayor
Shawn Sharifi, Band Leader
36180.ai_summary: College Park Day, the city's signature celebration, is returning on Oct. 15 after a 2-year hiatus, with about 150 organizations participating. The event will feature local food vendors, live entertainment, and activities for all ages, with Councilmember Denise Mitchell recalling that College Park Day grew out of discussions among Councilmember Fazlul Kabir, Mayor Patrick Wojahn, and a small group of residents. Band leader Shawn Sharifi said, 'We can’t wait to bring our boogy to College Park Day,' as the city expects around 8,000 residents to attend the celebration.

key_people: Emily Strab, College Park City Councilmember
Robert Croslin, College Park Mayor
Kevin Ward, College Park Mayor
Lisbeth M Melendez Rivera, College Park City Council Candidate
Kelly Burello, College Park City Council Candidate
organizations: City of College Park Board of Supervisors of Elections
35987.ai_summary: Emily Strab has won the special election for the Ward 2 city council seat, receiving 280 votes out of 698 cast. The seat was vacant after former councilmember Robert Croslin was elected mayor following the death of former mayor Kevin Ward. Strab will take the oath of office on October 20 at King Park, marking a new leadership transition for the city.

key_people: Lee Sommer, College Park Airport Manager
David Dorsch, College Park Airport Authority Chair
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission
35792.ai_summary: The College Park City Council discussed a new commuter program run by Tailwind Air, which plans to launch four daily flights between College Park Airport and New York City’s Skyport Marina. David Dorsch, Chair of the College Park Airport Authority, noted that 'The noise is generally the helicopters and the ones that are the worst are the military, because they’re the biggest and heaviest,' but stated that the planes in Tailwind’s fleet are quieter. The airport authority has asked the city council for approval to change the airport’s operating regulations to allow Tailwind to maintain their proposed flight schedules.

key_people: Robert Croslin, College Park Mayor
Ben Simasek, College Park City Councilmember (Ward 3)
Rommel Sandino, College Park City Councilmember (Ward 5)
Christopher Hatcher, Attorney for Perseus TDC
35965.ai_summary: A new apartment building called Residences at the Six is under construction by Perseus TDC at 6400 America Boulevard in the Prince George’s Plaza Transit District. The building will have 316 units, with three-quarters of the apartments having rents under $2,500, according to Perseus TDC. Councilmember Ben Simasek said the new building 'is a big opportunity to activate University Town Center, to improve the walkability of that area, and generally making it more attractive.'

key_people: Bill Gardiner, Assistant City Manager of College Park
Victoria MacDonald, Berwyn Resident
Bob Kuligowski, Meteorologist
Kathy Bryant, College Park Homeowner
organizations: College Park Department of Public Works
35659.ai_summary: A devastating bow echo storm hit College Park on July 12, causing widespread damage and leaving hundreds of households without power. According to Assistant City Manager Bill Gardiner, the city estimates the cost of the damage to be around $750,000, not including private property damage. Berwyn resident Victoria MacDonald suggested that the University of Maryland should have used its warning siren to caution residents of the impending storm, saying 'Maybe that has to be looked at, in my opinion, what are the criteria?'

key_people: Calvin Hawkins, Prince George's County Councilmember
Mel Franklin, Prince George's County Councilmember
Jennifer Kubit, Hyattsville Resident
35657.ai_summary: The July primary election in Prince George's County saw a voter turnout of nearly 214,000, with incumbents Calvin Hawkins and Mel Franklin winning the at-large race. Some residents, including Jennifer Kubit, reported issues with the voting process, such as being allowed to choose between Republican and Democratic ballots despite the state's closed primary law, and staffing issues that delayed the opening of the Ritchie Coliseum polling site. The College Park City Council discussed these issues during a meeting on August 3, highlighting concerns about the voting process and its impact on local governance.

key_people: James Crane, Columbia Telecommunications Corporation Communications and Public Safety Expert
Shawn Thompson, Columbia Telecommunications Corporation Vice President for Analytics
Suellen Ferguson, College Park City Attorney
Fazlul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember (District 1)
35636.ai_summary: The College Park City Council discussed the planned installation of 5G antennas with Columbia Telecommunications Corporation (CTC) representatives. A Federal Communications Commission (FCC) rule bans jurisdictions from blocking 5G SWF installations, and the city can only discuss the exact location of proposed arrays with telecommunications enterprises. According to Suellen Ferguson, College Park City Attorney, 'a state or local government must approve an eligible [SWF installation] request within 60 days,' and the city has received its first qualifying application from Verizon to install a 5G array at the intersection of Edgewood Road and Rhode Island Avenue.

key_people: Robert Croslin, Hyattsville Mayor
Kelly Burrello, College Park City Council Candidate
Lisbeth M. Melendez Rivera, College Park City Council Candidate
Emily Strab, College Park City Council Candidate
35721.ai_summary: The article discusses several upcoming elections in the area, including a special election for a College Park City Council seat. Robert Croslin, the former interim mayor of Hyattsville, won a special mayoral election with 1,329 votes. College Park had a staffing issue that led to a polling place opening an hour and a half late, and some voters in Hyattsville reported issues with voting machines offering a choice of Democratic or Republican ballots despite Maryland's closed primaries.

35522.ai_summary: Preliminary election results are in for the July 19 primary election in Prince George’s County, with Wanika Fisher leading in the Democratic nomination for county councilmember in District 2 and Eric Olson securing his nomination for District 3. Angela Alsobrooks has received 91% of the votes for county executive, and John D.B. Carr has won the sheriff’s nomination. The results are current as of the morning of July 20th, but could still change as not all precincts have finished reporting and mail-in and provisional ballots have not yet been counted.

key_people: James Rosapepe, State Senator and Vice Chair of the State Senate’s Budget and Taxation Committee
35361.ai_summary: The City of College Park is receiving over $22 million in funding from the State of Maryland for various projects, including $9.7 million for the Route 1 rebuilding project, $5 million for graduate student housing at the University of Maryland, and $200,000 for a new playground at the Al-Huda School. According to State Senator James Rosapepe, the improvements to Route 1 will increase safety and create a visually pleasing thoroughfare, which he calls the 'Champs-Élysées of College Park'. The College Park City-University Partnership will use $7.9 million to create the Community Preservation Trust, aiming to preserve affordable single-family homes in the city.

35345.ai_summary: The state primary election is set to take place on July 19, with early voting from July 7 to July 14, and many races in College Park will be decided in the Democratic primaries. Eric Olson, who represented District 3 from 2006-2014, is running again for the Prince George’s County Council, while incumbent Tom Dernoga is running unopposed in District 1. The election is significant for College Park governance as it will determine the representation of the city in the county council and other local offices.

key_people: Claire Worshtil, Prince George’s County Department of Parks and Recreation Lead Strategic Park Planner
Mary Cook, former College Park City Councilmember and former NCPCA President

35359.ai_summary: The Prince George’s County Department of Parks and Recreation is planning a new community center in North College Park, with a feasibility study expected to conclude by the end of 2022. According to Claire Worshtil, the project will likely move forward, but it is 'possible, but not likely' that it will not. The community center is expected to provide various amenities, with NCPCA President Alaina Pitt saying it will help make her vision of a more diverse and inclusive North College Park a reality, as she stated 'I think the community center can serve as a catalyst for bringing the community together and for more interaction between neighbors.'

34640.ai_summary: The state primary is July 19, with early voting from July 7 to July 14, and many local elections in College Park will be decided in the Democratic primaries. Candidates such as Wanika Fisher, Victor Ramirez, and Eric Olson are running for county council seats, while Angela Alsobrooks is running for re-election as Prince George's County Executive. The outcome of these elections will shape the governance of College Park and Prince George's County.

key_people: Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember

35179.ai_summary: The College Park City Council passed a law requiring residents to obtain a permit to cut down trees over 36 inches in circumference and to prune more than 20% of live branches on large trees, with fines ranging from $500 to $1,000 for non-compliance. Resident Robert O'Brien noted that trees are a community resource, while Resident Mary Cook expressed concerns that fines can cause emotional distress. The ordinance aims to preserve the city's tree canopy, with Councilmember Maria Mackie introducing an amendment to reimburse property owners up to $300 for planting new trees.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)

35203.ai_summary: The city of College Park is experiencing delays in the Calvert Hills drainage project, which aims to address the issue of flooding in the area. As Councilmember John Rigg stated, 'During the massive rainstorm of September 2020, there was literally 3 to 4 feet of water in the streets. And some neighbors experience flooded basements or sewer back-ups nearly every year.' The project, estimated to cost $15.5 million, has been plagued by delays and is over a year behind schedule. The Prince George's County Stormwater Management Division has developed a four-part improvement plan to address the flooding issues in Calvert Hills.

key_people: Mike Hunninghake, Sustainable Maryland Program Manager
organizations: University of Maryland Environmental Finance Center
35210.ai_summary: Sustainable Maryland's free certification program helps municipalities become more eco-friendly, with 41 certified municipalities in the state, including College Park. The program, which was launched in 2012, provides support and access to resources for municipalities to become more sustainable. As Mike Hunninghake, Sustainable Maryland's program manager, stated, 'The program operates on the premise that by engaging local governments in sustainability we build a strong foundation for the work that needs to be done with respect to critical issues like climate change, environmental justice, and strong local economies.'

key_people: Carlo Colella, University of Maryland Vice President and Chief Administrative Officer
David Mitchell, University of Maryland Police Department Chief
Katharine Wilson is not included as per the instruction and Llatera Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: University of Maryland Security Operations Center
35042.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has approved funding for live-video monitoring capacity in the fiscal 2023 budget, which will allow the University of Maryland Police Department to monitor cameras in areas of high student activity. According to University of Maryland Vice President and Chief Administrative Officer Carlo Colella, 'The university leadership respectfully ask the city to reinstate the monitor and the live monitoring of the city’s closed circuit TV cameras.' University of Maryland Police Department Chief David Mitchell emphasized the importance of live-monitored cameras for crime prevention and community safety, stating 'One of our major concerns is the increased call for service as well as mental health' and noting a past instance where live monitoring helped prevent a potential tragedy.

key_people: Robert Marsili, College Park Director of Public Works
Bill Lescure, descendant of the Calverts
organizations: Maryland National-Capital Park and Planning Commission
35026.ai_summary: The City of College Park has been restoring the historic Old Parish House, built in 1817, with the goal of making it a 'showpiece for the public', according to Robert Marsili, College Park Director of Public Works. The restoration has been ongoing for several years, with final work underway this spring, and has included replacing the roof and ceilings, repairing and replacing windows, and exterior masonry work. 'We worked hard to do this in a historically accurate way,' Marsili said, noting that the city chose to use a local firm and a preservation grant from the Maryland National-Capital Park and Planning Commission helped fund the project.

key_people: Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayor
Petrella Robinson, North Brentwood Mayor
Isabella Alcañiz, University of Maryland Associate Professor and Director of the Latin American and Caribbean Studies Center
35037.ai_summary: The College Park City Council established the Restorative Justice Commission to address the historic harm caused by the city's urban renewal project in the 1970s, which destroyed homes in the Lakeland community. As Mayor Patrick Wojahn said, the initiative aims to "making the community whole for what occurred in the past and putting them as close as possible into the place or even better than they were before the wrong occurred." The commission includes members such as Petrella Robinson, who grew up in a similar historic Black community and saw the effects of urban renewal firsthand, and Isabella Alcañiz, who has researched transitional justice in Latin America.

key_people: Hayden Renaghan, University of Maryland Student Government Association Deputy Liaison to the College Park City Council
Kislay Parahsar, UMD Student Body President
Dan Alpert, former UMD Student Government Association Liaison to the College Park City Council
organizations: University of Maryland Student Government Association

University of Maryland Office of Community Engagement
35015.ai_summary: Hayden Renaghan, the University of Maryland Student Government Association's deputy liaison to the College Park City Council, is working to enhance community engagement and cleanliness in College Park through the Clean City Project. As Renaghan noted, 'Students want it known that it's their town, too, not just a pit-stop for them.' Renaghan emphasizes the importance of showing people why they should care about their community, saying 'Show people why they should care. Prove there are tangible benefits to respecting their community, to contributing, to leaving it a better place than they found it.'

key_people: Mary Cook, North College Park Citizens Association President
Fazul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember (District 1)
Tom Dernoga, County Councilmember (District 1)

34892.ai_summary: The city of College Park has launched a feasibility study for a new community center in the North College Park neighborhood, with the Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission expected to foot most of the bill. As Councilmember Fazul Kabir (District 1) stated, 'A partnership will help expedite the construction. This will also help the city to have its stake in the way the services will be provided in the center once it’s built.' The study, led by WXY Studio, aims to identify North College Park residents' priorities for the new center and determine the type of facility that best addresses those priorities.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember
Whitney, no specific title or organization mentioned
34748.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is focused on encouraging homeownership through new programs, aiming to achieve a better balance of rental and owned homes in the city. According to Councilmember Fazlul Kabir, 'It's not against the renters. It's just the added benefit of stabilizing the neighborhood,' and established homeowners are more likely to invest in their community. The council is adjusting the New Neighbor Homeowner Grant Program to offer $5,000 to potential home buyers who agree to stay in their home for at least five years, with the goal of encouraging home buyers to establish themselves in properties that were formerly rentals.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember
Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayor
Kate Kennedy, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember

D.W. Rowlands, tenant at 4704 Calvert Rd.
34737.ai_summary: The College Park City Council approved a contract of sale to purchase a property in the Old Town neighborhood for $1.7 million. Councilmember Stuart Adams said 'The potential purchase of 4704 Calvert Road makes sense, it aligns with our strategic goals' and noted it could help stabilize affordable rental and explore community-focused development. The council also passed an emergency ordinance to expedite the purchase process, with Councilmember Fazlul Kabir noting he was 'uncomfortable with how fast this is moving' and Mayor Patrick Wojahn responding that 'sometimes opportunities present themselves, and we have to jump on them'.

key_people: Bibi Mohamed, Ansaar-ul-Birr Community Services Inc. Director
Azad Mohamed, Helpers to Good Volunteer
34755.ai_summary: Helpers to Good, a program under Ansaar-ul-Birr Community Services Inc., hosted a Kids Fun Festival on February 19 and 20, providing free lunches and school supplies to families in need. The event was supported by the City of College Park and Islamic Relief U.S.A, with almost 400 meals and 200 gifts distributed over the course of two days. As Azad Mohamed noted, the event's success was evident, with the organization running out of meals and supplies, prompting a repeat of the event the next day, and demonstrating the importance of community connections, as Bibi Mohamed wrote, 'We do a different activity monthly to [connect] with our community.'

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
Pamela Boozer-Strother, School Board Member (District 3)
Sarah Christopherson, President of the Hyattsville Middle School PTSO
Josie Jack, Kit Slack
34718.ai_summary: Hyattsville Middle School students are facing delayed bus arrivals, with up to 75% of buses arriving late in February, according to a partial log of arrival and departure times kept by the school administration. College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, who is a parent of a student at the school, said 'My own child’s bus late arrival means that he misses math pretty much daily.' School Board Member Pamela Boozer-Strother credited school officials for some recent improvement in bus arrival times, but emphasized that busing HMS students should be a priority due to the disruptions caused by the school's construction.

key_people: Denise Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Kate Kennedy, College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
34743.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmembers have been adapting to new meeting spaces and formats over the past two years, including meetings in Davis Hall and virtual sessions due to the pandemic. Councilmembers such as Denise Mitchell and Maria Mackie noted that Davis Hall was cramped, but also allowed for more informal interactions, while virtual meetings have presented technical challenges and benefits, including improved work-life balance, as noted by Kate Kennedy, who said 'It's a lot easier to have work-life balance when you're at home.' Llatetra Brown Esters also highlighted the benefits of virtual meetings, stating that 'the virtual option has allowed more people to participate, which I think is important.'

key_people: Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Manager
Sophie Gorman Oriani is not included as per instructions
organizations: Prince George’s County Economic Development Corporation
34775.ai_summary: The College Park City Council discussed a proposal to renew the city’s participation in the Greater College Park Regional Institution Strategic Enterprise (RISE) Zone. According to Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Manager, 'What we think is that start up companies and new companies are generally trying to get going and not trying to buy or purchase a building,' and the state has expanded the RISE zone program to provide rental assistance to businesses that don’t own their own buildings. The city will hold a public hearing to review participation in the RISE zone on March 8.

key_people: Darryll Pines, University of Maryland President

34586.ai_summary: The Adelphi Road Sector Plan has faced opposition from residents and officials, including Delegate Mary Lehman and College Park City Councilmember Stuart Adams, who are concerned about the upzoning of Guilford Woods, a 15-acre state-owned tract with mature trees and wildlife. As Adams stated, 'We have enough time to really thoughtfully consider how to add appropriate density, how to make sure we’re looking at our school burdens, how we can preserve our green space and make this absolutely fantastic.' The plan aims to guide development around the Adelphi Road station for the next 25 years, but the public hearing highlighted a disconnect between the plan’s environmental goals and the potential consequences of upzoning the woods.

key_people: Darryll Pines, University of Maryland President
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Steve Halpern, College Park City Engineer
Bob Baer, College Park resident
34607.ai_summary: The City of College Park is addressing its flooding issue through the Stormwater Assessment Project, which aims to upgrade stormwater management systems, particularly in the Calvert Hills neighborhood. The city plans to conduct a topographical survey to examine water flow and create a model to identify solutions, with an estimated cost of $600,000. As College Park resident Bob Baer noted, 'When Calvert road stormwater inlets max out, and the street water level rises only an inch or more, the street water goes down our driveway and into our yard and the neighbor’s yard,' highlighting the urgency of the issue.

key_people: Robert Craig Baum, Wisdom 1096 Project Founder
Maxine Gross, College Park Resident
34475.ai_summary: College Park residents reflect on the pandemic, with one resident, Maxine Gross, noting that the city passed Resolution 20-R016 in support of Black lives, which apologized for the city's past history of oppression and sought restorative justice. As Gross recovered from a medical episode, the city explored racial justice through forums and established a restorative justice steering committee, ultimately adopting a new resolution establishing a restorative justice commission. The resident's personal experience and the city's efforts towards racial justice highlight the complexities of community engagement and social justice in College Park.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Robert Day, College Park City Councilmember
34307.ai_summary: Stuart Adams was elected to represent District 3 on the College Park City Council, replacing Robert Day. Adams has been active in the community since 2015 and plans to use his expertise in floodplain management and sustainability to tackle stormwater management issues in District 3. He said, 'I've always been drawn to university towns. I like the activity and being able to meet younger folks' and 'That's been my passion for a while, community resilience. I know what it's like when your family loses your home and your community gets completely uprooted,' referencing his family's experience with Hurricane Katrina.

key_people: Susan Whitney, College Park City Council Representative for District 2
Monroe Dennis, Former College Park City Council Representative for District 2
Llatetra Esters, College Park City Council Representative
34310.ai_summary: Susan Whitney is the new District 2 representative on the College Park City Council, replacing Monroe Dennis. Whitney, who has volunteered extensively in her community, hopes to engage more residents in local politics, saying 'I love solving problems, getting things done and helping people. I want to be persistent and see what we can do to make positive change for our neighbors.' She earned her seat with 164 votes in the November 10 election.

key_people: Derrick Davis, Prince George's County Councilmember, District 6
Mel Franklin, Prince George's County Councilmember, At-Large

34197.ai_summary: The Prince George's County Council voted to accept a controversial redistricting map that divides the city of College Park, despite widespread citizen opposition. The new map, sponsored by council members including Derrick Davis and Mel Franklin, has been criticized for being gerrymandered to favor certain electoral candidates. As College Park Mayor Patrick Wojahn stated, 'The current proposed map divides College Park right through its center,' and councilmember Dannielle Glaros expressed her disappointment, saying 'I deeply question the moral compass of my colleagues.'

key_people: Mel Franklin, Prince George's County Councilmember
Derrick Leon Davis, Prince George's County Councilmember
Thomas Dernoga, Prince George's County Councilmember
Dannielle M. Glaros, Prince George's County Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Eric Olson, Candidate for Prince George's County Council
33953.ai_summary: The Prince George's County Council has proposed two redistricting plans, one by Councilmember Mel Franklin and another by Councilmember Derrick Leon Davis, which would impact College Park's representation on the county council. Franklin's plan, which passed 6 to 4, redesignates portions of College Park from District 3 to District 1, while keeping the University of Maryland in District 3. As Councilmember Thomas Dernoga said, 'With regard to the City of College Park, they're blindsided' by the initial proposal, and College Park City Councilmember John Rigg noted that the city has 'done very well … having two representatives on the [county] council that represented its interests, albeit from slightly different perspectives'.

key_people: Violetta Sharp-Jones, College Park Restorative Justice Steering Committee Member
Maxine Gross, College Park Restorative Justice Steering Committee Chair
Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayor
organizations: College Park Restorative Justice Steering Committee
34106.ai_summary: The College Park Restorative Justice Steering Committee has presented their suggestions to the city council, including a framework for establishing the city's Restorative Justice Commission to address harms done to the Lakeland community. As committee member Violetta Sharp-Jones explained, Lakeland was established in 1890 by African Americans seeking a better place to raise their families, but the community was later impacted by the Lakeland Urban Renewal Plan, which resulted in the destruction of ⅔ of the community and displaced over 100 families. According to Mayor Patrick Wojahn, the establishment of the commission will give people the chance to talk about what happened and 'make clear that there was real harm caused to families, caused to the community, caused to people as a result of what happened here.'

key_people: Danny Schaible, College Park City Councilmember (Ward 2)
organizations: Town of University Park Common Council
34137.ai_summary: The College Park City Council debated a ban on gas-powered leaf blowers, sponsored by Councilmember Danny Schaible, with the goal of reducing noise and gas pollution in the city. Schaible stated, '[A gas-powered leaf blower] can be as loud as 110 decibels, 115 decibels in the ear of an operator,' and compared the pollution from using a gas-powered blower for an hour to driving a Ford F-150 for 3,000 miles. The proposed ban aims to match efforts by surrounding jurisdictions, such as University Park and Montgomery County, which have implemented or are considering similar bans.

key_people: Ken Ulman, President of Margrave Strategies
Stacey Price, Co-founder and CEO of Shop Made in D.C.
Michael Babin, Co-founder of Shop Made in D.C.
34108.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has approved the use of funds from the American Rescue Plan Act of 2021 to support the opening of Shop Made in Maryland, a retail store featuring products from local artisans, in the new College Park City Hall. According to Stacey Price, Co-founder and CEO of Shop Made in D.C., 'We like to label ourselves as a modern retail incubator. We provide a place to showcase talent … We provide business assistance so they can become more profitable in what they are doing, from packaging to pricing and all the other things.' The store will offer a lively roster of activities and will take six months to curate products, with a focus on supporting artists and makers from Prince George's County and College Park.

key_people: Cheryl Harrington, Virtual Cooking Demo Host
Lisa Walker, Hyattsville Aging in Place Board Chair
Miriam Bader, College Park Senior City Planner
Agnes Artemel, Artemel & Associates Inc. President
organizations: University of Baltimore's Jacob France Institute
34100.ai_summary: The City of College Park is applying to become a member of AARP's Network of Age-Friendly States and Communities, which requires creating an action plan to address issues of aging within the community. As part of this effort, a team of consultants from the University of Baltimore's Jacob France Institute is leading the development of the plan, with funding from the Prince George's County Planning Department. 'What we wanted to do was to bring the people, the culture, the excitement, the creativity of Route 1 to the people of the area,' said Lisa Walker, chair of Hyattsville Aging in Place's board, and 'We want to help College Park become an even more age-friendly community than it already is,' said Agnes Artemel, president of Artemel & Associates Inc.

key_people: Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayoral candidate
Kate Kennedy, College Park District 1 candidate
Fazlul Kabir, College Park District 1 candidate
Llatetra Esters, College Park District 2 candidate
