33771.ai_summary: The College Park Here and Now hosted a virtual forum for candidates for Mayor as well as Districts 1, 2, and 4. Questions were made from the College Park Here and Now reporting, as well as from local residents. The forums featured candidates including Patrick Wojahn for Mayor, Kate Kennedy and Fazlul Kabir for District 1, Llatetra Esters, Bob Catlin, and Susan Whitney for District 2, and Maria Mackie and Denise Mitchell for District 4.

key_people: Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayor
Mary Cook, North College Park Neighborhood Association President
Danny Schaible, Hyattsville Councilmember
33836.ai_summary: The public feedback period for Prince George's County's Countywide Map Amendment (CMA) has closed, with College Park legislators and community activists expressing concerns that the new zoning classifications will substantively change the character of their neighborhoods. As Mary Cook, president of the North College Park Neighborhood Association, said, 'I am concerned that these new zoning classifications are going to set developers up so that they can create a new density in our area.' The CMA process has raised concerns about potential loopholes that could allow developers to sneak in zoning intensifications, with a coalition of 21 organizations sending a letter to the county council and planning board to echo these concerns.

key_people: Eric Olson, College Park Councilmember
33888.ai_summary: The City of College Park is undergoing significant construction projects, including the development of Tempo, a student housing project, the University of Maryland's new School of Public Policy building, and a new City Hall. These projects are expected to be completed in summer 2022, with the goal of transforming Baltimore Avenue into a college town main street. The upgrades will create a safer environment for pedestrians, cyclists, and motorists, and will include features such as bike trails, pedestrian bridges, and public plazas.

key_people: Tonia James, Event Organizer
Karen Stewart, Owner of Jesse's Soul Line Dance
Melissa Sites, Director of College Park Arts Exchange
Rev. Carrington Carter, Head Pastor at Embry AME Church
33883.ai_summary: The Embry AME Church in College Park's Lakeland neighborhood hosted a dance event, where members of the church and other city residents danced to religiously themed remixes of popular songs. As Rev. Carrington Carter, the head pastor at Embry AME, reflected on the history of the church, he said 'I'm very informed about the restorative justice act ... the church has weathered a lot of storms as far as its history, we are about to celebrate 118 years in October, next month.' The event is part of the city's efforts to move forward with restorative justice, after the City of College Park passed a resolution in 2020 renouncing systemic racism and declaring support for Black lives.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Alex Keating, VeoRide Director of Public Policy and Partnerships
Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember
Robert Day, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Terry Schum, City Planner
33773.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering extending VeoRide's micro-mobility pilot program to a three-year contract, despite concerns about the program's mismanagement. Several councilmembers, including John Rigg and Robert Day, expressed concerns about the company's management, with Rigg stating 'I've seen them throw them in the stream, I've seen them be hit with cars, I've seen people throw them in the streets,' and Day saying 'These things are not being put back, I know they are a great addition to our city, but if we can't manage them right then we can't have them here.' Councilmembers are seeking accountability and better management from VeoRide before extending the contract.

key_people: Todd Reitzel, College Park Tree and Landscape Board Member
Emily Friend, College Park Resident
Dr. Ian Mather, College Park Resident
Bill Gardiner, College Park Interim City Manager
33577.ai_summary: The College Park City Council delayed a vote on a proposed tree ordinance after a lengthy public hearing, where around 30 residents testified, including Emily Friend, who said 'I believe if we’re going to have a successful tree planting program, it should not be punitive.' The ordinance aims to boost the city's waning tree canopy by requiring residents to obtain a free permit to cut down a tree on their property or prune one more than 20%. Todd Reitzel, a member of the Tree and Landscape Board, stated that data shows homeowners are the main contributors to tree loss, which is why the ordinance focuses on them, with most tree loss concentrated around Route 1 and Rhode Island Ave.

key_people: Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember (District 4)
Kate Kennedy, College Park City Councilmember (District 1)
Jax Chaudhry, Literacy Lab Regional Director
33567.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has approved a pilot program with The Literacy Lab, a nonprofit organization that provides reading tutors, to be implemented at Hollywood Elementary School. Councilmember Maria Mackie said, 'We saw a problem. We found a solution in the Literacy Lab and we worked on implementing that solution in hopes that this small step could be the first step to helping many students throughout College Park and PG County.' The program will be funded with no more than $40,000 from College Park's FY22 budget, and aims to address the literacy gap in young students, particularly in K-3 grades.

key_people: James Keleti, Prince George’s County Police Department Major and Special Operations Division Commander
Bob Ryan, College Park Public Services Director
Fazlul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember
Gilbert Bowling, Prince George’s County Police Department Assistant Commander
33505.ai_summary: The College Park City Council and Prince George’s County Police Department are working together to address the issue of noisy cars on College Park streets, with police stopping 50 vehicles for excessive exhaust noise and issuing 26 warnings between April 16 and July 8. Councilmember Fazlul Kabir stated that 'This issue is getting worse' and suggested exploring additional enforcement options, such as a joint operation among law enforcement agencies. Police have also issued equipment repair orders and citations to owners of vehicles that make excessive noise, with Captain Gilbert Bowling noting that excessive exhaust noise is a growing problem in both the county and state.

key_people: Robert Marsili, College Park Department of Public Works Director
organizations: College Park Department of Public Works
33469.ai_summary: The City of College Park's Department of Public Works Director, Robert Marsili, explained how the city handles its solid waste, which is managed differently by each jurisdiction in Prince George's County. As Marsili noted, 'Every city or town in Prince George’s County handles its solid waste a little differently,' due to evolved policies over time. The county's waste disposal has evolved from unregulated dumping to the use of the Brown Station Road Sanitary Landfill, which has been expanded and upgraded to meet EPA requirements, particularly concerning groundwater pollution.

Pablo Regis de Oliveira, Prince George’s County Arts and Humanities Council Program Manager

organizations: Prince George’s County Arts and Humanities Council
33507.ai_summary: The College Park Arts Exchange, in collaboration with EducArte, hosted Brazilian percussion workshops at the Old Parish House, led by percussionist André Coelho. Pablo Regis de Oliveira, EducArte’s co-founder and executive director, organized the workshops, which were supported by Mayor Patrick Wojahn and the College Park City Council. Regis de Oliveira stated, 'I’ve been driving by this building for so many years, and it’s nice to actually be doing an event here that’s for our community,' highlighting the importance of community engagement and in-person events in College Park.

key_people: Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayor
Fazlul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Bill Gardiner, College Park Interim City Manager
Gary Fields, College Park Director of Finance
key_issues: grant assistance for small businesses and nonprofits
30398.ai_summary: College Park is expecting $22 million from the American Rescue Plan Act by the end of July, with plans to use the funds to pay off financial losses and provide grant assistance to hotels, tourism, restaurants, and nonprofit organizations. Councilmembers Fazlul Kabir and Maria Mackie expressed support for funding Meals on Wheels, with Kabir noting that the organization has been supporting College Park residents in need for many years. The city will also use the funds for capital projects, including renovations to Duvall Field and City Hall, and road improvements, as stated by Director of Finance Gary Fields, who said 'Yes, we can do some additional street and road improvements with these additional funds.'
